background_cases: A horse is a substance: it is a unified, self-subsistent particular that persists through change and possesses its own matter.
A stone is a substance: it is an individual, self-contained object with a determinate identity and material composition.
A man is a substance: he is a particular enduring thing, capable of independent existence, composed of matter organised by a vital principle.
definition: An entity is a substance if and only if (i) it is a self-subsistent particular, (ii) it is composed of matter, and (iii) it has a principle of unity that makes it one thing rather than many.
conditions: self-subsistent particular
composed of matter
has principle of unity making it one thing
counterexample: The soul, as conceived by the Cartesian, is a self-subsistent particular — it exists independently of other things — and it has a principle of unity, namely consciousness. But it is not composed of matter; it is an immaterial thinking substance. The second condition fails. Yet the Cartesian tradition insists that the soul is a substance par excellence — indeed, the substance of which we are most certain.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all material particulars — a horse, a stone, a man — and the definition abstracted the material composition they share. But the concept of substance was historically intended to cover immaterial as well as material entities, and the background cases were too uniform in their materiality to reveal this.
passage:
Consider three cases of substance. A horse is a substance: it is a unified, self-subsistent particular that persists through change, possessing its own matter organised by a vital principle. A stone is a substance: it is an individual object with a determinate identity and a definite material composition. A man is a substance: he is a particular enduring thing, composed of flesh and bone, capable of independent existence. From these cases one might extract a definition: an entity is a substance if and only if it is a self-subsistent particular, it is composed of matter, and it has a principle of unity that makes it one thing rather than many.

But this definition, drawn from material particulars, proves too restrictive. Consider the soul as conceived by the Cartesian tradition. The soul is a self-subsistent particular — it exists independently of the body, surviving, on the Cartesian account, the destruction of the material frame. It has a principle of unity, namely consciousness: it is one thinking thing, not a collection of disparate processes. But it is not composed of matter; it is an immaterial substance, a res cogitans, wholly distinct from the extended material world. The second condition fails. Yet the Cartesian tradition insists, with considerable philosophical force, that the soul is a substance in the fullest sense — indeed, the substance of which we have the most immediate and certain knowledge. The background cases were all material things, and the definition mistook their shared materiality for a necessary feature of substance. The concept of substance, however, was always intended to reach beyond the material.

background_cases: Striking a match causes it to ignite: the striking temporally precedes the ignition and is connected to it by a known physical mechanism.
A blow from a hammer causes the nail to sink: the blow precedes the sinking and the mechanism — the transfer of kinetic energy — is well understood.
Heating water causes it to boil: the application of heat precedes the boiling, and the causal path through molecular agitation is continuous.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C is connected to E by a continuous physical mechanism, (ii) C temporally precedes E, and (iii) the mechanism transmits a conserved physical quantity from C to E.
conditions: continuous physical mechanism connecting C to E
C temporally precedes E
mechanism transmits conserved physical quantity
counterexample: A gardener's failure to water his plants causes them to die. The failure temporally precedes (or is contemporaneous with) the death. But there is no continuous physical mechanism connecting the failure to the death — a failure is an absence, not a process, and it transmits no conserved physical quantity. The first and third conditions fail. Yet the gardener's neglect is plainly the cause of the plants' death.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved positive events transmitting physical quantities through continuous mechanisms. The definition abstracted these features as essential. But causation by omission — the failure to act — involves no mechanism, no transmission, and no positive event, yet it is as genuine a form of causation as any.
passage:
Consider three cases of causation. Striking a match causes it to ignite: the striking precedes the ignition, a continuous physical mechanism connects them, and kinetic energy is transmitted from the striker to the match head. A blow from a hammer causes a nail to sink: the blow precedes the sinking, the mechanism of energy transfer is straightforward, and a conserved quantity — momentum — passes from hammer to nail. Heating water causes it to boil: the heat precedes the boiling, the mechanism runs through the agitation of water molecules, and thermal energy is transmitted continuously. From these cases one might define causation: an event C causes an event E if and only if C is connected to E by a continuous physical mechanism, C temporally precedes E, and the mechanism transmits a conserved physical quantity from C to E.

But this definition, drawn from cases of positive physical interaction, cannot accommodate causation by omission. Consider a gardener who neglects to water his plants during a drought. The plants die. The gardener's failure to water is, by any ordinary reckoning, the cause of their death. Yet the failure is not an event in any positive sense — it is an absence, a non-occurrence. There is no continuous physical mechanism connecting the absence of watering to the death of the plants; an absence does not transmit anything. No conserved physical quantity passes from the gardener's inaction to the wilting leaves. The first and third conditions fail entirely. The background cases all involved positive events in which physical quantities were transmitted through material mechanisms, and the definition abstracted these features as essential to causation. But causation by omission — which is ubiquitous in both everyday life and the law — involves no mechanism, no transmission, and no positive event. The definition has generalised from too narrow a base.

background_cases: The duration of a footrace is a period of time: it begins when the starter fires the pistol and ends when the runner crosses the finish line, and a single clock measures the interval.
The duration of a day is a period of time: it begins at midnight and ends at the next midnight, and it is measured by the rotation of the earth relative to a single standard.
The duration of a pendulum's swing is a period of time: it begins at one extreme of the arc and ends at the return to that extreme, and a single clock can measure it.
definition: The temporal interval between two events is determinate if and only if (i) both events can be assigned positions on a single time axis, (ii) the interval between them is the same for all observers, and (iii) the interval can be measured by any sufficiently accurate clock.
conditions: events assignable to single time axis
interval same for all observers
measurable by any sufficiently accurate clock
counterexample: Two events — a flash of light on earth and a flash of light on a spaceship travelling at a significant fraction of the speed of light — can be assigned positions on a time axis, and the interval can be measured by clocks. But the interval between them is not the same for all observers: the observer on earth and the observer on the spaceship will disagree about the elapsed time, owing to relativistic time dilation. The second condition fails, yet the temporal interval is perfectly real.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved events at rest relative to one another and to the measuring clock. The definition abstracted the observer-independence of temporal intervals as essential. But relativistic physics reveals that temporal intervals are observer-dependent when the events or observers are in relative motion, and this dependence is not an imperfection of measurement but a feature of the nature of time itself.
passage:
Consider three cases of temporal measurement. The duration of a footrace is measured by a single clock: the starter fires the pistol and the runner crosses the finish line, and the elapsed time is determinate and unambiguous. The duration of a day is measured by the rotation of the earth: midnight to midnight, twenty-four hours, the same for all who stand upon the surface. The duration of a pendulum's swing is measured by counting oscillations against a standard clock: the interval is precise and reproducible. From these cases one might propose: the temporal interval between two events is determinate if and only if both events can be assigned positions on a single time axis, the interval between them is the same for all observers, and the interval can be measured by any sufficiently accurate clock.

But this definition, drawn from cases in which all events and observers are at rest relative to one another, proves untenable when relative motion enters the picture. Consider two events: a flash of light on earth and a flash of light on a spaceship travelling at a significant fraction of the speed of light. Both events can be assigned positions on a time axis, and both can be measured by clocks. But the temporal interval between them is not the same for all observers. The observer on earth and the observer on the spaceship will measure different elapsed times — not because their clocks are inaccurate, but because time itself passes at different rates for observers in relative motion. This is the relativistic dilation of time, confirmed by experiment to extraordinary precision. The second condition fails. The background cases all involved a world of rest, in which temporal intervals are indeed the same for everyone. The definition mistook this feature of the stationary world for a necessary feature of time as such.

background_cases: A ship that has had one plank replaced is the same ship: the replacement of a single part does not destroy identity.
A cathedral that has had its roof repaired is the same cathedral: the renovation of one component leaves the building's identity intact.
A man who has lost a tooth and had it replaced by a prosthetic is the same man: the substitution of one part preserves personal identity.
definition: An object that has undergone the replacement of a part is the same object if and only if (i) the majority of its parts remain original, (ii) the replacement was gradual rather than sudden, and (iii) the function of the object was preserved throughout.
conditions: majority of parts remain original
replacement gradual rather than sudden
function preserved throughout
counterexample: The ship of Theseus, in the classical puzzle, has every one of its planks replaced over a period of years, one at a time. The replacement is gradual, and the function — seaworthiness — is preserved throughout. But the majority of parts are not original; eventually, none are. The first condition fails. Yet ordinary judgement holds that the ship remains the same ship throughout the process, for at no point does it cease to be a functioning vessel, and at no point does more than one plank change.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved partial replacement — one plank, one roof, one tooth — and the definition abstracted the majority-original requirement from their shared feature. But the ship of Theseus shows that gradual total replacement preserves identity just as effectively as partial replacement, provided the process is sufficiently incremental. The definition mistook the partiality of the background replacements for a necessary condition of identity-preservation.
passage:
Consider three cases of replacement. A ship has one plank replaced: it remains the same ship. A cathedral has its roof repaired: it remains the same cathedral. A man loses a tooth and receives a prosthetic: he remains the same man. From these cases one might define identity through replacement: an object that has undergone the replacement of a part is the same object if and only if the majority of its parts remain original, the replacement was gradual rather than sudden, and the function of the object was preserved throughout.

But this definition, drawn from cases of partial replacement, founders upon total replacement. Consider the classical puzzle of the ship of Theseus. Over a period of years, every plank of the ship is removed and replaced by a new plank, one at a time. At each stage the replacement is gradual — only one plank is changed — and the function of the ship is preserved; it remains seaworthy and in service throughout. But eventually every original plank has been replaced; the majority of parts are not original — indeed, none of the parts are original. The first condition fails. Yet it seems natural to say that the ship is the same ship. At no moment in the process did it cease to be the ship of Theseus; at no moment was its identity disrupted. The background cases all involved partial replacement, and the definition mistook the partiality — the fact that most parts remained — for a necessary condition of identity-preservation. But the ship of Theseus shows that identity can survive total replacement, provided the replacement is sufficiently gradual.

background_cases: Redness is a universal: it is instantiated by many particular red things — red apples, red roses, red sunsets — and each instance shares the same quality.
Triangularity is a universal: it is instantiated by many particular triangles — drawn, constructed, imagined — and each instance exhibits the same geometrical form.
Hardness is a universal: it is instantiated by many particular hard things — diamonds, stones, iron bars — and each instance possesses the same resistance to deformation.
definition: A property is a genuine universal if and only if (i) it is instantiated by multiple particulars, (ii) each instance possesses the property in the same way, and (iii) the property admits of no degrees.
conditions: instantiated by multiple particulars
each instance possesses property in same way
property admits of no degrees
counterexample: Temperature is a property instantiated by many particulars — this cup of tea, that block of ice, the surface of the sun — and it appears to function as a universal. But it admits of degrees: the tea is hotter than the ice, and the sun is hotter than either. The third condition fails. Yet temperature is a genuine property of things, predicable of many, and functioning as a universal in all logical respects.
abductive_insight: The background cases — redness, triangularity, hardness — were chosen so that each instance resembles every other in a seemingly uniform way. But redness admits of shades, triangularity admits of shapes, and hardness admits of degrees on the Mohs scale. The definition imposed a uniformity that even the background cases do not strictly satisfy, and properties that vary continuously — temperature, mass, brightness — are universals despite their variation.
passage:
Consider three cases of universals. Redness is a universal: it is instantiated by many particular red things — an apple, a rose, a sunset — and each instance shares the same quality. Triangularity is a universal: it is instantiated by many particular triangles, and each exhibits the same geometrical form. Hardness is a universal: it is instantiated by many particular hard things, and each possesses the same resistance to deformation. From these cases one might define a genuine universal as a property that is instantiated by multiple particulars, is possessed by each instance in the same way, and admits of no degrees.

But this definition, drawn from apparently uniform properties, proves too restrictive. Consider temperature. It is instantiated by many particulars: this cup of tea has a temperature, that block of ice has a temperature, the surface of the sun has a temperature. Temperature functions as a universal in every logical respect — it is predicable of many things, it supports generalisations, it enters into laws of nature. But it admits of degrees: the tea is at seventy degrees, the ice at minus ten, the sun at five thousand. The third condition fails. Yet temperature is as genuine a property as redness or triangularity. Indeed, upon reflection, even the background cases admit of degrees: redness ranges from scarlet to crimson, triangularity encompasses acute, right, and obtuse triangles, and hardness is measured on a continuous scale. The definition imposed a requirement of absolute uniformity that even its own paradigm cases do not strictly satisfy. What makes a property a universal is its predicability of many, not its freedom from variation.

background_cases: It is possible that a coin tossed into the air lands on heads: the outcome is consistent with the laws of physics and depends upon the initial conditions.
It is possible that it rains tomorrow: the meteorological conditions are such that rain might or might not occur.
It is possible that a particular seed germinates: the biological conditions permit germination, though they do not guarantee it.
definition: A state of affairs is genuinely possible if and only if (i) it is consistent with all known physical laws, (ii) the initial conditions for its occurrence are present, and (iii) there is a nonzero probability of its occurrence given those conditions.
conditions: consistent with all known physical laws
initial conditions for occurrence are present
nonzero probability of occurrence given conditions
counterexample: Consider the possibility that the gravitational constant might have had a different value. This is, many physicists hold, a genuine possibility — the constant might have been different, and a universe with a different gravitational constant is conceivable and consistent with the general form of physical law. But the initial conditions for a different value of the gravitational constant are not present in our universe, and there is no probability, zero or otherwise, that can be meaningfully assigned to the occurrence given current conditions. The second and third conditions fail, yet the possibility appears genuine.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved possibilities within the fixed framework of our universe — coin tosses, weather, germination — where initial conditions and probabilities can be specified. The definition abstracted from this in-universe perspective and cannot accommodate trans-world or trans-cosmic possibilities concerning the fundamental parameters of nature themselves.
passage:
Consider three cases of possibility. It is possible that a tossed coin lands on heads: the outcome is consistent with physics, the initial conditions are present, and there is a nonzero probability of heads. It is possible that it rains tomorrow: the meteorological conditions permit rain, and the probability is assessable. It is possible that a seed germinates: the biological conditions are favourable, and there is a real chance. From these cases one might define genuine possibility: a state of affairs is genuinely possible if and only if it is consistent with all known physical laws, the initial conditions for its occurrence are present, and there is a nonzero probability of its occurrence given those conditions.

But this definition, drawn from possibilities within our universe, proves too narrow when one considers the possibility that the fundamental constants of nature might have been different. Many physicists and philosophers hold that the gravitational constant, for example, might have had a different value — that a universe with stronger or weaker gravity is a genuine possibility. Such a universe would be consistent with the general mathematical form of physical law, though not with the specific parameters of our universe. But the initial conditions for a different gravitational constant are not present in our universe; our universe has the gravitational constant it has, and no existing conditions tend towards altering it. Nor can a meaningful probability be assigned to a different value, for we have no ensemble of universes over which to define probabilities. The second and third conditions fail. The background cases were all possibilities within the fixed framework of our world, and the definition abstracted from this parochial standpoint. It cannot reach the broader class of possibilities concerning the fundamental parameters of nature itself.

background_cases: A green leaf turns red in autumn: it loses the property of being green and acquires the property of being red, while remaining the same leaf.
A block of ice melts into water: it loses the property of being solid and acquires the property of being liquid, while remaining the same quantity of H2O.
A child grows into an adult: the child loses certain properties (smallness, dependence) and acquires others (height, independence), while remaining the same person.
definition: An entity undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) it loses one intrinsic property and gains another, (ii) the entity persists as numerically the same individual, and (iii) the new property is incompatible with the old.
conditions: loses one intrinsic property and gains another
persists as numerically same individual
new property incompatible with old
counterexample: A man becomes the tallest person in the room, not because he has grown taller, but because the only taller person has left the room. He has gained a property — being the tallest — that he previously lacked. The new property is incompatible with the old (being the second tallest). He persists as the same individual. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet he has not undergone any genuine change; his intrinsic properties are exactly as they were. The change is merely relational or, as some philosophers say, a 'Cambridge change.'
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved changes in genuinely intrinsic properties — colour, physical state, bodily size — and the definition abstracted from this uniformity. But by requiring only that an intrinsic property be lost and gained, without specifying which kinds of properties count, the definition admits merely relational changes — such as becoming the tallest — that involve no real alteration of the entity itself.
passage:
Consider three cases of change. A green leaf turns red in autumn: it loses greenness and acquires redness, remaining the same leaf throughout. A block of ice melts into water: it loses solidity and acquires liquidity, remaining the same quantity of H2O. A child grows into an adult: small becomes tall, dependent becomes independent, the same person persisting throughout. From these cases one might define genuine change: an entity undergoes genuine change if and only if it loses one intrinsic property and gains another, persists as numerically the same individual, and the new property is incompatible with the old.

But the definition admits spurious cases. Consider a man standing in a room who is the second tallest person present. The tallest person leaves. The man is now the tallest person in the room. He has gained a property — being the tallest — that he previously lacked, and this property is incompatible with his former property of being the second tallest. He persists as the same individual. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the man has not undergone any genuine change. He has not grown; his height, his mass, his posture, every intrinsic feature of his body is exactly as it was a moment ago. What has changed is merely his relation to the other occupants of the room. The background cases — the leaf, the ice, the child — all involved changes in genuinely intrinsic properties, and the definition abstracted incompatible-property exchange as the mark of change. But it failed to require that the exchanged properties be intrinsic in the right way, and so it admits merely relational changes that involve no real alteration of the entity itself.

background_cases: A man persists from youth to old age: his body changes, but a continuous stream of consciousness and a continuous bodily life link the boy to the man.
A cat persists from kitten to adult: the kitten grows, but the same organism endures throughout, linked by biological continuity.
An oak tree persists from acorn to ancient timber: the seedling and the great tree are connected by an unbroken chain of growth.
definition: An organism persists through time if and only if (i) there is biological continuity between its earlier and later stages, (ii) the organism maintains its characteristic life functions throughout, and (iii) the organism is spatiotemporally continuous.
conditions: biological continuity between earlier and later stages
maintains characteristic life functions throughout
spatiotemporally continuous
counterexample: Certain species of tardigrade can enter a state of cryptobiosis in which all metabolic activity ceases — no respiration, no digestion, no reproduction, no detectable life function whatever. The organism can remain in this state for decades. When favourable conditions return, it resumes its life functions. The tardigrade persists as the same organism, linked by biological continuity and spatiotemporal continuity. But during cryptobiosis it does not maintain its characteristic life functions. The second condition fails, yet the organism persists.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the man, the cat, the oak — all involved organisms whose life functions continued without interruption. The definition abstracted the maintenance of life functions as essential to persistence. But the tardigrade shows that an organism can persist through an interval in which all life functions cease, provided the capacity for resumption is retained. Persistence of organisms requires continuity of structure, not continuity of function.
passage:
Consider three cases of organic persistence. A man persists from youth to old age: his body changes profoundly, but a continuous stream of consciousness and a continuous bodily existence link the boy to the old man. A cat persists from kitten to adult: the organism endures, linked by biological continuity. An oak tree persists from acorn to ancient timber: growth is unbroken, and the seedling and the great tree are the same organism. From these cases one might propose that an organism persists through time if and only if there is biological continuity between its earlier and later stages, it maintains its characteristic life functions throughout, and it is spatiotemporally continuous.

But this definition, drawn from organisms whose life functions are sustained without interruption, cannot accommodate certain remarkable cases. Consider the tardigrade, a microscopic animal capable of entering a state known as cryptobiosis. In cryptobiosis, all detectable metabolic activity ceases. There is no respiration, no digestion, no movement, no reproduction — no life function whatever. The organism is, to all outward appearances, inert. It can remain in this state for decades, enduring extremes of temperature, pressure, and radiation that would destroy any active organism. When favourable conditions return, the tardigrade resumes its life functions and continues as before. Biological continuity obtains — the same genetic material, the same cellular structure. Spatiotemporal continuity obtains — the organism has not been destroyed and reconstituted. But the second condition fails: during cryptobiosis, no characteristic life functions are maintained. The background cases all involved organisms in continuous activity, and the definition mistook the continuity of function for a necessary condition of persistence. The tardigrade shows that what is essential is the continuity of biological structure and the capacity for resumption, not the uninterrupted exercise of life functions.

background_cases: The wheel is part of the bicycle: removing the wheel diminishes the bicycle, and the wheel contributes to the bicycle's capacity for locomotion.
The heart is part of the body: removing the heart destroys the body's capacity for life, and the heart contributes to circulation.
The chapter is part of the book: removing the chapter diminishes the book's narrative, and the chapter contributes to the book's overall meaning.
definition: X is a part of Y if and only if (i) removing X would diminish Y, (ii) X contributes to the characteristic activity or purpose of Y, and (iii) X cannot perform its function independently of Y.
conditions: removing X would diminish Y
X contributes to characteristic activity of Y
X cannot perform function independently of Y
counterexample: A battery is part of a torch. Removing it would diminish the torch, and it contributes to the torch's function of illumination. But the battery can perform its function — supplying electrical current — independently of the torch; it can power a radio, a clock, or any other device that requires a battery. The third condition fails, yet the battery is plainly a part of the torch.
abductive_insight: The background cases — wheel, heart, chapter — all involved parts whose functions are specialised to the wholes they serve: a bicycle wheel is of little use outside a bicycle, a heart serves no purpose outside a body, a chapter makes little sense outside its book. The definition abstracted functional dependence as essential to parthood. But many parts — batteries, screws, standardised components — can perform their functions in a wide variety of wholes, and their transferability does not diminish their status as parts of the wholes in which they currently serve.
passage:
Consider three cases of parthood. The wheel is part of the bicycle: removing it diminishes the bicycle, and the wheel contributes to the bicycle's function. The heart is part of the body: removing it destroys the body, and the heart contributes to circulation. The chapter is part of the book: removing it diminishes the narrative, and the chapter contributes to the book's meaning. From these cases one might define parthood: X is a part of Y if and only if removing X would diminish Y, X contributes to the characteristic activity of Y, and X cannot perform its function independently of Y.

But this definition, drawn from parts whose functions are specialised to their wholes, proves too restrictive. Consider a battery in a torch. Removing the battery diminishes the torch — it will no longer function. The battery contributes to the torch's characteristic activity of illumination. But the battery can perform its function — supplying electrical current — independently of the torch. The very same battery can power a radio, a clock, or a small motor; its function is not tied to any particular whole. The third condition fails. Yet the battery is plainly a part of the torch: it is contained within the torch, it contributes to the torch's operation, and its removal renders the torch useless. The background cases all involved parts whose functions were specialised to their wholes — the bicycle wheel, the biological heart, the narrative chapter — and the definition abstracted this specialisation as essential. But many parts are standardised, interchangeable components whose function is not confined to any one whole, and their generality of function does not diminish their status as parts of the wholes they currently serve.

background_cases: The interior of a room is a region of space: it is three-dimensional, it is bounded by walls, floor, and ceiling, and it can be traversed by a body moving in a straight line.
The interior of a sphere is a region of space: it is three-dimensional, it is bounded by a surface, and straight-line traversal is possible within it.
An open field is a region of space: it is three-dimensional, it extends in every direction to its boundaries, and a body can traverse it along a straight path.
definition: A region is a portion of Euclidean space if and only if (i) it is three-dimensional, (ii) straight lines within it satisfy Euclid's parallel postulate, and (iii) the angles of any triangle within it sum to exactly 180 degrees.
conditions: three-dimensional
straight lines satisfy parallel postulate
angles of any triangle sum to 180 degrees
counterexample: The surface of the earth, considered as a two-dimensional space, is a region in which the parallel postulate fails: lines of longitude that are parallel at the equator converge and meet at the poles. A triangle drawn on the earth's surface — say, from the North Pole to two points on the equator — has angles summing to more than 180 degrees. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the surface of the earth is a perfectly good region of space; it is simply a space of non-Euclidean geometry.
abductive_insight: The background cases — rooms, spheres, fields — were all regions of flat, Euclidean space in which the parallel postulate holds and triangle angles sum to 180 degrees. The definition abstracted these Euclidean features as essential. But the geometry of space is an empirical matter, and curved spaces — the surface of a sphere, the large-scale geometry of the universe — are genuine spaces that violate Euclidean conditions.
passage:
Consider three cases of spatial regions. The interior of a room is a region of space: three-dimensional, bounded, and such that straight lines within it obey the familiar postulates of Euclid. The interior of a sphere is likewise three-dimensional, bounded, and Euclidean. An open field extends in three dimensions, and the geometry of paths within it conforms to the Euclidean ideal. From these cases one might define a region of space: a region is a portion of Euclidean space if and only if it is three-dimensional, straight lines within it satisfy the parallel postulate, and the angles of any triangle within it sum to exactly 180 degrees.

But this definition, drawn from flat, local regions, cannot accommodate the geometry of curved spaces. Consider the surface of the earth as a two-dimensional space. Draw two lines of longitude from the equator towards the North Pole. At the equator they are parallel — they point in the same direction and are equidistant. But as they extend northward they converge, meeting at the Pole. The parallel postulate fails: lines that begin parallel do not remain equidistant. Draw a triangle from the North Pole to two points on the equator ninety degrees apart. The angle at the Pole is ninety degrees, and each angle at the equator is ninety degrees; the triangle's angles sum to 270 degrees, far more than 180. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the surface of the earth is a perfectly genuine space — it is simply not Euclidean. The background cases were all regions of flat space, and the definition mistook the Euclidean properties of these particular regions for necessary features of space as such. Space, it appears, may be curved as well as flat.

background_cases: A particular lump of gold, which retains its chemical nature through all alterations of shape and size, persisting as gold whether it is melted, hammered, or drawn into wire.
A diamond, which preserves its crystalline structure and hardness through all changes of location, temperature, and ownership, remaining the same substance throughout.
A block of marble, which endures through the sculptor's work, retaining its material identity even as chips are removed and its shape is altered.
definition: A thing is a substance if and only if (i) it is composed of a single, uniform material throughout, (ii) it retains its identity through all changes of shape, size, and location, and (iii) it is indestructible under ordinary physical conditions.
conditions: composed of single uniform material throughout
retains identity through all changes of shape, size, and location
indestructible under ordinary physical conditions
counterexample: A living horse is a substance if anything is — it is a unified, self-sustaining, persisting individual. Yet it is not composed of a single, uniform material; it is composed of bone, muscle, blood, nerve, cartilage, and many other tissues, heterogeneous in the extreme. It does not retain its identity through all changes of shape; a horse that lost all four legs would be so changed as to raise grave questions about the conditions of its continued identity. And it is far from indestructible under ordinary conditions; it is fragile, mortal, and subject to disease and death. All three conditions fail, yet the horse is a paradigmatic substance.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all homogeneous, inorganic materials — gold, diamond, marble — whose persistence depends upon chemical and structural uniformity. The definition abstracted uniformity, shape-independence, and indestructibility as essential to substance, but the living horse reveals that substances may be radically heterogeneous, vulnerable, and mortal, their unity consisting not in material uniformity but in the organisation of diverse parts into a functioning whole.
passage:
Consider three cases of substance. A lump of gold, which retains its chemical nature through all alterations of shape and size, persisting as gold whether melted, hammered, or drawn into wire. A diamond, which preserves its crystalline structure through all vicissitudes of location and temperature. A block of marble, which endures through the sculptor's chisel, remaining the same material even as its shape is altered. From these cases one might extract a definition: a thing is a substance if and only if it is composed of a single, uniform material throughout, it retains its identity through all changes of shape, size, and location, and it is indestructible under ordinary physical conditions.

But this definition, drawn from homogeneous, inorganic materials, proves far too narrow when applied to the substances one encounters in the living world. Consider a horse. A horse is a substance if anything is: it is a unified, self-sustaining, persisting individual with a nature of its own. Yet it is emphatically not composed of a single, uniform material. It is made up of bone, muscle, blood, nerve, skin, cartilage, and a host of other tissues, each with its own character and composition. It is heterogeneous through and through. Furthermore, it does not retain its identity through all changes of shape; there are alterations so drastic — the loss of limbs, the wasting of disease — that the continued identity of the animal may be called into question. And it is assuredly not indestructible under ordinary physical conditions; on the contrary, it is mortal, vulnerable to injury and illness, and will in the natural course of events perish. All three conditions fail. The background cases were inorganic stuffs whose persistence depends upon chemical uniformity and physical durability. The definition mistook these particular features for universal requirements of substance, overlooking the living substances whose unity consists not in homogeneity or indestructibility but in the organised cooperation of diverse, perishable parts.

background_cases: A billiard ball strikes a second ball, which immediately begins to move. The impact of the first ball is the cause of the motion of the second.
A stone is thrown at a window, striking it and shattering it. The impact of the stone is the cause of the shattering.
A spark falls into a pile of dry leaves, which immediately ignites. The spark is the cause of the fire.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C involves a transfer of energy or momentum to the system in which E occurs, (ii) C and E are linked by a continuous physical process, and (iii) the magnitude of E is proportional to the magnitude of C.
conditions: C involves transfer of energy or momentum to system in which E occurs
C and E linked by continuous physical process
magnitude of E proportional to magnitude of C
counterexample: A signalman pulls a lever, which releases a set of points on a railway line, and a train that would have continued straight is diverted onto a branch line, where it travels for a hundred miles. The pulling of the lever is the cause of the train's diversion, yet the energy transferred by the signalman's arm is negligible compared to the kinetic energy of the train. There is no proportionality between the cause and the effect. The signalman did not push the train; he merely redirected it. The third condition fails, and arguably the first is doubtful as well, since the signalman's energy transfer is not what moves the train.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of mechanical impact, where a forceful collision transfers energy and produces a proportionate effect. The definition abstracted energy transfer, continuous physical process, and proportionality as essential features of causation, but the signalman's lever reveals that many causes operate not by transferring energy but by redirecting or releasing forces already present, functioning as triggers or switches rather than as sources of power.
passage:
Consider three cases of causation. A billiard ball strikes a second, which begins to move. A stone is thrown at a window and shatters it. A spark falls into dry leaves and ignites them. From these cases one might extract a definition: an event C causes an event E if and only if C involves a transfer of energy or momentum to the system in which E occurs, C and E are linked by a continuous physical process, and the magnitude of E is proportional to the magnitude of C.

This definition, drawn from cases of mechanical impact, captures a familiar and satisfying picture of causation: causes are forceful events that push, strike, and transfer energy, producing effects in proportion to their power. But consider a rather different case. A signalman on a railway line pulls a lever, which releases a set of points. A train that would have continued straight along the main line is diverted onto a branch, where it travels for a hundred miles before reaching its new destination. The pulling of the lever is indisputably the cause of the train's diversion; without it, the train would have gone elsewhere. Yet the energy transferred by the signalman's arm in pulling the lever is minuscule — a few foot-pounds at most — whilst the kinetic energy of the moving train is enormous. There is no proportionality between the magnitude of the cause and the magnitude of the effect. Moreover, the signalman did not transfer energy to the train; the train's energy came from its engine. What the signalman did was redirect a process already in motion. The background cases were all collisions in which a forceful impact produced a proportionate result. The definition abstracted these features as essential, but the signalman's lever shows that many causes operate not by supplying energy but by directing, releasing, or channelling forces that are already present — functioning as triggers or switches rather than as engines, and producing effects vastly disproportionate to the energy they contribute.

background_cases: The ageing of a man from youth to old age, in which each successive stage differs qualitatively from the last, the changes accumulating visibly over the decades.
The slow erosion of a cliff face by the sea, each year wearing away a little more rock, the cliff gradually retreating from its original position.
The growth of a tree from a sapling to a mature specimen, the trunk thickening, the branches multiplying, the canopy spreading wider with each passing year.
definition: Time passes if and only if (i) things undergo qualitative change, (ii) these changes are gradual and cumulative, and (iii) the passage of time is measurable by the degree of change that has occurred.
conditions: things undergo qualitative change
changes are gradual and cumulative
passage of time measurable by degree of change
counterexample: Consider a perfectly preserved fly in a piece of amber, sealed forty million years ago. The fly has undergone no qualitative change in that vast interval; it is, to all appearances, exactly as it was on the day it was entombed. The changes are neither gradual nor cumulative — there have been none. And the passage of time cannot be measured by the degree of change, for there has been no change to measure. All three conditions fail. Yet forty million years have passed, and the fly has persisted through an immensity of time.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all dynamic processes — ageing, erosion, growth — in which the passage of time is made manifest by visible, cumulative change. The definition equated temporal passage with qualitative alteration, but the fly in amber demonstrates that time may pass without any change in the thing through which it passes, and that the passage of time is not constituted by change but is rather the dimension in which change may or may not occur.
passage:
Consider three cases of the passage of time. The ageing of a man from youth to old age, each stage differing qualitatively from the last. The erosion of a cliff by the sea, each year wearing away more rock. The growth of a tree from sapling to maturity, its trunk thickening and its canopy spreading with each decade. From these cases one might extract a definition: time passes if and only if things undergo qualitative change, these changes are gradual and cumulative, and the passage of time is measurable by the degree of change that has occurred.

This definition, drawn from dynamic processes, captures a natural and appealing intuition: that time is made manifest in the alterations of things, that the grey hair and the lined face are the very evidence of time's passage. But consider a case of a very different character. A fly is trapped in a droplet of resin some forty million years ago. The resin hardens into amber, sealing the fly in a transparent tomb. When the amber is discovered and examined in the present day, the fly is perfectly preserved — its wings, its legs, its compound eyes are exactly as they were on the day of its entombment. It has undergone no qualitative change whatever. The changes are not gradual and cumulative; there have been no changes at all. And the passage of time cannot be measured by the degree of alteration, for there has been no alteration to measure. All three conditions fail. Yet forty million years have indisputably passed. The geological strata in which the amber is found, the radioactive decay of surrounding minerals, the evolution of the species to which the fly belonged — all attest to the immensity of the elapsed time. The background cases were all processes in which change and time went hand in hand. The definition mistook this correlation for an identity, equating temporal passage with qualitative alteration, when the fly in amber shows that time passes regardless of whether anything changes, and that the passage of time is the dimension in which change may occur rather than the change itself.

background_cases: A man recognised by his friends after an absence of twenty years, his identity confirmed by his face, his voice, his mannerisms, and his memories of shared experiences.
A painting authenticated as the work of a particular artist by comparison of its brushwork, pigments, and style with the artist's known works.
A criminal identified by his fingerprints, which match those taken at the time of his arrest years earlier.
definition: An entity X is the same entity as Y if and only if (i) X and Y share a set of qualitative features that are unique to them and to no other entity, (ii) these features are stable over time, and (iii) the features can be detected by a suitably equipped observer.
conditions: share qualitative features unique to them and no other entity
features stable over time
features detectable by suitably equipped observer
counterexample: Consider a pair of identical twins at the moment of their birth. They share all their qualitative features: the same DNA, the same physical appearance, the same weight and length, the same blood type. No observer, however well equipped, can distinguish one from the other on the basis of qualitative features alone; every detectable feature of the one is matched by the other. The first condition fails — their features are not unique to one of them — and yet each twin is a distinct individual, numerically different from the other. Their identity as distinct entities is a fact that no catalogue of shared qualities can capture.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved re-identifying a single entity across time by means of distinctive qualitative markers — a face, a brushstroke, a fingerprint. The definition treated qualitative uniqueness as the ground of identity, but identical twins show that numerical identity and distinctness are not constituted by qualitative features at all; two entities may share every qualitative feature and yet be distinct, their distinctness grounded in their separate spatiotemporal origins rather than in any qualitative difference.
passage:
Consider three cases of identity. A man recognised by his friends after twenty years' absence, his identity confirmed by his face, his voice, and his memories. A painting authenticated by the comparison of brushwork and pigments with the artist's known output. A criminal identified by fingerprints matching those taken years earlier. From these cases one might extract a definition: an entity X is the same entity as Y if and only if X and Y share a set of qualitative features unique to them and to no other entity, these features are stable over time, and they can be detected by a suitably equipped observer.

This definition captures the ordinary practice of re-identification admirably. We recognise a friend by his distinctive features; we authenticate a painting by its unique stylistic signature; we identify a criminal by the singular pattern of his fingerprints. In each case, qualitative uniqueness serves as the criterion of identity. But consider a pair of identical twins at the moment of their birth. They share all their qualitative features without exception. Their DNA is the same, their physical appearance is indistinguishable, their weight and length are identical, their blood type matches. No observer, however well equipped with microscopes and instruments, can find a qualitative feature that belongs to one and not to the other. The first condition fails: their qualitative features are not unique to one of them. Yet each twin is a distinct individual. They are two, not one. They will go on to lead different lives, have different experiences, and become different persons. Their numerical distinctness is a brute fact that cannot be grounded in qualitative differences, for at the outset there are none. The background cases were all situations in which qualitative markers sufficed for re-identification. The definition mistook these practical criteria for the metaphysical ground of identity, when in truth identity and distinctness are prior to qualitative features, and two entities may share every quality and yet be irreducibly two.

background_cases: The redness of a ripe tomato, which is the very same redness found in a red postbox, a red rose, and a red sunset — a single property present in many particulars.
The sphericality of a billiard ball, which is the same sphericality found in a marble, a soap bubble, and the full moon — one shape instantiated in many objects.
The hardness of a diamond, which is the same hardness found in sapphire and corundum — a single physical property shared by distinct minerals.
definition: A universal is a property if and only if (i) it is a quality directly perceivable by the senses, (ii) it is present in the particular as an intrinsic feature, not dependent upon the particular's relations to other things, and (iii) it admits of degrees — that is, a thing can have more or less of it.
conditions: directly perceivable by the senses
present as intrinsic feature not dependent on relations
admits of degrees
counterexample: Consider the property of being prime, as instantiated by the numbers three, seven, and thirteen. Primality is not perceivable by the senses; one cannot see, hear, or touch it. It is not clear that it is intrinsic to a number in the way that redness is intrinsic to a tomato; whether a number is prime depends upon its relations to other numbers — specifically, upon whether any number other than one and itself divides it evenly. And primality does not admit of degrees; a number is either prime or it is not. All three conditions fail, yet primality is a genuine universal, a property shared by infinitely many distinct particulars.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all perceptible physical qualities — colour, shape, hardness — that are intrinsic to their bearers and admit of gradation. The definition treated these sensory and scalar features as essential to universals, but primality reveals that universals may be abstract, relational, and all-or-nothing, their reality consisting not in sensory manifestation but in the objective, recurrent structure they impose upon their instances.
passage:
Consider three cases of universals. The redness of a ripe tomato, which is the very same redness found in a postbox, a rose, and a sunset. The sphericality of a billiard ball, the same shape instantiated in a marble, a soap bubble, and the full moon. The hardness of a diamond, shared by sapphire and corundum. From these cases one might extract a definition: a universal is a property if and only if it is a quality directly perceivable by the senses, it is present in the particular as an intrinsic feature not dependent upon relations to other things, and it admits of degrees.

This definition, drawn from perceptible physical qualities, captures a natural and intuitive picture of universals: they are the colours, shapes, and textures we encounter in experience, features that inhere in objects and that can be greater or lesser in degree. But consider a property of a rather different character. The property of being prime — that is, of being a natural number divisible only by one and itself — is instantiated by three, by seven, by thirteen, and by infinitely many other numbers. It is a genuine universal: a single property recurring in multiple distinct particulars. Yet it is not perceivable by the senses. One cannot see primality as one sees redness; it is apprehended by the intellect, not by the eyes or the hands. Nor is it straightforwardly intrinsic in the way that redness is intrinsic to a tomato. Whether a number is prime depends upon its divisibility relations to other numbers — it is, in a sense, a relational property. And primality does not admit of degrees: a number is either prime or it is not; there is no being more or less prime. All three conditions fail. The background cases were sensory, scalar, intrinsic qualities. The definition abstracted these features as essential to universals, but primality shows that universals may be abstract, relational, and binary, their reality consisting not in sensory presence but in the objective structure they impose upon their instances.

background_cases: It is possible for this chair to be in the next room, since we can imagine carrying it there and placing it by the window.
It is possible for this glass of water to be frozen, since we can imagine placing it in sufficiently cold conditions.
It is possible for this piece of paper to be folded into an aeroplane, since we can imagine a person folding it in the appropriate manner.
definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) some agent could, in principle, bring S about through a series of physical actions, (ii) S is compatible with the present state of the world, and (iii) the transition from the actual state to S does not require the violation of any law of nature.
conditions: some agent could in principle bring S about through physical actions
S compatible with present state of world
transition does not require violation of any law of nature
counterexample: It is possible that the earth might never have existed — that the particular cloud of gas and dust from which the solar system formed might have dispersed without coalescing into planets. This is a genuine possibility; there is nothing in the laws of nature that required the earth to form. Yet no agent could bring this about through physical actions, for the event in question lies in the past and concerns the initial conditions of the solar system. Nor is it compatible with the present state of the world, which includes the earth as one of its constituents. All three conditions fail, yet the possibility is genuine.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all practical possibilities — things an agent could do to nearby objects by manipulating them in familiar ways. The definition treated possibility as a species of practical feasibility, but the possibility that the earth might never have formed is a possibility concerning initial conditions and unrealised histories, which no agent could enact and which is incompatible with the present state of affairs, revealing that metaphysical possibility extends far beyond what any agent could bring about.
passage:
Consider three cases of possibility. It is possible for this chair to be in the next room, for we can imagine carrying it there. It is possible for this glass of water to be frozen, for we can imagine placing it in the cold. It is possible for this piece of paper to be folded into an aeroplane, for we can imagine someone folding it. From these cases one might extract a definition: a state of affairs S is possible if and only if some agent could, in principle, bring S about through a series of physical actions, S is compatible with the present state of the world, and the transition from the actual state to S does not require the violation of any law of nature.

This definition, drawn from everyday practical possibilities, captures the sense in which familiar objects might be otherwise than they are. But consider a possibility of a very different order. It is possible that the earth might never have existed — that the cloud of interstellar gas and dust from which the solar system formed might have dispersed without coalescing into planets. There is nothing in the laws of physics that required the earth to form; the process depended upon particular initial conditions that might well have been different. The possibility is genuine and is recognised as such by cosmologists and philosophers alike. Yet no agent could bring this about through physical actions. The formation of the earth lies billions of years in the past, and no action in the present can alter the initial conditions of the solar system. The state of affairs is not compatible with the present state of the world, which includes the earth as one of its most prominent constituents. And the question of transition does not arise, for the possibility concerns an alternative history of the universe rather than a change from the present to some future state. All three conditions fail. The background cases were practical possibilities — things one might do to objects within arm's reach. The definition mistook practical feasibility for metaphysical possibility, when in truth possibility extends to unrealised histories, alternative initial conditions, and states of affairs that no agent could produce and that are incompatible with the world as it is.

background_cases: A clock, whose parts — the case, the face, the hands, the springs, the gears — are assembled by a craftsman into a functioning whole that tells the time.
A house, whose parts — the bricks, the timber, the tiles, the plaster — are combined by builders into a structure that provides shelter and habitation.
A ship, whose parts — the hull, the mast, the rigging, the rudder — are joined by shipwrights into a vessel that can navigate the sea.
definition: A collection of parts constitutes a genuine whole if and only if (i) the parts have been deliberately assembled by an agent, (ii) the arrangement of the parts serves a function or purpose, and (iii) the whole would not exist if the parts were merely scattered at random.
conditions: parts deliberately assembled by an agent
arrangement serves a function or purpose
whole would not exist if parts scattered at random
counterexample: A stalactite in a cave is a genuine whole — a unified, bounded, persisting object with a determinate shape and structure. Yet it was not assembled by any agent; it formed over millennia through the gradual deposition of calcium carbonate from dripping water. Its structure does not serve a function or purpose in any teleological sense; it exists because of blind chemical processes. And although it would not exist if its constituent particles were scattered at random, the same is true of any material object whatever, so the third condition is trivially satisfied and does no distinguishing work. The first and second conditions fail, yet the stalactite is a whole as genuine as any clock or house.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all artefacts — objects deliberately made by human agents for particular purposes. The definition treated deliberate assembly and functional purpose as necessary conditions for wholehood, but the stalactite shows that genuine wholes may arise from natural processes without any agent or purpose, their unity consisting in the causal-historical process that produced them rather than in any intentional design.
passage:
Consider three cases of wholes. A clock, whose parts are assembled by a craftsman into a functioning timepiece. A house, whose materials are combined by builders into a habitable structure. A ship, whose components are joined by shipwrights into a seaworthy vessel. From these cases one might extract a definition: a collection of parts constitutes a genuine whole if and only if the parts have been deliberately assembled by an agent, the arrangement serves a function or purpose, and the whole would not exist if the parts were scattered at random.

This definition, drawn from artefacts, captures the intuition that wholes are more than mere collections — that they possess an organisation and a unity that distinguishes them from heaps and aggregates. But consider a stalactite hanging from the ceiling of a limestone cave. The stalactite is a genuine whole: it is a unified, bounded, persisting object with a determinate shape, a characteristic internal structure, and a continuous history. It can be weighed, measured, and described. It is not a mere aggregate of particles; it has a form that distinguishes it from the surrounding rock. Yet it was not deliberately assembled by any agent. It formed over thousands of years through the gradual deposition of calcium carbonate from water percolating through the limestone above. No craftsman designed it; no builder arranged its parts. Its structure does not serve a function or purpose in any teleological sense; it is the product of blind chemical and physical processes operating over immense spans of time. The first and second conditions fail decisively. The third condition — that the whole would not exist if its parts were scattered at random — is satisfied, but trivially so, for the same could be said of any material arrangement whatever. The background cases were all products of human intention and design. The definition mistook intentional assembly and purposive organisation for essential features of wholehood, overlooking the natural wholes that arise from causal processes without any guiding intelligence, their unity constituted by the history of their formation rather than by any purpose they serve.

background_cases: A billiard ball strikes a second ball, and the second ball begins to move; the cause and the effect are two distinct events connected by spatial contact and temporal succession.
A stone is released from a height and falls to the ground; the release is the cause and the fall is the effect, two distinct events separated by a measurable interval of time.
A spark ignites a quantity of gunpowder, producing an explosion; the spark is the cause and the explosion is the effect, two discrete events linked by a physical mechanism.
definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C and E are two distinct events, (ii) C is temporally prior to E, and (iii) C is connected to E by a physical mechanism that transfers energy or momentum from C to E.
conditions: C and E are two distinct events
C is temporally prior to E
C is connected to E by a physical mechanism that transfers energy or momentum from C to E
counterexample: Consider the case of a person's decision to raise his arm and the subsequent rising of the arm. On one natural description, the decision is the cause of the bodily movement. But the decision and the movement are not obviously two distinct events in the way that the collision of billiard balls comprises two distinct events; the decision is intimately bound up with the movement in a manner that resists clean separation. Moreover, it is far from clear that the decision is temporally prior to the movement in any straightforward sense; neurophysiological investigations suggest that the neural processes underlying the movement may begin before the agent is conscious of having decided. And the connection between the mental event of deciding and the physical event of the arm's rising does not consist in a transfer of energy or momentum of the sort that connects the billiard ball to its target. Yet one would be reluctant to deny that the decision is a cause of the movement.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of mechanical causation — collisions, falls, explosions — in which cause and effect are clearly distinct events, temporally ordered, and connected by a physical mechanism involving the transfer of energy. The definition abstracted these features as essential to causation. But the cases were drawn from the narrow domain of physical mechanics, and the definition mistook the features of mechanical causation for the features of causation as such. The case of mental causation — a decision causing a bodily movement — reveals that causes may be intimately intertwined with their effects rather than cleanly distinct from them, may not be straightforwardly temporally prior, and may not operate through a transfer of energy or momentum. The definition confused one species of causation with the genus.
passage:
Consider three cases that are paradigmatic instances of causation. A billiard ball strikes a second ball, and the second ball begins to move; the impact transfers momentum from the first ball to the second, and the two events — the collision and the subsequent motion — are clearly distinct and temporally ordered. A stone is released from a height and falls to the ground; the release precedes the fall, and the gravitational mechanism connecting them is well understood. A spark ignites a quantity of gunpowder, producing an explosion; the spark and the explosion are distinct events, the spark is temporally prior, and the mechanism of ignition transfers energy from the spark to the powder. From these cases one might extract a definition: an event is a cause of another event if and only if the two are distinct events, the cause is temporally prior to the effect, and the cause is connected to the effect by a physical mechanism that transfers energy or momentum.

But one must consider the case of a person who decides to raise his arm and the subsequent rising of the arm. On any natural description, the decision is the cause of the movement. Yet the decision and the movement are not obviously two distinct events in the manner of colliding billiard balls. The decision is not an event that occurs and then, after a perceptible interval, produces a separate event; it is intimately bound up with the movement, intertwined with it in ways that resist clean analysis. Nor is it clear that the decision is temporally prior to the movement in any straightforward sense. Neurophysiological investigations have revealed that the neural processes underlying voluntary movement may be initiated before the agent is conscious of having decided; the temporal ordering of decision and movement is far more complex than the ordering of spark and explosion. And the connection between the mental event of deciding and the physical event of the arm's rising does not consist in a transfer of energy or momentum of the familiar mechanical sort. No billiard ball strikes another; no spark ignites a powder. The relation between a decision and its bodily execution remains one of the most puzzling in the whole of philosophy. The definition, drawn from cases of mechanical causation involving discrete physical events connected by energy transfer, mistook the features of that particular domain for the essential features of causation, and thereby excluded the vast and important domain of mental causation, in which causes and effects are intimately intertwined rather than cleanly separable.

background_cases: It is possible that this chair might have been painted red rather than blue, since its colour is a contingent property that could have been otherwise without affecting its identity.
It is possible that the next general election might be held in October rather than in May, since the timing of elections is a contingent matter determined by political circumstance.
It is possible that a particular ship might have been constructed from different timbers, since the material from which an artefact is made is a contingent feature of the artefact.
definition: A proposition P is possibly true if and only if (i) P describes a state of affairs that is compatible with the laws of logic, (ii) P describes a state of affairs that involves only a rearrangement or substitution of actually existing entities or properties, and (iii) P describes a state of affairs that a suitably informed person could coherently imagine.
conditions: P describes a state of affairs compatible with the laws of logic
P describes a state of affairs involving only a rearrangement or substitution of actually existing entities or properties
P describes a state of affairs that a suitably informed person could coherently imagine
counterexample: Consider the proposition that there might have existed a colour that no human being has ever perceived — a colour wholly unlike red, blue, green, or any other colour in the spectrum of human experience. This proposition does not describe a rearrangement or substitution of actually existing entities or properties; it posits a property that does not actually exist and is not a recombination of existing properties. Nor is it clear that a suitably informed person could coherently imagine such a colour, since one cannot form a mental image of a colour one has never experienced. The second and third conditions fail. Yet the proposition seems genuinely possible; there is no logical contradiction in the supposition that the space of possible colours exceeds the space of actual human colour experience.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all possibilities involving the rearrangement of familiar, actually existing entities and properties — repainting a chair, rescheduling an election, substituting one set of timbers for another. The definition abstracted the recombinatorial character of these possibilities as essential to possibility itself. But the cases were drawn from the narrow domain of mundane, easily imaginable rearrangements of the actual, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of possibility as such. The case of an unperceived colour reveals that possibility may outrun the actual — that there may be genuinely possible properties that are not rearrangements of actually existing properties and that resist coherent imagination. The definition confused the epistemically accessible possibilities with the full range of metaphysical possibility.
passage:
Consider three propositions that are plainly possible. It is possible that this chair, which is blue, might have been painted red; the colour of the chair is a contingent property, and its replacement by another actual colour involves no logical difficulty. It is possible that the next general election might be held in October rather than in May; the timing is a contingent matter, and the substitution of one date for another is easily conceived. It is possible that a particular ship might have been constructed from different timbers; the material is contingent, and timber is timber. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proposition is possibly true if and only if it describes a state of affairs compatible with the laws of logic, involves only a rearrangement or substitution of actually existing entities or properties, and describes a state of affairs that a suitably informed person could coherently imagine.

But one must consider the proposition that there might have existed a colour wholly unlike any colour that any human being has ever perceived — a colour that is not red, not blue, not green, not a shade or mixture of any colour in the spectrum of human experience, but something altogether alien to our perceptual capacities. This proposition does not describe a rearrangement or substitution of actually existing properties. It posits a property that does not actually exist and that is not a recombination of existing properties; it asserts the possibility of something genuinely novel, something that stands outside the inventory of the actual. Nor is it evident that a suitably informed person could coherently imagine such a colour. One cannot form a mental image of a colour one has never experienced; the attempt to imagine an alien colour seems to collapse into the imagination of a known colour or into blankness. Yet the proposition appears to be genuinely possible. There is no logical contradiction in the supposition that the space of possible colours exceeds the space of colours accessible to human perception. The laws of logic do not restrict the range of possible properties to those that happen to be instantiated in the actual world. The definition, drawn from cases involving the rearrangement of familiar actualities, mistook the features of that restricted class of possibilities for the features of possibility in general, and thereby excluded possibilities that transcend the actual and resist imaginative reconstruction.

background_cases: A watch is composed of its case, dial, hands, gears, and mainspring; one can enumerate the parts, and the watch is the sum of them.
A house is composed of its foundations, walls, floors, roof, and fittings; the house is the totality of its structural components.
A chess set is composed of thirty-two pieces and a board; the set is the collection of its discrete, enumerable parts.
definition: An object O is a whole composed of parts if and only if (i) the parts of O are discrete, enumerable entities, (ii) the identity of O is determined entirely by the identity of its parts, and (iii) the removal of any part diminishes O without destroying it.
conditions: the parts of O are discrete, enumerable entities
the identity of O is determined entirely by the identity of its parts
the removal of any part diminishes O without destroying it
counterexample: Consider a cloud. A cloud is a whole composed of parts — water droplets or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere. But the parts are not discrete, enumerable entities in the manner of watch-gears or chess pieces; they are countless, in constant flux, evaporating and condensing, entering and leaving the cloud at every moment. The identity of the cloud is not determined by the identity of its parts; the droplets are continuously replaced, yet the cloud persists. And the removal of a part — a single droplet — does not diminish the cloud in any meaningful sense; the cloud's identity and persistence are indifferent to the fate of any particular droplet. Yet the cloud is a whole composed of parts.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all composed of discrete, stable, enumerable parts — gears, walls, chess pieces — whose identity was fixed and whose relation to the whole was determinate. The definition abstracted discreteness, enumerability, and the identity-determining character of parts as essential to mereological composition. But the cases were drawn from the domain of manufactured artefacts with well-defined components, and the definition mistook the features of that domain for the features of composition as such. The cloud reveals that a whole may be composed of parts that are not discrete or enumerable, that are in constant flux, and that do not determine the identity of the whole. The definition confused the neat mereology of artefacts with the general nature of part-whole relations.
passage:
Consider three objects that are plainly wholes composed of parts. A watch, composed of its case, dial, hands, gears, and mainspring — one can enumerate the parts, lay them out upon a table, and understand the watch as the sum of them. A house, composed of its foundations, walls, floors, roof, and fittings — the house is the totality of its structural components, and the removal of a wall diminishes the house without wholly destroying it. A chess set, composed of thirty-two pieces and a board — the set is the collection of its discrete parts, and the identity of the set is determined by the identity of those parts. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object is a whole composed of parts if and only if the parts are discrete, enumerable entities, the identity of the whole is determined entirely by the identity of its parts, and the removal of any part diminishes the whole without destroying it.

But one must consider a cloud. A cloud is, by any ordinary reckoning, a whole composed of parts — the parts being the water droplets or ice crystals that constitute it. Yet the parts of a cloud are not discrete, enumerable entities in the manner of watch-gears or chess pieces. They are countless — perhaps millions or billions of droplets in a single cumulus — and they are in constant flux. Droplets form by condensation, grow, merge, evaporate, and are replaced by new droplets, all within the span of minutes. The cloud is a dynamic, ever-changing assembly rather than a fixed collection of stable components. The identity of the cloud is not determined by the identity of its parts. One cannot point to a particular set of droplets and say that they constitute the cloud, for the droplets that compose the cloud at one moment are not the same droplets that compose it five minutes later, yet the cloud persists. And the removal of a part — a single droplet, or even a million droplets — does not diminish the cloud in any meaningful sense; new droplets form to replace those that are lost, and the cloud's continued existence is indifferent to the fate of any particular constituent. The definition, drawn from artefacts with well-defined, stable, enumerable components, mistook the neat mereology of manufactured objects for the general nature of part-whole composition, and thereby excluded the vast class of natural wholes whose parts are fluid, innumerable, and individually inessential.
