background_cases: The proposition that all bachelors are unmarried is necessarily true; its truth follows from the meanings of the words, and its denial involves a contradiction.

The proposition that no object is both wholly red and wholly green at the same point at the same time is necessarily true; the denial is incoherent, and the truth is grasped by reflection upon the concepts of colour.
The proposition that seven plus five equals twelve is necessarily true; it can be known by reason alone, and its denial is self-contradictory.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) the truth of P can be established by analysis of the meanings of its constituent terms, (ii) the denial of P is self-contradictory, and (iii) P is knowable by reason alone, without recourse to experience.
conditions: the truth of P can be established by analysis of the meanings of its constituent terms
the denial of P is self-contradictory
P is knowable by reason alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition that gold has the atomic number 79. This proposition is, by wide philosophical agreement, necessarily true: any substance that did not have atomic number 79 would not be gold, however closely it might resemble gold in colour, weight, and lustre. Gold is essentially the element with seventy-nine protons in its nucleus. Yet the truth of this proposition cannot be established by analysis of the meaning of the word 'gold'; the meaning of 'gold' as it is ordinarily understood — a heavy, yellow, lustrous metal — does not entail any particular atomic number. The denial of the proposition is not self-contradictory in the logical sense; 'gold does not have atomic number 79' contains no formal contradiction. And the proposition is not knowable by reason alone; it was discovered by empirical investigation — by the physicists and chemists who determined the structure of the atom. All three conditions fail, yet the proposition is necessarily true.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the bachelor proposition, the colour proposition, the arithmetical truth — were all analytic or conceptual truths, knowable a priori and whose denials are self-contradictory. The definition abstracted analyticity, self-contradictory denial, and a priori knowability as essential to necessity. But the background cases were drawn from the narrow class of truths whose necessity is grounded in the meanings of words or in the relations of concepts, and the definition mistook the features of that class for the features of necessity as such. The proposition about gold's atomic number reveals that there are necessary truths that are synthetic rather than analytic, whose denials are not self-contradictory, and that are known a posteriori through empirical investigation. The definition confused conceptual necessity with metaphysical necessity.
passage:
Consider three propositions that are paradigmatically necessary. The proposition that all bachelors are unmarried is necessarily true; one grasps its truth simply by understanding the meanings of the words, and its denial — that some bachelor is married — is self-contradictory. The proposition that no surface is both wholly red and wholly green at the same point at the same time is necessarily true; its denial is incoherent, and its truth is established by reflection upon the concepts of colour. The proposition that seven plus five equals twelve is necessarily true; it is knowable by reason alone, and its denial is absurd. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proposition is necessarily true if and only if its truth can be established by analysis of the meanings of its terms, its denial is self-contradictory, and it is knowable by reason alone without recourse to experience.

But one must consider the proposition that gold has the atomic number 79. This proposition is, by wide philosophical consensus, necessarily true. Any substance that did not possess atomic number 79 — that did not have seventy-nine protons in its nucleus — would not be gold, regardless of its superficial resemblance to gold. A substance might be yellow, heavy, lustrous, malleable, and resistant to corrosion, yet if it had seventy-eight protons or eighty, it would be platinum or mercury, not gold. The identity of gold is determined by its atomic number, and this determination holds of necessity. Yet the truth of the proposition cannot be established by analysis of the meaning of the word 'gold.' The ordinary meaning of the term — a heavy, yellow, precious metal — makes no reference to atomic number; a speaker who understands the word perfectly well may have no knowledge of atomic structure. The denial of the proposition — 'gold does not have atomic number 79' — is not self-contradictory in any logical sense; there is no formal inconsistency in the sentence, and a person ignorant of chemistry might assert it without incoherence. And the proposition is not knowable by reason alone. It was discovered through painstaking empirical investigation — through the work of the physicists who probed the structure of the atom and the chemists who established the periodic table. No amount of conceptual analysis or armchair reflection could have revealed it. The background cases were all drawn from the domain of analytic or conceptual truths — truths grounded in the meanings of words — and the definition abstracted the features of that domain as essential to necessity. The case of gold reveals that necessity extends beyond the analytic, that there are necessary truths which are discovered empirically and whose denials are coherent, and that the definition confused one species of necessity with the whole genus.

background_cases: A man strikes a match against a rough surface; the match ignites. The striking is the cause of the ignition: the two events are contiguous, the striking precedes the ignition, and the striking is something the agent did — an action performed upon the match.

A boy kicks a football; the football sails through the air and breaks a window. The kick is the cause of the breaking: the kick precedes the breaking, the kick is an action performed upon the ball, and the ball's trajectory connects the two events.

A woman pushes a book off the edge of a table; the book falls to the floor. The push is the cause of the fall: the push precedes the fall, it is an action performed upon the book, and the spatial path from table to floor is continuous.
definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C involves the action of an agent upon an object — that is, some person does something to something, and (iii) there is a continuous physical process connecting C and E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C involves the action of an agent upon an object
there is a continuous physical process connecting C and E
counterexample: Consider a boulder perched upon the lip of a cliff. Over the course of many years, the wind and the rain erode the rock beneath it, until one morning the boulder loses its support and tumbles into the valley below, crushing a fence. The erosion is the cause of the fall. The erosion temporally precedes the fall, and there is a continuous physical process — the gradual weakening of the supporting rock, the shift of the centre of gravity, the onset of motion — connecting the erosion with the boulder's descent. Yet no agent acts upon the boulder. No person pushes it, strikes it, or does anything to it. The wind and rain are natural forces, not agents; they do not act with intention or purpose. The second condition fails, yet the causal relation between the erosion and the fall is not in doubt.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of agent causation — a man striking a match, a boy kicking a ball, a woman pushing a book. In each case, a person performed a deliberate action upon an object, and the effect followed. The definition abstracted agency as essential to causation, treating the cause as something that an agent does. But the background cases were drawn from the narrow domain of human action upon objects, and the definition mistook a feature of that domain — the presence of an acting agent — for a necessary condition of causation as such. The boulder reveals that causation may operate through impersonal natural processes, without any agent performing any action.
passage:
Consider three cases in which one event causes another. A man strikes a match against a rough surface, and the match ignites: the friction generates heat, the heat reaches the ignition point of the match-head, and the flame appears. A boy kicks a football, and the football sails across the garden and breaks a neighbour's window: the force of the kick propels the ball along a trajectory that terminates in the glass. A woman pushes a book off the edge of a table, and the book falls to the floor: the push overcomes the friction that held the book in place, and gravity does the rest. From these cases one might extract a definition: an event is the cause of another if and only if it temporally precedes the effect, it involves the action of an agent upon an object, and there is a continuous physical process connecting the two.

But one must consider a boulder perched upon the lip of a cliff in a remote mountain valley. For many years the wind drives rain against the rock face, and the water seeps into cracks and fissures, expanding when it freezes, contracting when it thaws. The rock beneath the boulder is gradually weakened — grain by grain, season by season — until one morning in early spring the last supporting fragment gives way and the boulder tumbles down the cliff face into the valley below, where it crushes a shepherd's fence. The erosion is the cause of the fall; no one who examines the circumstances would doubt it. The erosion temporally precedes the fall by many years. There is a continuous physical process connecting the two — the progressive weakening of the rock, the shift in the boulder's centre of gravity past the point of support, the onset of motion under the influence of gravity. Yet no agent acts upon the boulder. No person pushes it, no hand strikes it, no conscious will directs its fall. The wind and the rain are impersonal natural forces; they do not act upon the boulder in the way that the man acts upon the match or the boy acts upon the ball. They have no intentions, no purposes, no designs. The background cases were all drawn from the domain of human action — striking, kicking, pushing — and the definition abstracted agency as essential to causation. The boulder reveals that causation extends far beyond the sphere of human action, that events may be caused by impersonal processes that involve no agent whatever, and that the definition confused the most familiar occasions of causal experience with the necessary conditions of causation itself.

background_cases: The interior of a room is a space: it is a bounded, three-dimensional region that contains objects — furniture, persons, air — and within which objects can be moved from one position to another.
A playing field is a space: it is a bounded, two-dimensional surface upon which players move and upon which the positions of the ball and the players can be specified.
The interior of a box is a space: it is a bounded, three-dimensional region that can contain objects and within which the positions of those objects can be described.
definition: A region R is a space if and only if (i) R is bounded — that is, R has limits or borders that distinguish it from what lies outside, (ii) R is capable of containing material objects, and (iii) the positions of objects within R can be specified by a finite set of numerical coordinates.
conditions: R is bounded — it has limits or borders
R is capable of containing material objects
positions within R can be specified by numerical coordinates
counterexample: Consider the open sky above a flat desert, stretching in every direction to the horizon and upward without apparent limit. A pilot flying across this expanse occupies a space — the space above the desert — and his position within it can be described, at least approximately, by coordinates. Objects exist within it: birds, clouds, the aeroplane itself. Yet this space is not bounded in any ordinary sense. The sky does not have borders; it extends continuously in every horizontal direction and fades upward into the atmosphere and beyond. There is no wall, no fence, no line of demarcation that marks where the space ends and something else begins. The first condition fails, yet the sky above the desert is a space — a region within which objects have positions and through which they can move.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the room, the playing field, the box — were all enclosed or demarcated regions with clear boundaries. The definition abstracted boundedness as essential to space. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of humanly constructed or humanly delimited enclosures, and the definition mistook a feature of those particular spaces for a necessary condition of space as such. The open sky reveals that a region may function as a space — containing objects, permitting movement, admitting of positional description — without being bounded by any border or limit.
passage:
Consider three regions that are paradigmatically spaces. The interior of a room is a space: it is bounded by walls, floor, and ceiling; it contains objects — a table, a chair, a lamp; and the position of any object within it can be specified by reference to the walls and corners. A playing field is a space: it is bounded by touchlines and goal-lines; players and the ball move within it; and positions can be described by coordinates measured from the sidelines. The interior of a box is a space: it is bounded on all six sides; objects can be placed within it; and positions can be given in three dimensions. From these cases one might extract a definition: a region is a space if and only if it is bounded, it is capable of containing material objects, and positions within it can be specified by numerical coordinates.

But one must consider the open sky above a flat desert. A pilot takes off from a small airstrip and flies westward across an expanse of sand that stretches to the horizon in every direction. Above him the sky extends upward — pale blue, deepening to indigo, fading at last into the blackness of the upper atmosphere. He occupies a space; no one would deny it. He moves through the space, changes his position within it, and his position can be described — by latitude, longitude, and altitude — with as much precision as the position of a chair in a room. Objects exist within this space: birds fly through it, clouds drift across it, the aeroplane itself traces a path through it. Yet this space is not bounded. There are no walls, no borders, no lines of demarcation. The desert extends to the horizon in every direction, and the sky extends upward without any surface or barrier to mark its limit. One cannot point to a boundary and say 'here the space ends.' The first condition of the definition fails. The background cases were all humanly constructed or humanly delimited enclosures — rooms, fields, boxes — with clear and definite boundaries, and the definition treated boundedness as essential to the concept of space. The open sky reveals that a region may be a space in the fullest sense — containing objects, permitting movement, admitting of positional description — without possessing any boundary at all. The definition confused a feature of enclosed spaces with a necessary feature of space as such.

background_cases: A marble statue stands in a museum for centuries; it is the same statue at the end as at the beginning because its material — the marble — has remained the same and its form has not changed.
A diamond is passed from grandmother to granddaughter; it persists as the same diamond because its material — the carbon lattice — is unchanged and its form — the cut — is preserved.

An ancient coin is discovered in an archaeological dig; it is identified as the same coin minted two thousand years ago because its material — the bronze — and its form — the impression of the emperor's head — have endured.
definition: An object O persists through time as the same object if and only if (i) the material of which O is composed remains the same, (ii) the form or structure of O remains the same, and (iii) there is a continuous causal chain connecting O at the earlier time with O at the later time.
conditions: the material of O remains the same
the form or structure of O remains the same
there is a continuous causal chain connecting O across time
counterexample: Consider an oak tree observed over the course of a century. A sapling planted in 1850 is, by every ordinary reckoning, the same oak that stands in the garden in 1950. The tree persists; it is one tree, not a succession of trees. There is a continuous causal chain connecting the sapling with the mature oak — the tree has grown continuously, without interruption, from the one state to the other. Yet neither its material nor its form has remained the same. The material of the sapling — its wood, its bark, its leaves — has been entirely replaced many times over; the tree has drawn in water and minerals from the soil, carbon from the air, and has shed its leaves each autumn. And its form has changed dramatically: the slender sapling bears no structural resemblance to the spreading, thick-trunked oak of a century later. The first two conditions fail, yet the tree persists as the same individual.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the statue, the diamond, the coin — were all inert objects whose identity through time rests upon the preservation of material and form. The definition abstracted material and formal continuity as essential to persistence. But the background cases were drawn from the narrow class of non-living, stable objects, and the definition mistook the conditions of their persistence for the conditions of persistence as such. The oak tree reveals that a living organism persists not by preserving its material and form but by maintaining a continuous process of growth and self-renewal in which both material and form are constantly transformed.
passage:
Consider three objects that persist through time. A marble statue stands in a museum for centuries. It is the same statue that the sculptor carved; visitors in the twentieth century behold the same object that visitors in the seventeenth century beheld. Its material — the marble — has remained the same, and its form — the arrangement of surfaces that constitute the figure — has not changed. A diamond is passed from a grandmother to her granddaughter. The diamond persists as the same stone across the generations; its material — the carbon lattice — is unchanged, and its form — the cut given to it by the jeweller — is preserved. An ancient coin is unearthed in an archaeological excavation and identified as the same coin that was struck in a Roman mint two thousand years ago; the bronze has endured, and the impression of the emperor's profile is still legible. From these cases one might extract a definition: an object persists through time as the same object if and only if its material remains the same, its form remains the same, and there is a continuous causal chain connecting the object at the earlier time with the object at the later time.

But one must consider an oak tree planted as a sapling in a garden in the middle of the nineteenth century. A century later, the tree still stands — an immense, spreading oak with a trunk three feet in diameter, its branches reaching outward and upward in a great canopy. It is the same tree that was planted as a sapling; the gardener's records confirm it, and the continuous history of the tree in the garden is not in doubt. There is a continuous causal chain connecting the sapling with the mature oak — the tree has grown without interruption, drawing sustenance from the soil and the air, adding ring upon ring of wood, year after year. But the material of the tree has not remained the same. The wood of the sapling is not the wood of the mature oak; the cells have been replaced many times over through the processes of growth and decay. The tree has drawn in water, minerals, and carbon dioxide, and has incorporated them into its structure, whilst shedding leaves, bark, and dead branches. And the form of the tree has changed beyond recognition. The slender, flexible sapling of 1850 bears no structural resemblance to the massive, spreading oak of 1950; the form has been transformed utterly by a century of growth. Both the first and the second conditions of the definition fail. The background cases — the statue, the diamond, the coin — were all inert objects, stable and unchanging, whose persistence rests upon the preservation of material and form. The oak tree reveals that living things persist not by preserving their material and form but by maintaining a continuous process of self-renewal, in which both material and form are ceaselessly transformed, and yet the organism remains, through all these transformations, the same individual.

background_cases: The proposition that all triangles have three sides is necessarily true: one cannot coherently deny it, and its truth is established by understanding the concept of a triangle.
The proposition that nothing is both entirely red and entirely blue at the same point at the same time is necessarily true: its denial is incoherent, and its truth follows from the nature of colour.

The proposition that if A is taller than B and B is taller than C then A is taller than C is necessarily true: its truth follows from the transitivity of the taller-than relation, and its denial is absurd.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) the denial of P leads to an absurdity or a contradiction, (ii) the truth of P is grasped through understanding the concepts it involves, not through observation, and (iii) P is a universal generalisation — that is, P has the form 'All Fs are Gs' or an equivalent.
conditions: the denial of P leads to absurdity or contradiction
the truth of P is grasped through conceptual understanding, not observation
P is a universal generalisation
counterexample: Consider the proposition that this particular object — this very desk at which I am now writing — could not have been made from a wholly different block of wood from the one from which it was in fact made. The proposition concerns a particular object, not a universal class; it does not have the form 'All Fs are Gs.' It asserts that this desk, the specific desk before me, has its material origin essentially: it could not have originated from entirely different matter and still been this desk. Many philosophers have found this proposition necessarily true — a desk made from a wholly different block of wood would be a different desk, however closely it resembled this one. Yet the proposition is not a universal generalisation; it is a singular claim about a particular thing. The third condition fails, yet the proposition appears to be necessarily true.
abductive_insight: The background cases — about triangles, colours, and the transitivity of relations — were all universal generalisations whose truth is grasped a priori. The definition abstracted the form of universal generalisation as essential to necessity. But the background cases were drawn from the domain of conceptual truths that happen to be universal in scope, and the definition mistook the logical form of those truths for a necessary feature of all necessary truths. The desk proposition reveals that necessity may attach to singular claims about particular objects — claims about what a specific thing could or could not have been — and that the definition confused the universality of its examples with a requirement of necessity itself.
passage:
Consider three propositions that are paradigmatically necessary. The proposition that all triangles have three sides is necessarily true; one cannot coherently conceive of a triangle that lacks a third side, and the truth of the proposition is grasped simply by understanding the concept of a triangle. The proposition that nothing is both entirely red and entirely blue at the same point at the same time is necessarily true; its denial is incoherent, and its truth is established by reflection upon the nature of colour. The proposition that if A is taller than B and B is taller than C then A is taller than C is necessarily true; the transitivity of the taller-than relation is grasped through conceptual understanding, and its denial is absurd. From these cases one might extract a definition: a proposition is necessarily true if and only if its denial leads to absurdity, its truth is grasped through conceptual understanding rather than through observation, and it is a universal generalisation.

But one must consider a proposition of a very different character. Consider this particular desk at which I am now writing — a desk made from a specific block of English oak, cut from a specific tree, by a specific carpenter. The proposition is this: this desk could not have been made from a wholly different block of wood. If the carpenter had taken a block of pine — or even a different block of oak, cut from a different tree — and fashioned a desk of exactly the same dimensions and design, the resulting desk would not have been this desk. It would have been a different desk, however indistinguishable in appearance. The origin of this desk in this particular block of wood is, many philosophers have argued, essential to it; the desk could not have had a wholly different material origin and still been this very desk. This proposition appears to be necessarily true. Yet it is not a universal generalisation. It does not say that all desks must be made from the wood from which they were in fact made; it says that this desk — this particular, individual object before me — could not have originated from entirely different matter. It is a singular proposition about a particular thing. The third condition of the definition fails. The background cases — triangles, colours, taller-than — were all universal in form, and the definition treated universality as essential to necessity. The desk reveals that necessary truths may be singular and particular, attaching to specific objects and their specific origins, and that the definition confused the universality of its examples with a condition of necessity as such.

background_cases: A bronze statue persists through minor surface changes — scratches, patina, slight weathering — while remaining the same statue, because the underlying matter and form are preserved.
A river persists through the continuous replacement of its water, because the channel, the banks, and the pattern of flow remain constant even as the particular water molecules change.
A ship gradually has its planks replaced one by one over a period of years; so long as the replacement is gradual and the overall structure is maintained, we regard it as the same ship.
A human body replaces nearly all of its cells over the course of several years, yet the person persists as the same individual because the biological organisation continues without interruption.
definition: An entity X is the same substance at time t2 as it was at time t1 if and only if (i) X maintains a continuous spatiotemporal path from t1 to t2, (ii) X retains the same overall structure or form throughout the interval, and (iii) any change in X's material constituents occurs gradually rather than all at once.
conditions: X maintains a continuous spatiotemporal path
X retains the same overall structure or form
Any change in material constituents occurs gradually
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis. During the pupal stage, the caterpillar's internal structure is very nearly dissolved — the body is broken down into a largely undifferentiated organic mass before being reorganised into the entirely different structure of a butterfly. The spatiotemporal path is continuous; the change in material constituents is, in one sense, gradual — it occurs over days rather than instantaneously. Yet the overall structure is not retained: the caterpillar's form is destroyed and replaced by the butterfly's form. The second condition is violated, yet biologists and ordinary speakers alike regard the butterfly as the same individual organism as the caterpillar.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved entities whose persistence was secured by the continuity of an organising principle — a pattern of flow, a biological programme, a structural plan — that governed the arrangement of matter even as the matter itself changed. The definition captured structural continuity but failed to recognise that the organising principle may itself undergo radical transformation while preserving the identity of the individual, as when a single biological programme directs both the caterpillar and the butterfly stages.
passage:
When one considers the cases in which an entity persists through material change, a common pattern emerges. The bronze statue endures through surface wear because its form and matter are preserved. The river persists through the replacement of its water because the channel and the pattern of flow remain. The ship whose planks are gradually replaced remains the same ship because the overall structure is maintained. The human body, replacing its cells over years, persists because biological organisation continues without interruption. From these cases, one might extract the following definition: an entity is the same substance at a later time as at an earlier time if and only if it maintains a continuous spatiotemporal path, retains the same overall structure, and undergoes only gradual changes in its material constituents.

Yet this definition, though it accommodates the background cases well, proves insufficient when confronted with a case that the background cases did not anticipate. Consider the metamorphosis of a caterpillar into a butterfly. The caterpillar enters a chrysalis, and during the pupal stage its internal structure is very nearly dissolved — the body is broken down into an undifferentiated organic mass before being reorganised into the radically different form of a butterfly. The spatiotemporal path is continuous, and the change in constituents is, in a temporal sense, gradual. But the overall structure is not retained: the caterpillar's form is destroyed, and what emerges is an organism with a wholly different shape, different organs, and a different mode of life. The second condition is plainly violated. Yet the butterfly is the same individual organism as the caterpillar — the same biological programme, encoded in the same DNA, has directed both stages. The background cases were all cases in which the organising principle remained structurally stable while matter changed beneath it. What they concealed was the possibility that the organising principle itself might undergo radical transformation — that the very programme governing the entity's structure might prescribe the destruction of one form and the construction of another — while the identity of the individual is preserved by the continuity of that programme. The definition captured structural constancy but missed the deeper fact that identity may be carried by a principle that encompasses multiple, radically different structural phases.

background_cases: Striking a match causes it to light: the striking precedes the lighting, the striking produces the lighting through a physical mechanism (friction generating heat), and absent the striking, the match would not have lit.

A stone thrown at a window causes the window to break: the throw precedes the breaking, the stone's impact produces the fracture through the transfer of kinetic energy, and absent the throw, the window would not have broken.
Ingesting a poison causes illness: the ingestion precedes the illness, a chemical mechanism links the two, and absent the ingestion, the illness would not have occurred.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) there is a physical mechanism connecting C to E, and (iii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred — that is, C is a necessary condition of E in the circumstances.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
A physical mechanism connects C to E
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Consider a case of causal pre-emption. Two assassins, acting independently, each fire a bullet at a victim at the same moment. Assassin A's bullet arrives first and kills the victim; Assassin B's bullet, which would have killed the victim had A's missed, arrives a fraction of a second later and passes through the already-dead body. A's firing precedes the death; a physical mechanism connects A's bullet to the death; but the third condition — that had A not fired, the death would not have occurred — is false, because B's bullet would have killed the victim anyway. All conditions except the third are met for A's shot, yet A's shot is plainly the cause of death. The counterfactual condition is not satisfied in the circumstances, despite the clear causal relation.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all cases of solitary causation — cases in which only one causal process was at work and no alternative cause was waiting in the wings. The definition's counterfactual condition works well for solitary causes but collapses when multiple sufficient causes are present, because the removal of one cause does not remove the effect. What the background cases shared, but the definition failed to capture, was not the necessity of the cause but its actual productive role in bringing about the effect.
passage:
When one examines the paradigmatic cases of causation — the match struck and lit, the stone thrown and window broken, the poison ingested and illness produced — a pattern appears. In each, the cause precedes the effect, a physical mechanism links the two, and the effect would not have occurred had the cause been absent. One might distil from these cases the following definition: an event C causes an event E if and only if C precedes E, a physical mechanism connects them, and had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.

Yet this definition, natural though it is, encounters a difficulty when one moves beyond cases of solitary causation to cases where multiple sufficient causes converge upon the same effect. Consider two assassins, acting independently, who each fire a bullet at a victim. Assassin A's bullet arrives first and kills the victim. Assassin B's bullet, which would have been lethal, arrives a fraction of a second later and passes harmlessly through the body. A's shot precedes the death; a clear physical mechanism — the bullet's impact — connects it to the death. But the counterfactual condition fails: had A not fired, B's bullet would have killed the victim anyway. The death would have occurred regardless. The three conditions thus fail to identify A's shot as the cause, even though it plainly is. The background cases were cases in which only one causal process was operative, so the counterfactual condition tracked causation perfectly. But once a second sufficient cause is standing by, the counterfactual test breaks down, because the removal of the actual cause does not remove the effect. What the background cases shared was not the mere necessity of the cause but its actual productive role — the fact that the cause was the process that actually brought the effect about. The definition captured necessity but missed the notion of actual causal production, which is the feature that distinguishes the cause that fires from the cause that is merely waiting.

background_cases: A leaf changes from green to brown in autumn: at one time it possesses the intrinsic property of greenness, at a later time it possesses the intrinsic property of brownness, and it remains the same leaf throughout.
A bar of iron changes from cold to hot when placed in a furnace: it possesses one temperature at one time and a different temperature at a later time, remaining the same bar.
A rubber band changes from being slack to being taut when stretched: it possesses one shape at one time and a different shape at a later time, remaining the same band.
definition: An entity X undergoes genuine change if and only if (i) X possesses a property F at time t1, (ii) X does not possess F at a later time t2, and (iii) X is numerically the same entity at t1 and t2.
conditions: X possesses a property F at t1
X does not possess F at t2
X is the same entity at t1 and t2
counterexample: Consider Socrates, who at time t1 is taller than Theaetetus and at time t2 is no longer taller than Theaetetus — not because Socrates has shrunk, but because Theaetetus has grown. Socrates possesses the property of being-taller-than-Theaetetus at t1 and does not possess it at t2. He is the same individual. All three conditions are met. Yet Socrates has undergone no genuine change — he has done nothing, gained nothing, lost nothing. It is Theaetetus who has changed. The definition counts as a change in Socrates what is merely a change in his relational surroundings.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved changes in intrinsic properties — colour, temperature, shape — properties that belong to the entity in virtue of what it is in itself, not in virtue of how it stands in relation to other things. The definition generalised from these cases to all properties, but the generalisation was too broad: it included relational properties whose alteration need not reflect any genuine change in the entity to which they are ascribed.
passage:
The paradigm cases of change are straightforward: a leaf turns brown, an iron bar grows hot, a rubber band is stretched taut. In each, an entity possesses one intrinsic property at an earlier time and a different one at a later time while remaining numerically the same thing. From these cases, one might extract the definition: an entity undergoes genuine change if and only if it possesses a property at one time that it does not possess at a later time, and it is the same entity throughout.

Yet this definition, generalised from cases of intrinsic change, extends to cases that we should hesitate to call genuine changes at all. Consider Socrates at a time when he is taller than Theaetetus. If Theaetetus subsequently grows while Socrates remains physically unchanged, Socrates loses the property of being taller than Theaetetus. He possesses the property at the earlier time; he does not possess it at the later time; he is the same individual. The definition therefore pronounces that Socrates has undergone change. But Socrates has done nothing — he has not grown, shrunk, moved, or altered in any intrinsic respect. It is Theaetetus who has changed, and Socrates has merely stood still while the world shifted around him. The background cases all involved intrinsic properties — colour, temperature, shape — properties that belong to an entity in virtue of how it is in itself. The definition, by failing to restrict attention to intrinsic properties, admits relational properties whose alteration tells us nothing about the entity in question but only about its environment. What is needed is a condition distinguishing intrinsic from relational change, so that the definition captures only those alterations that reflect a genuine modification in the entity itself.

background_cases: The proposition that no bachelor is married is necessarily true: its truth is guaranteed by the meanings of its terms, and its negation is self-contradictory.
The proposition that all triangles have three sides is necessarily true: anyone who denies it reveals a failure to understand the concept of a triangle.
The proposition that if A is larger than B and B is larger than C then A is larger than C is necessarily true: the relation of being larger is transitive by definition.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the negation of P entails a logical contradiction, and (iii) P is knowable independently of experience — that is, P is a priori.
conditions: P is true
The negation of P entails a logical contradiction
P is knowable independently of experience
counterexample: Consider the proposition that gold has the atomic number 79. Many philosophers regard this as a necessarily true proposition — in every possible situation, anything that is gold has atomic number 79, and anything with atomic number 79 is gold. The proposition is true. Yet its negation does not entail a logical contradiction: one can coherently conceive of a substance with all the superficial properties of gold — its colour, malleability, lustre — that has a different atomic number. And the proposition is not knowable a priori — it required extensive empirical investigation to determine the atomic number of gold. The second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is, on the relevant philosophical view, necessarily true.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all cases of analytic necessity — cases in which the truth of the proposition was secured by the meanings of its constituent terms and was discoverable by conceptual analysis alone. The definition captured this species of necessity perfectly but could not accommodate the possibility that some truths are necessary in virtue of the essential nature of the things they concern, discoverable only through empirical inquiry, and such that their negation is conceivable even though it is metaphysically impossible.
passage:
When one considers the paradigm cases of necessary truth — that no bachelor is married, that triangles have three sides, that the larger-than relation is transitive — a common pattern emerges. Each is true, the negation of each is self-contradictory, and each is knowable independently of experience. From these cases one might extract the definition: a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true, its negation entails a logical contradiction, and it is knowable a priori.

Yet this definition, drawn from cases of analytic necessity, proves too narrow when one turns to propositions whose necessity has a different source. Consider the proposition that gold has the atomic number 79. Many philosophers hold that this proposition is necessarily true — that in every possible situation, gold has atomic number 79, and that anything lacking this number is not gold, however much it may resemble gold in its superficial properties. The proposition is true. But its negation does not entail a logical contradiction: one can coherently describe a world in which a golden-coloured, malleable, lustrous metal turns out to have a different atomic structure. And the proposition is plainly not knowable a priori — it took centuries of chemical and physical investigation to discover the atomic number of gold. The definition's second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is, on the relevant view, necessarily true. The background cases were uniformly cases of analytic necessity, where meaning alone secured truth. They concealed the possibility that necessity might arise from the essential nature of substances themselves — from what gold is, at the deepest level — rather than from the meanings of our words. The definition captured one species of necessity but excluded another, equally important, species.

background_cases: A marble statue is a substance: it exists independently, persists through minor weathering and chipping, and bears properties such as colour, weight, and shape. Its substantiality is grounded in the fact that a single, continuous portion of marble underlies all its changes.

A living oak tree is a substance: it exists independently, persists through the cycle of seasons — losing leaves, growing new ones, adding rings — and bears properties such as height and girth. Its substantiality is grounded in the continuous biological organisation that maintains its identity through the replacement of material.

A lump of gold is a substance: it exists independently, persists through being melted and recast, and bears properties such as mass, lustre, and malleability. Its substantiality is grounded in the persistence of the same quantity of gold through changes in shape.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular, (ii) X persists through change, and (iii) X is the subject of properties without itself being a property of anything else.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular
X persists through change
X is the subject of properties and not itself a property of anything
counterexample: Consider a whirlpool in a river. The whirlpool exists independently of any other particular whirlpool; it persists through change — it may grow larger or smaller, spin faster or slower, yet remain recognisably the same whirlpool for hours or even days; and it is the subject of properties — we say it is three feet across, that it rotates clockwise, that it is dangerous to swimmers. All three conditions are met. Yet a whirlpool is not a substance. It is a pattern of motion in flowing water; the water that composes it at one moment is entirely different from the water that composes it a minute later. It has no material of its own — it is constituted, from moment to moment, by whatever water happens to pass through its region.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the statue, the tree, the lump of gold — all involve entities whose persistence is grounded in a continuing material or organisational substrate: the marble endures, the biological organisation is maintained, the gold persists through recasting. The definition captures independence, persistence, and property-bearing, but it omits the requirement that persistence be grounded in the continuity of some underlying material or organisational principle, rather than in the mere continuity of a spatial pattern through which different material flows.
passage:
When one considers the paradigmatic substances — a marble statue, a living tree, a lump of gold — a common feature presents itself. Each exists independently; each persists through various changes; each is the subject of properties. But a further feature, less often remarked upon, is shared by all three: in each case, the entity's persistence is grounded in the continuity of some underlying substrate. The statue persists because the same marble endures; the tree persists because a continuous biological organisation maintains its identity even as individual cells are replaced; the lump of gold persists because the same quantity of gold survives being melted and recast. In each case, there is something — a material, an organisation, a quantity — that underlies the entity's changes and grounds its continued identity.

One might attempt to capture the substance-hood of these cases with the definition: an entity is a substance if and only if it exists independently, persists through change, and is the subject of properties without being a property of anything else. Yet this definition, though it accommodates the background cases, proves too permissive. Consider a whirlpool in a river. The whirlpool exists independently of other whirlpools; it persists through changes in speed and size; and it is the subject of properties — diameter, rotational velocity, depth. All three conditions are satisfied. But a whirlpool is not a substance. It is a stable pattern of motion through which water continuously flows; the water composing it at one moment is entirely replaced the next. It has no material of its own and no organisational principle that maintains a fixed set of constituents. The background cases all possessed what the whirlpool lacks: a continuing substrate — whether material or organisational — that grounds the entity's persistence. The definition has captured the outward marks of substantiality — independence, persistence, property-bearing — but has dropped the grounding condition that distinguishes a genuine substance from a mere persistent pattern.

background_cases: Striking a match causes it to light: the friction generates heat, which raises the temperature of the match head above its ignition point, producing flame. The cause operates by transmitting energy to the effect.

A billiard ball striking a second ball causes the second to move: the kinetic energy of the first ball is transferred to the second through the collision. The cause operates by the physical transmission of momentum.

Heating water causes it to boil: the thermal energy agitates the water molecules until they overcome the intermolecular forces binding them in the liquid state. The cause operates by the transfer of energy across a physical medium.

Dropping a glass causes it to shatter: the gravitational acceleration imparts kinetic energy to the glass, which is transferred to the floor upon impact, and the resulting stresses exceed the glass's structural tolerance. The cause operates through the physical transmission of force.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C is temporally prior to E, (ii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and (iii) events of the same general type as C are invariably followed by events of the same general type as E under similar circumstances.
conditions: C is temporally prior to E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous
Events of type C are invariably followed by events of type E under similar circumstances
counterexample: Consider night invariably following day. Day is temporally prior to night. Day and night are spatiotemporally contiguous — there is no gap between them. And the regularity is exceptionless: under similar circumstances (the continued rotation of the Earth), day is invariably followed by night. All three conditions are met. Yet day does not cause night. Both are effects of the same underlying process — the rotation of the Earth relative to the sun. The succession of day and night is a regularity produced by a common cause, not a causal sequence in which the earlier member produces the later.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the match, the billiard ball, the boiling water, the shattered glass — all involve a physical process by which energy or force is transmitted from the cause to the effect. The definition captures the temporal order and the regularity of these cases but drops the requirement that the cause physically produce the effect through the transmission of energy, force, or some other conserved quantity. Without this requirement, any invariable succession — including successions produced by a common cause — qualifies as causal.
passage:
The paradigmatic causal sequences — striking a match, the collision of billiard balls, heating water to its boiling point, dropping a glass — share a common structure. In each, the cause is temporally prior to the effect; cause and effect are spatiotemporally contiguous; and the conjunction of cause and effect is regular and exceptionless under similar circumstances. But they share a further feature, one that is easy to overlook precisely because it is so ubiquitous: in each case, the cause operates on the effect by transmitting something physical — energy, momentum, force — across the spatiotemporal interval that separates them. The match lights because friction generates heat; the billiard ball moves because momentum is transferred through the collision; the water boils because thermal energy agitates its molecules; the glass shatters because the force of impact exceeds its structural tolerance.

One might propose the definition: an event C causes an event E if and only if C is temporally prior to E, C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous, and events of the same type are invariably conjoined under similar circumstances. This definition captures the surface features of the background cases admirably. Yet it proves too permissive. Consider the succession of day and night. Day is temporally prior to night; the two are contiguous; and the regularity is exceptionless — day is invariably followed by night, so long as the Earth continues to rotate. All three conditions are met. But day does not cause night. Both are effects of the Earth's rotation relative to the sun; neither produces the other. The background cases all involved a physical process — the transmission of energy or force — by which the cause brought about the effect. The definition has captured the temporal and regularistic features of causation but has dropped the productive or transmissive element that distinguishes genuine causation from the mere regular succession of events with a common origin.

background_cases: It is necessarily true that no bachelor is married: the predicate 'bachelor' means 'unmarried man,' and so the proposition that no bachelor is married is guaranteed by the meanings of the terms alone. The necessity is grounded in the definitional relations among the concepts involved.

It is necessarily true that two plus three equals five: the proposition is guaranteed by the axioms and rules of arithmetic, and no coherent assignment of meaning to the terms could render it false. The necessity is grounded in the logical structure of mathematics.

It is necessarily true that nothing is both entirely red and entirely green at the same time: the concepts of redness and greenness are mutually exclusive with respect to the same surface at the same moment. The necessity is grounded in the structure of colour concepts.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) P could not have been false — there is no possible situation in which P fails to hold, and (iii) the truth of P can be recognised by reflection on the meanings of the terms involved, without recourse to empirical investigation.
conditions: P is true
P could not have been false
P's truth is recognisable by reflection on the meanings of terms alone
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is H2O. This proposition is true. And there is a strong case that it could not have been false — that in any possible situation in which the substance we call water exists, it is composed of hydrogen and oxygen in the ratio two to one. The molecular composition of water is not an accidental feature but is constitutive of the substance itself; something with a different molecular composition would not be water but a different substance. The first two conditions are met. Yet the truth of the proposition cannot be recognised by reflection on the meanings of the terms alone. It took centuries of empirical chemistry to discover that water is H2O; no amount of conceptual analysis could have revealed it. The third condition fails.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the bachelor proposition, the arithmetic truth, the colour exclusion — are all cases in which the necessity is analytic: it arises from the logical or definitional relations among concepts. The definition generalises from these cases to require that all necessary truths be recognisable through conceptual reflection. But the case of water and H2O reveals a different species of necessity — one grounded not in conceptual relations but in the essential nature of a substance, discoverable only through empirical inquiry. The definition captures analytic necessity but excludes metaphysical necessity.
passage:
The propositions most readily recognised as necessarily true — that no bachelor is married, that two plus three equals five, that nothing is both entirely red and entirely green at the same moment — share a common feature. In each case, the necessity is, as it were, visible to the intellect: one can recognise the truth of the proposition simply by reflecting on the meanings of the terms and the logical relations among them. No empirical investigation is required to establish that bachelors are unmarried, that arithmetic truths hold, or that the colour concepts exclude one another. One might accordingly propose: a proposition is necessarily true if and only if it is true, could not have been false, and its truth is recognisable by reflection on meanings alone.

Yet this definition, abstracted from analytic paradigms, proves too narrow. Consider the proposition that water is H2O. The proposition is true, and there is strong reason to hold that it could not have been false — that the molecular composition of water is not accidental but constitutive, so that any substance with a different composition would not be water but something else. The first two conditions are met. But the third is not: the truth of the proposition was discovered only after centuries of empirical investigation. No amount of reflection on the meaning of 'water' could have revealed its molecular composition; the ancient Greeks, who understood the word 'water' perfectly well, had no inkling that water was H2O. The background cases were all instances of analytic necessity — necessity grounded in the relations among concepts. The definition has generalised from these cases and imposed the requirement that all necessary truths be analytically recognisable. But the case of water reveals a different and perhaps deeper form of necessity — one grounded in the essential nature of substances, discoverable not by conceptual reflection but only through the patient empirical investigation of the world.

background_cases: A ship that has one plank replaced remains the same ship: the replacement is minor, the overall structure is preserved, and the ship continues to function as before. The identity persists because the change is small relative to the whole.

A human body that replaces its cells over a period of years remains the same body: the replacement is gradual, the overall organisation is preserved, and the body continues to function. The identity persists because the change is incremental and governed by the body's own biological processes.

A river that changes its water continuously remains the same river: the water flows through, but the banks, the course, and the overall hydrological pattern persist. The identity is maintained by the continuity of the geographical and dynamic structure.
definition: An entity X at time t1 is the same entity as Y at time t2 if and only if (i) Y has developed from X through a continuous series of small changes, (ii) Y retains the overall structure or organisation of X, and (iii) there is no rival candidate — no other entity Z that has an equal or better claim to be identical with X.
conditions: Y has developed from X through a continuous series of small changes
Y retains the overall structure of X
There is no rival candidate with an equal or better claim to identity with X
counterexample: Consider a ship — call it the Ship of Theseus — whose planks are replaced one at a time over many years until every original plank has been replaced. Suppose further that a collector has preserved every removed plank and has reassembled them, in their original arrangement, into a second ship. Call the continuously repaired ship A and the reassembled ship B. Ship A has developed from the original through a continuous series of small changes and retains its overall structure — conditions (i) and (ii) are met. But ship B is composed of the very same planks as the original, in the very same arrangement. It too has a strong claim to identity with the original. Condition (iii) fails, for there is now a rival candidate. The definition cannot adjudicate between the two; it offers no principle for deciding which ship is the Ship of Theseus when both have legitimate claims.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the ship with one replaced plank, the human body, the river — all involved situations in which no rival candidate existed. The gradual replacement produced a single continuous entity, and no second entity was assembled from the discarded material. The definition's third condition — the no-rival requirement — functioned as a default rather than as a principled criterion: it worked only because the background cases were uncontested. When a rival candidate is introduced, the definition reveals that it has no substantive principle for resolving identity disputes — it merely rules out competition without explaining why one claimant should prevail over another.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which identity through time seems least controversial — a ship with a single replaced plank, a human body gradually renewing its cells, a river whose water changes continuously — a common pattern emerges. In each case, the entity at the later time has developed from the earlier entity through a continuous series of small changes; the overall structure or organisation has been preserved; and there is no rival candidate — no second entity that could claim to be the same thing. From these cases one might extract the definition: X at t1 is the same entity as Y at t2 if and only if Y has developed from X through continuous small changes, Y retains X's overall structure, and no rival candidate exists.

Yet this definition, adequate for the uncontested cases, collapses when a rival candidate appears. Consider the ancient puzzle of the Ship of Theseus. A ship's planks are replaced one at a time over many years until every original plank has been removed and replaced. Meanwhile, a collector preserves every removed plank and reassembles them into a second ship, in the original arrangement. Call the continuously repaired ship A and the reassembled ship B. Ship A satisfies conditions (i) and (ii): it has developed from the original through continuous small changes and retains the original's overall structure. But ship B, composed of the very same planks in the very same arrangement, also has a powerful claim to be the original. Condition (iii) now fails, for a rival candidate exists. The definition offers no way to choose between them. The background cases all lacked competitors — there was only one continuous descendant — and the no-rival condition worked as a silent default. But it was never a principled criterion; it merely excluded competition without providing a substantive ground for preferring one claimant over another when competition arises.

background_cases: A lump of gold is a paradigmatic substance: it exists independently of any other particular, it persists through changes in temperature and shape, and it is not constitutively dependent upon any medium or substrate — one can remove it from its surroundings entirely and it continues to exist undiminished.

A living oak tree is a substance: it exists independently of other particulars, persists through the loss of leaves and the growth of new branches, and is not a mere modification of the soil in which it grows — uprooted and transplanted, it remains the same tree.

A marble statue is a substance: it exists independently, persists through changes in surface colour and minor chipping, and is not a pattern in a medium but a self-subsistent particular that can be moved from place to place without ceasing to be.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently of any other particular entity, (ii) X persists through changes in at least some of its properties, and (iii) X is the bearer of properties rather than itself a property of something else.
conditions: X exists independently of any other particular entity
X persists through changes in at least some of its properties
X is the bearer of properties rather than itself a property of something else
counterexample: Consider a knot tied in a length of rope. The knot exists independently of any other particular knot. It persists through changes — one can loosen or tighten it, move the rope, change the rope's colour — and it bears properties: it has a position, a degree of tightness, a type (bowline, reef, clove hitch). All three conditions are met. Yet a knot is not a substance; it is a configuration of the rope. Untie the rope and the knot ceases to exist, not because it has been destroyed in the way a substance is destroyed, but because the pattern has been dissolved. The knot is constitutively dependent upon the arrangement of the rope in a way that the gold, the oak, and the statue are not dependent upon any arrangement of a medium.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve entities that are not constitutively dependent upon the arrangement of any medium or substrate. The gold does not depend upon being in any particular configuration of something else; the tree does not depend upon a pattern in the soil; the statue is not a ripple in the marble. The definition captures independence, persistence, and property-bearing, but it omits the requirement that the entity not be a mere configuration or topological feature of an underlying medium — a condition that the background cases silently satisfy.
passage:
When one considers the entities most confidently designated as substances, a pattern emerges that repays careful attention. A lump of gold exists independently, persists through heating and cooling, and bears its properties — malleability, lustre, density — without being a property of anything else. An oak tree exists independently of its surroundings, persists through seasonal changes, and is not a modification of the soil. A marble statue exists independently, survives minor damage, and is not a pattern within some larger medium. In each case, the entity is self-subsistent in a sense that goes beyond the three conditions of independence, persistence, and property-bearing: none of these substances is constitutively dependent upon the arrangement of an underlying medium.

One might attempt to codify these cases by defining a substance as an entity that exists independently, persists through changes, and bears properties. This definition captures the formal structure of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is furnished by a knot tied in a length of rope. The knot exists independently of any other particular knot. It persists through changes — one can adjust its tightness, shift its position along the rope, even dye the rope a different colour, and the knot remains. It bears properties: a type (bowline, reef, figure-of-eight), a location, a degree of tautness. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet a knot is not a substance. It is a configuration of the rope — a topological feature that depends constitutively upon the arrangement of the rope's fibres. Untie the rope, and the knot vanishes, not through destruction in the manner of a substance but through the dissolution of a pattern. The background cases all lacked this constitutive dependence upon the arrangement of a medium: gold does not depend upon being configured within anything; an oak is not a pattern in the earth; a statue is not a ripple in stone. What the definition omits, and what the background cases tacitly satisfy, is the requirement that the entity not be a mere configuration or topological feature of an underlying substrate.

background_cases: The striking of a match causes its lighting: the striking is a specific intervention that makes a difference to whether the match lights; had the match not been struck, it would not have lit. The cause is a difference-maker with respect to the effect.

The ingestion of a poison causes the death of the organism: the poison is a specific agent whose introduction makes a difference to whether the organism dies; had the poison not been ingested, the organism would have survived.

The opening of a dam's sluice gate causes the flooding of the valley below: the opening is a specific action that makes a difference to whether the valley floods; had the gate remained shut, the valley would have been dry.
definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) there is a regular conjunction between events of type C and events of type E, and (iii) C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous or connected by a chain of spatiotemporally contiguous events.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
There is a regular conjunction between events of type C and events of type E
C and E are spatiotemporally contiguous or connected by a contiguous chain
counterexample: Consider the case of a barometer whose mercury drops before a storm. The drop of the mercury temporally precedes the storm. There is a regular conjunction between mercury drops and storms — whenever the barometer falls, a storm follows. And the events are connected by a chain of spatiotemporally contiguous atmospheric processes. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the falling of the mercury does not cause the storm. Both the mercury's fall and the storm are effects of the same cause — a drop in atmospheric pressure. The barometer reading is a symptom, not a cause.
abductive_insight: In the background cases — the match, the poison, the sluice gate — the putative cause is a difference-maker: removing it would have removed the effect. The match would not have lit without the striking; the organism would not have died without the poison; the valley would not have flooded without the opening of the gate. The definition captures temporal ordering, regularity, and contiguity but omits the difference-making condition. The barometer case exposes this omission: removing the mercury drop (by shielding the barometer) would not prevent the storm, because the mercury drop is not a difference-maker but a co-effect.
passage:
The causal relations most securely recognised in ordinary experience and in science share a feature that merits close examination. When a match is struck and lights, the striking is a difference-maker: had the match not been struck, it would not have lit. When a poison is ingested and the organism dies, the poison is the difference-maker: had it not been ingested, the organism would have survived. When a sluice gate is opened and a valley floods, the opening is the difference-maker: had the gate remained closed, the valley would have been dry. In each case, the cause is not merely a precursor or correlate but an event whose occurrence makes the difference between the effect's obtaining and its not obtaining.

One might codify these observations by defining a cause as a temporally prior event regularly conjoined with the effect and connected to it by a spatiotemporally contiguous chain. This definition captures three important features of the paradigm cases: temporal ordering, regularity, and physical connectedness. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is readily found. When a barometer's mercury drops before a storm, the drop precedes the storm, the conjunction is regular — mercury falls are reliably followed by storms — and the events are connected by a chain of atmospheric processes. All three conditions are satisfied. But the barometer's falling does not cause the storm. Both are effects of a common cause: a drop in atmospheric pressure. If one were to shield the barometer from the pressure change, the mercury would not fall, but the storm would arrive nonetheless. The definition captures correlation and contiguity but not the difference-making relation that the background cases all exhibit. What is needed is a condition requiring that the putative cause make a difference to the occurrence of the effect — that its removal would have prevented the effect — a condition the barometer case conspicuously fails to meet.

background_cases: A ship that has one plank replaced remains the same ship, because the replacement is gradual and the functional and structural continuity of the vessel is preserved — it continues to sail, to bear the same name, and to serve the same crew.

A human body that replaces its cells over the course of years remains the same body, because at no point is there a discontinuity in the organism's functioning, and the replacement is so gradual as to preserve the continuity of biological processes.

A river that shifts its course over centuries remains the same river, because the flow of water is continuous and the geographical identity — name, source, general region — is preserved despite the alteration of its banks.
definition: An object X at time t1 is the same object as Y at time t2 if and only if (i) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path from X at t1 to Y at t2, (ii) X and Y belong to the same kind, and (iii) the changes between X's state at t1 and Y's state at t2 are gradual rather than abrupt.
conditions: There is a continuous spatiotemporal path from X to Y
X and Y belong to the same kind
Changes are gradual rather than abrupt
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly. The spatiotemporal path is continuous — the organism occupies space continuously throughout the transformation. It arguably belongs to the same biological kind (it is the same individual organism). And yet the changes are not gradual in any ordinary sense: the caterpillar's body is almost entirely dissolved within the chrysalis and reconstructed into a radically different form. The passage from caterpillar to butterfly is as dramatic a transformation as any in nature. The third condition is not met, yet biologists regard the caterpillar and the butterfly as the same individual organism.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the ship, the body, the river — all involve changes that are piecemeal and incremental. The definition abstracts from these cases the requirement of gradualness. But the caterpillar case shows that an organism can persist through radical, non-gradual transformation provided biological continuity of a deeper sort is maintained. The definition mistakes the gradualness that happens to characterise the background cases for a necessary condition of persistence, when what actually matters is the continuity of an underlying organising process or principle.
passage:
The cases in which we are most confident that an object has persisted through change share a recognisable profile. When a ship has a single plank replaced, no one doubts that it remains the same ship; the change is small relative to the whole, and the vessel's structure and function are preserved. When the cells of a human body are replaced over years, we do not say a new person has come into existence; the replacement is gradual and the biological continuity unbroken. When a river slowly shifts its course, it remains the same river; the flow continues and the name persists. In each case, the changes are incremental — no single alteration is so dramatic as to raise a question about identity.

One might distil from these cases the following definition: an object persists from one time to another if there is a continuous spatiotemporal path, the object belongs to the same kind throughout, and the changes are gradual rather than abrupt. This captures the structure of the paradigm cases faithfully. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is furnished by the metamorphosis of a caterpillar into a butterfly. The spatiotemporal path is continuous — the organism occupies a definite location at every moment, first as caterpillar, then within the chrysalis, then as butterfly. The organism belongs to the same biological kind throughout — it is the same individual of the same species. But the changes are not gradual. Within the chrysalis, the caterpillar's tissues are almost entirely broken down and reorganised into a creature of utterly different form, possessing wings, compound eyes, and a proboscis where once there were legs, simple eyes, and mandibles. The transformation is as radical as any in the natural world. Yet no biologist hesitates to say that the butterfly is the same organism as the caterpillar. What the background cases exhibited — and what the definition mistakenly elevated to a necessary condition — was gradualness of change. The caterpillar case shows that persistence may survive even dramatic discontinuity, provided there is continuity of an underlying biological process that organises the transformation.

background_cases: The proposition that there are eight planets in the solar system is contingently true: it happens to be the case, but the solar system might have formed with nine or seven planets. One can coherently conceive of a solar system with a different number of planets without encountering any logical or conceptual difficulty.

The proposition that water boils at 100 degrees Celsius at standard pressure is contingently true: it depends upon the physical laws and conditions that happen to obtain. One can conceive of different physical constants under which water would boil at a different temperature.

The proposition that Julius Caesar crossed the Rubicon in 49 B.C. is contingently true: the event occurred, but Caesar might have chosen otherwise, and the history of Rome would have unfolded differently.
definition: A proposition P is contingent if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the negation of P involves no logical contradiction, and (iii) P can be conceived to be false without incoherence.
conditions: P is true
The negation of P involves no logical contradiction
P can be conceived to be false without incoherence
counterexample: Consider the proposition that Hesperus is Phosphorus — that the evening star is the morning star. The proposition is true: both names refer to the planet Venus. Its negation — that Hesperus is not Phosphorus — does not appear to involve a logical contradiction; one can utter the words 'Hesperus is not Phosphorus' without generating a formal inconsistency of the form 'P and not-P.' And one can apparently conceive the proposition to be false without incoherence — ancient astronomers did precisely that, believing the morning star and the evening star to be distinct bodies. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet many philosophers hold that the identity of Hesperus with Phosphorus is necessary, not contingent: if the two names designate the same object, then they necessarily designate the same object, and the proposition cannot be false.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the number of planets, the boiling point of water, Caesar's crossing — are all propositions whose truth depends upon how things happen to be arranged, not upon the essential natures of the objects involved. The definition captures the formal and conceivability marks of contingency but fails to distinguish genuine contingency from the appearance of contingency that arises when a necessary truth is couched in language that obscures its necessity. The Hesperus-Phosphorus case shows that conceivability of falsity may reflect ignorance of an underlying identity rather than genuine metaphysical contingency.
passage:
The propositions most readily recognised as contingent share a common character. That the solar system contains eight planets is contingently true: the solar system might have formed differently. That water boils at a certain temperature is contingently true: different physical constants would yield a different boiling point. That Caesar crossed the Rubicon is contingently true: he might have chosen otherwise. In each case, the truth of the proposition depends upon how things happen to be arranged — upon the contingent configuration of matter, law, or human decision — rather than upon the essential natures of the objects involved.

One might attempt to codify this by defining a contingent proposition as one that is true, whose negation involves no logical contradiction, and which can be conceived to be false without incoherence.
