This captures the formal structure and the conceivability test that the paradigm cases suggest. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the definition is furnished by the identity of Hesperus with Phosphorus. The proposition that the evening star is the morning star is true — both are Venus. Its negation does not generate a formal contradiction: 'Hesperus is not Phosphorus' is not of the form 'P and not-P.' And ancient astronomers conceived it to be false without any apparent incoherence, believing the two celestial bodies to be distinct. All three conditions are satisfied, yet the proposition is, on a widely held view, necessarily true. If Hesperus and Phosphorus are the same object, they could not have failed to be the same object; the identity holds of necessity. The conceivability of their distinctness reflects not genuine metaphysical possibility but the ignorance of those who did not know the astronomical facts. What the definition misses, and what the background cases all possessed, is genuine metaphysical contingency as opposed to the mere appearance of contingency generated by incomplete information about the objects in question.

background_cases: The brick thrown through the window is a cause of the window's breaking: the brick is a particular object that makes contact with the glass and transfers kinetic energy to it, shattering the molecular bonds that hold the pane together. The causal connection is mediated by a physical process — the transfer of momentum — that links cause and effect in a continuous chain.

The lightning bolt is a cause of the forest fire: the electrical discharge makes contact with dry timber and raises its temperature above the ignition point. The causal connection is mediated by a physical process — the conversion of electrical energy into thermal energy — that links the discharge to the conflagration.

The ingestion of arsenic is a cause of the victim's death: the arsenic enters the body, disrupts cellular respiration, and produces organ failure. The causal connection is mediated by a biochemical process — the binding of arsenic to enzymes — that links ingestion to death in a continuous sequence of physical events.
definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C precedes E in time, (ii) C is connected to E by a continuous physical process, and (iii) without C, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C precedes E in time
C is connected to E by a continuous physical process
Without C, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Consider a case in which two assassins, acting independently and in ignorance of each other, simultaneously fire at a victim. Each bullet is individually sufficient to kill, and both strike the victim at the same instant. Each shot precedes the death, and each is connected to the death by a continuous physical process — the trajectory of the bullet, the impact, the wound. But the third condition — that without C, E would not have occurred — fails for both shots. Without the first assassin's bullet, the victim would still have died from the second; without the second, he would still have died from the first. Neither shot is such that, without it, the death would not have occurred. The definition pronounces neither shot a cause of the death, yet both manifestly are.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve situations in which a single cause operates to produce the effect, so that the removal of the cause would indeed have prevented the effect. The definition captures this one-to-one structure but fails when two or more independently sufficient causes converge upon the same effect, because the counterfactual condition — that without the cause the effect would not have occurred — presupposes that each cause operates alone.
passage:
The cases that most readily come to mind when one thinks of causation share a characteristic structure: a single event initiates a physical process that terminates in the effect, and without that initiating event the effect would not have occurred. The brick shatters the window through the transfer of momentum; remove the brick and the window remains intact. The lightning ignites the forest through thermal energy; remove the lightning and the forest stands unburnt. The arsenic kills through biochemical disruption; remove the arsenic and the victim lives. In each case, one cause, one process, one effect, and a clear counterfactual dependence.

One might codify this pattern by defining a cause as an event that precedes the effect, is connected to it by a continuous physical process, and is such that without it the effect would not have occurred. This definition captures the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the phenomenon of causal overdetermination. Suppose two assassins, acting independently and in ignorance of each other, fire at a victim at the same instant. Each bullet follows a continuous physical trajectory and strikes the victim. Each is individually sufficient to cause death. The victim dies. Each shot precedes the death and is connected to it by a physical process. But neither satisfies the counterfactual condition: without the first shot, the second would have killed the victim; without the second, the first would have done so. The definition, which requires that the effect would not have occurred without the cause, cannot identify either shot as a cause. Yet both are causes in every intuitive sense. What the background cases all possessed — the singular, non-redundant character of the cause — the definition has taken as essential, when in fact it is merely a feature of the simplest cases. Causation admits of overdetermination, and a definition that requires counterfactual dependence cannot accommodate this possibility.

background_cases: A bronze statue of a horse persists through the repainting of its surface because the repainting alters only the outermost layer whilst the underlying bronze — the material that constitutes the statue — remains unchanged. The identity of the statue is secured by the continuity of its constitutive material.

A river persists through the daily replacement of its water because the river is identified not with any particular quantity of water but with the continuous channel through which water flows. The identity of the river is secured by the continuity of its form — the banks, the bed, the path of flow.

A ship persists through the replacement of a single plank because the overwhelming majority of its material remains the same and the structural organisation — the arrangement of keel, ribs, and planking — is preserved. The identity of the ship is secured by the joint continuity of material and structure.
definition: An object A at time t1 is identical with an object B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B share a continuous spatiotemporal path from t1 to t2, (ii) A and B share the same organising principle — the same structural plan or form, and (iii) the changes undergone between t1 and t2 are gradual rather than abrupt.
conditions: A and B share a continuous spatiotemporal path
A and B share the same organising principle or structural form
Changes between t1 and t2 are gradual rather than abrupt
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly. The organism occupies a continuous spatiotemporal path from the caterpillar stage through the pupal stage to the butterfly. The changes, though dramatic, occur gradually over a period of weeks within the chrysalis. But the organising principle — the structural form — changes utterly. The caterpillar is a segmented, crawling larva with chewing mouthparts and simple eyes; the butterfly is a winged, flying insect with a proboscis and compound eyes. The body plan is reorganised at the most fundamental level. The second condition fails. Yet the butterfly is the same organism as the caterpillar — biologists identify them as stages of a single individual life.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the statue, the river, the ship — all involve objects whose structural form is preserved throughout the changes they undergo. The definition captures this preservation of form but cannot accommodate transformations in which the organising principle itself changes radically, as in metamorphosis, where identity is secured not by continuity of form but by the continuity of an underlying biological process that generates successive and radically different forms.
passage:
When one examines the objects whose persistence through change seems least controversial, a common pattern emerges. The bronze statue retains its constitutive material through a change of surface colour. The river retains its form — banks, bed, path of flow — through a change of constituent water. The ship retains both material and structural plan through the replacement of a single plank. In each case, the object shares a continuous spatiotemporal path with its later self, maintains the same organising principle or structural form, and the changes it undergoes are gradual. These three features might be taken as jointly sufficient for identity over time.

One might therefore define the persistence of an object as requiring a continuous spatiotemporal path, a shared organising principle, and gradual change. This definition yields the correct verdict for the background cases: the statue, the river, and the ship all satisfy the three conditions. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the metamorphosis of insects. Consider a caterpillar that spins a chrysalis and emerges, some weeks later, as a butterfly. The organism occupies a continuous spatiotemporal path — at every moment between the caterpillar stage and the butterfly stage, it exists at a definite location in space. The changes occur gradually within the chrysalis, unfolding over days and weeks. But the organising principle changes utterly. The caterpillar is a segmented, crawling creature with a body plan organised around locomotion on surfaces; the butterfly is a winged insect whose body plan is organised around flight. The structural form of the caterpillar is dissolved within the chrysalis and a radically different form is assembled. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet biologists do not hesitate to identify the butterfly as the same individual organism as the caterpillar. What secures the identity in this case is not the preservation of structural form but the continuity of an underlying biological process — a developmental programme encoded in the organism's cells — that generates the successive forms. The definition, built from cases in which form is preserved, cannot accommodate cases in which form is transformed whilst the deeper continuity of a generative process persists.

background_cases: A cathedral persists through the centuries because each generation of builders and restorers replaces worn stones with stones of the same type, set in the same positions, according to the same architectural plan. The cathedral's persistence is secured by the continuous maintenance of its form in accordance with an authoritative design.

A family recipe persists through generations because each cook who inherits the recipe follows the same instructions, using the same ingredients and the same techniques, so that each instantiation of the dish is recognisably the same as its predecessors.

A musical score persists through centuries of performance because each performer reads the same notation and produces, within the limits of interpretation, the same sequence of notes, rhythms, and harmonies. The score's persistence is secured by the fidelity of each performance to the written text.
definition: An entity E persists through time if and only if (i) E has a continuous existence — there is no moment at which E ceases to exist and then resumes existing, (ii) E is maintained by a practice of faithful reproduction or repair, and (iii) each stage of E is recognisably similar to the preceding stage.
conditions: E has a continuous existence with no temporal gap
E is maintained by faithful reproduction or repair
Each stage is recognisably similar to the preceding stage
counterexample: Consider the tradition of ancient Greek drama. The plays of Aeschylus, Sophocles, and Euripides were performed continuously in the ancient world, fell into neglect during the medieval period — a gap of many centuries during which no performances took place and some texts were lost — and were revived during the Renaissance. There is a temporal gap: for centuries the tradition ceased to exist as a living practice. The tradition was not maintained by faithful reproduction during the gap; it was interrupted and later reconstituted from fragmentary texts. Yet we speak of the revival of Greek drama, not of the creation of a new tradition, and scholars and performers alike regard the modern performance of these plays as a continuation, however interrupted, of the ancient tradition.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the cathedral, the recipe, the musical score — all exhibit unbroken continuity: each stage follows the last without interruption, and each is maintained by a practice of faithful reproduction. The definition captures this unbroken chain but cannot accommodate cases in which a tradition or entity is interrupted, lost, and later reconstituted, yet is regarded as persisting across the gap. What secures identity in the case of the revived tradition is not continuous existence but the causal connection to the original — the surviving texts, the archaeological evidence, the scholarly labour of recovery — which bridges the gap even though the living practice was interrupted.
passage:
The entities whose persistence seems most secure share a pattern that rewards attention. A medieval cathedral persists because each generation of masons replaces worn stones according to the same architectural plan, maintaining an unbroken chain of repair. A family recipe persists because each generation of cooks follows the same instructions, maintaining an unbroken chain of faithful reproduction. A musical score persists because each generation of performers reads the same notation, maintaining an unbroken chain of performance. In each case, the entity exists continuously, is maintained by faithful reproduction or repair, and each stage is recognisably similar to the last.

One might codify these observations by defining persistence as requiring continuous existence, maintenance by faithful reproduction, and recognisable similarity between successive stages. The definition accommodates the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the tradition of ancient Greek drama. The tragedies of Aeschylus and Sophocles were performed regularly in the ancient world — at festivals, in theatres, before large audiences. But during the medieval period, the tradition of performance was interrupted. For centuries, no one performed these plays. Many texts were lost entirely; others survived only in fragmentary copies preserved by chance in monastic libraries. The first condition of the definition — continuous existence — fails: there is a gap of many hundreds of years during which the tradition of Greek drama did not exist as a living practice. The second condition — maintenance by faithful reproduction — also fails during the gap: no one was reproducing or repairing the tradition. Yet when Renaissance scholars recovered the surviving texts, when modern directors staged the plays anew, when audiences once again watched the Oresteia performed in a theatre, the universal judgement was that an ancient tradition had been revived, not that a new one had been created. The modern performances are understood as continuous with the ancient ones, despite the interruption of many centuries. What bridges the gap is not unbroken existence but causal connection: the surviving manuscripts, the scholarly tradition of recovery, the intention to continue what was begun in Athens twenty-five centuries ago. The definition, by requiring continuity, cannot accommodate the persistence of traditions that are interrupted and reconstituted.

background_cases: The proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen is necessarily true: it is not merely a fact that happens to obtain but a truth rooted in the nature of water itself. One cannot coherently imagine a substance that is water yet is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen, for the composition is constitutive of what water is.

The proposition that gold has atomic number 79 is necessarily true: it is not a contingent feature of gold but a fact about what makes gold the substance it is. A substance with a different atomic number would not be gold, regardless of its superficial resemblance.

The proposition that the interior angles of a triangle in Euclidean space sum to two right angles is necessarily true: it follows from the axioms and definitions of Euclidean geometry and could not be otherwise so long as the axioms hold.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) P is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms, and (iii) the denial of P is self-contradictory.
conditions: P is true
P is true in virtue of the meanings of its terms
The denial of P is self-contradictory
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. The proposition is true and is widely regarded as necessarily true — water could not be water without this composition. Yet the proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms. The word 'water' does not mean 'substance composed of hydrogen and oxygen'; it means, roughly, the familiar colourless, odourless liquid that fills rivers and lakes. The compositional fact was discovered empirically, not deduced from meanings. Moreover, the denial of the proposition — 'water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen' — is not self-contradictory in the way that 'a bachelor is married' is self-contradictory. One can entertain the denial without logical absurdity. The second and third conditions fail, yet the proposition is necessary.
abductive_insight: The background cases include both analytic necessities (the geometrical truth) and what appear to be necessary truths about natural kinds (water, gold). The definition captures the analytic cases — where truth flows from meaning and denial is contradictory — but cannot accommodate necessary truths that are discovered empirically and whose denial is coherent at the level of meaning, even though it is impossible at the level of nature. The class of necessary truths is broader than the class of analytic truths, and the definition has conflated the two.
passage:
The propositions most confidently regarded as necessary share, at first glance, a common character. That water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen seems to be a truth about the very nature of water. That gold has atomic number 79 seems to be a truth about the very nature of gold. That the angles of a Euclidean triangle sum to two right angles follows inexorably from the axioms. In each case, the truth appears to admit of no exception and to hold in every possible circumstance.

One might attempt to capture this common character by defining a necessary truth as a proposition that is true, true in virtue of the meanings of its terms, and such that its denial is self-contradictory. This definition accommodates the geometrical case with ease: that the angles of a triangle sum to two right angles is true, follows from the definitions of 'triangle' and 'Euclidean space,' and its denial is contradictory. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this definition is the proposition that water is composed of hydrogen and oxygen. This proposition is true and is, by the lights of many philosophers, necessarily true: a substance that lacked this composition would not be water, whatever its superficial properties. But the proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms. The word 'water' was in use for millennia before anyone knew that water had any chemical composition at all. Its meaning — the clear, drinkable liquid of rivers and rain — does not contain the concept of hydrogen or oxygen. The compositional fact was discovered by Lavoisier through experiment, not deduced from the meanings of words. And the denial of the proposition — that water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen — is not self-contradictory in any logical sense. One can entertain the thought without falling into absurdity, even though the thought turns out to be impossible. The background cases, upon reflection, divide into two kinds: those whose necessity is analytic, flowing from meaning, and those whose necessity is grounded in the natures of things. The definition captures only the first kind and thereby excludes some of the most important necessary truths from the category of necessity.

background_cases: A bronze statue is a substance: it exists independently, requiring no other particular entity to sustain it; it possesses a determinate nature — its shape, its material, its weight — that distinguishes it from all other entities; and it endures through time, persisting through minor surface changes whilst retaining its identity.

A living oak tree is a substance: it stands on its own, drawing sustenance from the soil but not depending for its existence on any other particular entity; it has a determinate nature — its species, its form, its pattern of growth — that marks it out; and it persists through decades of change in its leaves, bark, and branches.

A diamond is a substance: it exists independently of any observer or context; it possesses a determinate crystalline structure that distinguishes it from graphite or coal; and it endures through extraordinary lengths of time without alteration.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X exists independently, not requiring any other particular entity for its continued existence, (ii) X possesses a determinate nature that distinguishes it from all other entities, and (iii) X endures through time, persisting through changes in its accidental properties.
conditions: X exists independently, not requiring any other particular entity for its continued existence
X possesses a determinate nature that distinguishes it from all other entities
X endures through time, persisting through changes in its accidental properties
counterexample: Consider a shadow cast by a pillar in the afternoon sun. The shadow exists, in one sense, independently — no other particular entity besides the pillar and the sun is needed to produce it. It possesses a determinate nature: a definite shape, size, and position that distinguish it from every other shadow. And it endures through time, persisting through changes in its accidental properties — it grows longer as the afternoon progresses, changes its angle, yet remains recognisably the same shadow. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one would hesitate to call a shadow a substance. A shadow is not a thing in its own right but an absence — a region of diminished illumination defined entirely by the spatial relation between the source of light and the opaque object.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve entities that are positive existences — things composed of matter or organised processes that possess causal powers of their own. The definition captures the independence, determinacy, and endurance of these positive entities but cannot exclude negative or privative phenomena — shadows, holes, silences — that satisfy the conditions formally whilst lacking the causal self-sufficiency and positive ontological character that we associate with genuine substances.
passage:
The entities that serve as our paradigmatic substances share a telling pattern. The bronze statue stands on its own, possesses a nature that sets it apart from every other thing, and persists through surface changes. The oak tree draws sustenance from the soil but depends on no other particular entity for its existence as a tree; it has a determinate species-nature and endures for decades. The diamond exists independently, possesses a unique crystalline structure, and endures for geological ages. In each case, we find an entity that is independent, determinate, and enduring — an entity, moreover, that possesses causal powers of its own: the statue resists pressure, the tree produces acorns, the diamond scratches glass.

One might codify these observations by defining a substance as an entity that exists independently, possesses a determinate nature, and endures through changes in its accidental properties. This definition accommodates the background cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the shadow cast by a pillar in the afternoon sun. The shadow satisfies all three conditions. It exists independently, in the sense that no particular entity besides the pillar and the sun is required for it. It possesses a determinate nature — a definite shape and size. And it endures through time, growing longer as the sun descends, changing angle and proportion, yet remaining recognisably the same shadow. But we should hesitate to call a shadow a substance. A shadow is a privation — a region where light is absent — rather than a positive existence. It has no causal powers of its own; it cannot resist pressure, produce effects, or interact with other entities. The background cases all involved positive existences — entities constituted by matter or organised processes, entities with causal powers that flow from their nature. The definition, by attending to independence, determinacy, and endurance, captures the structural features these positive entities exhibit but neglects the positive ontological character that distinguishes them from mere absences and privations.

background_cases: The spark causes the fire: the spark occurs first, the fire follows, and without the spark the dry kindling would not have ignited. The causal relation runs from the earlier event to the later, and the removal of the earlier event would have prevented the later.

The administration of penicillin causes the patient's recovery: the injection precedes the recovery, and without the penicillin the bacterial infection would have continued unchecked. The counterfactual dependence is clear — no injection, no recovery.
The opening of the dam causes the flooding of the valley: the gates are opened, water rushes through, and the valley is inundated. Without the opening, the valley would have remained dry.
definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C occurs before E, (ii) without C, E would not have occurred, and (iii) C and E are connected by a continuous chain of intermediate events.
conditions: C occurs before E
Without C, E would not have occurred
C and E are connected by a continuous chain of intermediate events
counterexample: Consider a case of preemptive causation. A marksman fires and kills his target. Unknown to the marksman, a second assassin had independently aimed at the same target and would have fired and killed him a moment later had the first shot not already done so. The first shot precedes the death and is connected to it by a continuous chain of events — the trajectory of the bullet, the impact, the wound. But the second condition is not satisfied: without the first shot, the target would still have died, killed by the second assassin's bullet a moment later. The definition cannot identify the first shot as a cause, for the counterfactual dependence fails. Yet the first shot is plainly the cause of the death — it is the event that actually produced it.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the definition has silently incorporated as essential: in each, the cause is the sole sufficient condition for the effect, so that removing the cause would indeed have prevented the effect. The definition works when causes operate in isolation but fails when a backup cause stands ready to produce the same effect, because the counterfactual condition — that the effect would not have occurred without the cause — presupposes the absence of preemptive alternatives.
passage:
When one examines the clearest cases of causation, a pattern emerges that might seem to exhaust the concept. The spark causes the fire: the spark occurs first, a continuous chain of combustion connects the two, and without the spark the kindling would have remained inert. The penicillin causes the recovery: the injection comes first, the antibiotics work through the body in a continuous biochemical process, and without the injection the infection would have persisted. The opening of the dam causes the flood: the gates open, water flows through a continuous channel, and without the opening the valley would have remained dry. In each case, the cause precedes the effect, the effect depends counterfactually upon the cause, and a continuous chain of events connects them.

One might therefore define causation by these three conditions: temporal precedence, counterfactual dependence, and a continuous connecting chain. The definition yields the correct verdict for the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers the phenomenon of preemptive causation. A marksman fires at his target and kills him. Unknown to the marksman, a second assassin had independently aimed at the same target and would have fired a moment later, killing the target by a different route. The first shot precedes the death and is connected to it by a continuous chain — the bullet's flight, the impact, the fatal wound. But without the first shot, the target would still have died: the second assassin would have fired and killed him. The counterfactual condition fails. The definition must therefore pronounce the first shot a non-cause, which is manifestly wrong — the first shot is precisely what caused the death. What the background cases all possessed was the feature that the cause was the sole sufficient antecedent of the effect. The definition captures this one-cause structure but cannot accommodate situations in which a backup cause stands ready to produce the same effect if the actual cause had not operated.

background_cases: A river persists through the continuous replacement of its water because the channel — the banks, the bed, the course — remains the same; each momentary stage of the river is connected to the next by the flow of water through a stable form.

A flame persists on a candle because, although the wax molecules that fuel it are constantly consumed and replaced by fresh molecules drawn up the wick, the process of combustion is continuous and the spatial location of the flame remains stable.

A university persists through the centuries because, although its students, professors, and administrators are constantly replaced, the institutional structure — its charter, its governing procedures, its buildings — provides a continuous framework within which successive generations operate.
definition: An entity E persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) E at t1 and E at t2 are connected by a continuous process of material or structural maintenance, (ii) each successive stage of E is causally produced by the preceding stage, and (iii) the characteristic form or organisation of E is preserved throughout.
conditions: E at t1 and E at t2 are connected by a continuous process of maintenance
Each successive stage is causally produced by the preceding stage
The characteristic form or organisation is preserved throughout
counterexample: Consider the case of a frozen lake in winter. In autumn, the lake is a body of liquid water. As the temperature drops, the surface freezes; as winter deepens, the ice extends downward. In spring, the ice melts and the lake returns to its liquid state. The lake persists through this cycle — we call it the same lake before, during, and after the freezing. The first two conditions are satisfied: there is a continuous physical process connecting the liquid and frozen stages, and each stage is causally produced by the preceding one through the thermodynamic exchange of heat. But the characteristic form or organisation is not preserved. Liquid water and solid ice have profoundly different structures — different molecular arrangements, different physical properties, different behaviour. The third condition fails. Yet the lake persists through the phase transition.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the river, the flame, the university — all involve entities whose characteristic form is maintained throughout their persistence: the river keeps its channel, the flame keeps its conical shape, the university keeps its institutional structure. The definition captures this form-preserving pattern but cannot accommodate entities that persist through radical phase transitions, in which the characteristic form or organisation is transformed rather than preserved. What secures the lake's persistence is not the preservation of form but the continuity of the material and the causal process that transforms it and returns it to its earlier state.
passage:
The entities whose persistence through change seems most natural share a notable feature. A river persists through the replacement of its water because the channel — banks, bed, course — remains constant. A flame persists because the process of combustion continues without interruption in the same location, even though the fuel is constantly consumed. A university persists because its institutional framework — charter, governance, buildings — provides a stable structure through which generations of scholars pass. In each case, a continuous process of maintenance connects the earlier and later stages, each stage causally produces the next, and the characteristic form or organisation is preserved.

One might codify these observations by defining persistence as requiring a continuous process of maintenance, the causal production of each stage by its predecessor, and the preservation of characteristic form. This definition accommodates the background cases well. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the persistence of a lake through the cycle of freezing and thawing. In autumn, the lake is a body of liquid water with the properties characteristic of that state — fluidity, transparency, the capacity to sustain aquatic life. As winter descends, the surface freezes and the ice gradually extends downward. The liquid water becomes solid ice — a substance with a profoundly different molecular arrangement, different optical properties, and different mechanical behaviour. In spring, the ice melts and the lake returns to its liquid state. The first two conditions of the definition are satisfied: there is a continuous thermodynamic process connecting the stages, and each is causally produced by the preceding one through the exchange of heat with the atmosphere. But the third condition fails: the characteristic form of the lake — its fluidity, its transparency, its capacity to sustain fish and aquatic plants — is not preserved during the frozen period. The ice is, structurally and organisationally, a very different thing from the liquid water. Yet we say without hesitation that the lake persists through the winter. The background cases all involved form-preservation; the definition has mistaken this common feature for a necessary one.

background_cases: It is possible that this table could have been placed in the corner rather than the centre of the room: the table exists, the corner exists, and there is no physical or logical obstacle to the table's being in the corner; we can coherently conceive of the alternative arrangement without any contradiction.

It is possible that the 1966 World Cup final could have been won by West Germany rather than England: the match was played, the outcome was contingent, and we can coherently conceive of a different result without any violation of the laws of football or of physics.

It is possible that Queen Victoria could have refused to sign the Reform Act of 1867: she was a rational agent with the legal power to withhold her signature, and we can coherently conceive of her exercising that power without contradiction.
definition: A state of affairs S is possible if and only if (i) S is conceivable without contradiction, (ii) S involves only entities or kinds of entities that actually exist, and (iii) S is compatible with the laws of nature as we understand them.
conditions: S is conceivable without contradiction
S involves only entities or kinds of entities that actually exist
S is compatible with the laws of nature
counterexample: Consider the possibility that a species of large flightless bird, now wholly extinct, might still inhabit an undiscovered island in the Pacific. The state of affairs is conceivable without contradiction. It is compatible with the laws of nature — there is no physical law that prevents large flightless birds from existing. But the second condition requires that S involve only entities or kinds of entities that actually exist, and the species in question — the dodo, say — is extinct. There are no dodos; the kind exists only in the past. If we interpret the condition strictly, the state of affairs is excluded. Yet it seems perfectly possible, in an important sense, that a dodo might have survived on an undiscovered island.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve entities and kinds of entities that are uncontroversially actual — tables, football matches, historical persons. The definition captures the conditions that apply when the constituents of the possible state of affairs are drawn from the existing inventory of the world, but it breaks down when possibility concerns entities or kinds that once existed but exist no longer, because the second condition confuses what currently exists with what could exist given the laws of nature and the history of the world.
passage:
When one considers the clearest cases of possibility, a natural pattern emerges. It is possible that this table could have been placed in the corner: the table exists, the corner exists, no contradiction arises. It is possible that the 1966 World Cup could have gone to West Germany: the match was played, the outcome was contingent, no law of nature was at stake. It is possible that Queen Victoria could have withheld her signature: she existed, she had the power, no contradiction follows. In each case, the state of affairs is conceivable without contradiction, involves entities that actually exist, and accords with the laws of nature.

One might therefore define possibility as conceivability without contradiction, involvement of only actually existing entities, and compatibility with the laws of nature. The definition yields the correct verdict for the background cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider possibilities involving entities that once existed but exist no longer. Consider the suggestion that a dodo might still survive on some undiscovered island in the Pacific. The state of affairs is conceivable without contradiction — there is nothing logically incoherent about a surviving dodo. It is compatible with the laws of nature — no physical law prevents a large flightless bird from inhabiting an island. But the dodo is extinct. There are no dodos in the world today. The second condition — that S involve only entities or kinds that actually exist — fails, at least on a strict reading. Yet the possibility seems genuine. Biologists recognise that the extinction of the dodo was a contingent historical event, and that the species might have survived under slightly different circumstances. The background cases all involved entities drawn from the present inventory of the world. The definition captures this feature but wrongly elevates it to a necessary condition, excluding perfectly intelligible possibilities that concern entities or kinds whose actual existence has ceased.

background_cases: A marble statue weighs precisely as much as the marble from which it is carved, occupies the same region of space, and is composed of the same atoms. Yet the statue can be destroyed by reshaping the marble into a sphere, while the marble persists through the change. The statue and the marble are materially identical but differ in their conditions of persistence.

A river persists as the same river despite the continuous replacement of its water. The Thames today contains none of the water molecules it contained a century ago, yet it is the same river. Its identity is constituted not by its material composition but by the continuity of its form and the causal processes that maintain it.

A human body replaces nearly all of its cells over the course of several years, yet the person persists. The person's identity cannot consist in the identity of the material parts, for these are entirely replaced.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X has a determinate material composition at any given time, (ii) X occupies a definite region of space, and (iii) X persists through time by maintaining its material identity.
conditions: X has a determinate material composition at any given time
X occupies a definite region of space
X persists through time by maintaining its material identity
counterexample: Consider a wave moving across the surface of the ocean. The wave has a determinate material composition at any given instant — it is composed of particular water molecules. It occupies a definite region of space. Yet it does not persist by maintaining its material identity: as the wave propagates, the water molecules that compose it are continuously replaced, each parcel of water rising and falling in place while the wave form travels onward. The wave persists not through material continuity but through the propagation of a pattern.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the statue, the river, the human body — all exhibit a separation between material composition and identity over time. The wave case brings this separation into sharpest relief: the wave is a paradigmatic instance of an entity that persists through the propagation of form rather than the retention of matter. What the definition lacks, and what the background cases jointly suggest, is the recognition that substances may persist through the continuity of their organising principles — their form, structure, or causal pattern — rather than through the continuity of their material constituents.
passage:
When one examines the conditions under which particular things persist through change, a recurring pattern suggests itself. A marble statue and its marble coincide materially, yet the statue is destroyed by a reshaping that leaves the marble intact. The Thames persists as the same river despite the complete replacement of its waters. The human body persists as the same organism despite the gradual replacement of nearly all its cells. In each case, identity over time is not grounded in the retention of material parts.

One might attempt to characterise substance as that which has a determinate material composition, occupies a definite spatial region, and persists by maintaining its material identity over time. This characterisation captures the intuitive thought that substances are material things that endure. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with striking clarity in the phenomenon of ocean waves. A wave moving across the surface of the sea has, at any given instant, a determinate material composition — it is constituted by particular water molecules occupying a definite region of space. Yet as the wave propagates, those molecules are continuously replaced: each parcel of water merely rises and falls in place, while the wave form travels onward across the ocean. The wave persists not through the retention of its material constituents but through the propagation of a dynamical pattern. It appears that the definition, by tying persistence to material identity, cannot accommodate entities whose continued existence consists in the maintenance of form or pattern rather than of matter. The background cases jointly suggest that what we call a substance need not be individuated by its material composition at all, but rather by the organising principle — the structure, form, or causal pattern — that makes it the thing it is.

background_cases: Striking a match in the presence of oxygen causes it to ignite. The striking is not merely followed by ignition; it brings about ignition by generating friction that raises the temperature of the match head to its ignition point. The causal relation involves a specifiable physical mechanism linking cause to effect.

Heating water to one hundred degrees Celsius at standard atmospheric pressure causes it to boil. The causal connexion is mediated by the increase in kinetic energy of the water molecules, which at the boiling point acquire sufficient energy to escape the liquid phase. The mechanism is continuous and traceable.

Dropping a porcelain vase onto a stone floor causes it to shatter. The impact generates stresses that exceed the tensile strength of the porcelain, propagating fractures through the material. The causal link consists in a chain of physical interactions that can be described in precise mechanical terms.
definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is a necessary condition for E — that is, E would not have occurred had C not occurred, (ii) C occurs temporally prior to E, and (iii) there is a lawful regularity connecting events of type C with events of type E.
conditions: C is a necessary condition for E — that is, E would not have occurred had C not occurred
C occurs temporally prior to E
There is a lawful regularity connecting events of type C with events of type E
counterexample: Consider a man who is crossing the desert and whose water canteen has been secretly poisoned by an enemy. Before the man drinks the poisoned water, a second enemy, acting independently, punctures the canteen with a rifle shot from a great distance. The water drains into the sand, and the man dies of thirst. The puncturing of the canteen is a necessary condition for the man's death by thirst — had the canteen not been punctured, he would have drunk the poisoned water and died of poisoning instead, not of thirst. The puncturing precedes the death. And there is a lawful regularity connecting the loss of water in a desert with death by dehydration. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the second enemy's shot also, paradoxically, saved the man from a swifter death by poisoning.
abductive_insight: The background cases all exhibit a continuous physical mechanism linking cause to effect — friction generating heat, kinetic energy driving phase transitions, mechanical stress propagating fractures. The desert case satisfies the counterfactual, temporal, and nomological conditions, yet the causal relation feels peculiar because the puncturing of the canteen both caused one kind of death and prevented another. What the definition lacks is a requirement that the cause bring about the effect through a continuous and non-preempted causal mechanism, rather than merely satisfying abstract counterfactual and nomological criteria.
passage:
When one examines clear cases of causation, a common structural feature becomes apparent. The striking of a match causes ignition through a traceable physical mechanism — friction generates heat, which raises the temperature of the match head past its ignition point. The heating of water causes boiling through the continuous increase of molecular kinetic energy. The dropping of a vase causes shattering through the propagation of mechanical stress. In each paradigmatic case, cause and effect are linked by a continuous chain of physical interactions.

One might attempt to codify these observations by defining a cause as a necessary condition for the effect, temporally prior to it, and connected to it by a lawful regularity. This definition captures the logical and nomological structure of causal relations. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed in the following manner. Suppose a man is crossing a desert carrying a canteen that has been secretly poisoned by an enemy. Before the man drinks, a second enemy, acting independently, shoots a hole in the canteen from a great distance. The water drains into the sand, and the man dies of thirst. The puncturing of the canteen is a necessary condition for death by thirst: had the canteen not been punctured, the man would have drunk the poisoned water and died of poisoning, not of dehydration. The puncturing precedes the death. And there is a lawful regularity connecting water deprivation in desert conditions with death by dehydration. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the causal structure is far more complex than the definition suggests: the second enemy's action both caused one mode of death and prevented another. The background cases suggest that genuine causation requires a continuous mechanism linking cause to effect, whereas the desert case, though it satisfies the counterfactual and nomological criteria, involves a causal pathway that is entangled with the preemption of an alternative process. It appears that the definition cannot distinguish straightforward causation from cases in which the causal history is complicated by the preemption of alternative causal routes.

background_cases: The succession of day and night provides the most familiar measure of temporal passage. Each day is distinguishable from the last by the events that occur within it — the weather changes, conversations are held, meals are eaten. The passage of time is here constituted by the occurrence of qualitative change.

The decay of a radioactive isotope provides a measure of time that is independent of any observer. The half-life of carbon-14 is approximately 5,730 years regardless of the surrounding conditions. Here, temporal passage is grounded in a process that is lawful, regular, and invariant under changes of context.

The swinging of a pendulum measures time by the repetition of a uniform motion. Each oscillation is qualitatively identical to the last, yet we regard successive swings as occurring at distinct moments. The passage of time is marked not by qualitative novelty but by the repetition of an identical process.
definition: Time passes if and only if (i) events occur in a determinate linear sequence, (ii) each event is qualitatively distinguishable from every other, and (iii) the sequence is irreversible — earlier events cannot recur after later events.
conditions: Events occur in a determinate linear sequence
Each event is qualitatively distinguishable from every other
The sequence is irreversible — earlier events cannot recur after later events
counterexample: Consider the swinging of a perfect frictionless pendulum in a vacuum. Each oscillation is qualitatively identical to the last — the same arc, the same speed, the same position at corresponding phases. Time passes during the pendulum's motion; indeed, the pendulum is used to measure its passage. Yet successive oscillations are not qualitatively distinguishable from one another. The second condition fails. Moreover, in a perfectly periodic system, the state of the pendulum at the beginning of each cycle is identical to its state at the beginning of every other cycle, raising the question of whether 'recurrence' and 'irreversibility' can even be defined for such a system.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal a tension within the concept of temporal passage. The succession of days grounds temporal passage in qualitative change; radioactive decay grounds it in a lawful process independent of qualitative novelty; and the pendulum grounds it in the repetition of qualitatively identical states. The pendulum case shows most clearly that temporal passage does not require qualitative distinguishability — time passes even when nothing qualitatively new occurs. What the definition lacks is the recognition that temporal passage may be constituted by the mere fact of succession itself, independently of whether successive states differ qualitatively.
passage:
The measurement of time in physics and in daily life proceeds by diverse means, yet certain features recur. The succession of days provides a measure grounded in qualitative change — each day brings new events. The decay of radioactive isotopes provides a measure grounded in lawful regularity, independent of any observer's experience. The swinging of a pendulum provides a measure grounded in the repetition of a uniform motion. These background cases suggest that temporal passage is compatible with both qualitative novelty and qualitative sameness.

One might attempt to characterise temporal passage by holding that time passes if and only if events occur in a determinate linear sequence, each event is qualitatively distinguishable from every other, and the sequence is irreversible. This characterisation captures the intuitive sense that time brings something new at each moment and that the past cannot be revisited. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with the simplest of timekeeping devices. Consider a perfect frictionless pendulum swinging in a vacuum. Each oscillation is qualitatively identical to the last — the same trajectory, the same velocity, the same position at corresponding phases. Time manifestly passes during the pendulum's motion; the pendulum is used to measure that passage. Yet successive oscillations are not qualitatively distinguishable. The second condition fails. Furthermore, the state at the beginning of each cycle is identical to the state at the beginning of every other cycle, raising a genuine difficulty about what irreversibility could mean for such a system. The background cases jointly suggest that temporal passage does not require qualitative novelty — the pendulum case shows that time can pass in the absence of qualitative change. It appears that the definition, by requiring distinguishability, conflates temporal passage with qualitative variation.

background_cases: The redness of a ripe tomato and the redness of a fire engine are instances of the same property. Despite the differences between the two objects — one organic, the other mechanical — they share a genuine common feature that is not reducible to the resemblance between them; the redness is wholly present in each.

The sphericity of a billiard ball and the sphericity of the Earth are instances of the same geometrical property. The two differ enormously in scale, material, and context, yet they share the same shape, and this shared shape explains why certain geometrical theorems apply equally to both.

The mass of one electron is identical to the mass of every other electron. This exact sameness — not mere resemblance but strict numerical identity of a property — is what grounds the universality of the laws of particle physics.
definition: A property P is a genuine universal if and only if (i) P is instantiated by at least two distinct particulars, (ii) P is intrinsic to the particulars that instantiate it — that is, P characterises each particular independently of its relations to other things, and (iii) P grounds objective resemblance among its instances.
conditions: P is instantiated by at least two distinct particulars
P is intrinsic to the particulars that instantiate it — that is, P characterises each particular independently of its relations to other things
P grounds objective resemblance among its instances
counterexample: Consider the property of being famous. Many distinct particulars instantiate this property — Napoleon, Shakespeare, the Mona Lisa. It grounds a genuine resemblance: famous things resemble one another in being widely recognised. Yet fame is not intrinsic to the objects that possess it. Napoleon's fame depends upon the beliefs and attitudes of millions of other people; it is a relational property that the individual possesses only in virtue of his relations to a community of observers. The second condition fails. Yet one might observe that fame seems to be a genuine, objective feature of the social world — not a mere fiction — and that a theory of universals which excludes it may be too restrictive.
abductive_insight: The background cases — redness, sphericity, electron mass — all involve properties that inhere in their instances independently of the wider relational context. Fame reveals that not all genuine, objective properties are intrinsic: some are constituted by the relations between an object and a community. The definition, by requiring intrinsicality, captures the paradigmatic physical universals but cannot accommodate the social and relational properties that are equally real and equally grounding of objective resemblance. What the background cases suggest, when taken together with the fame case, is that the mark of a genuine universal is not intrinsicality but objective explanatory role — and relational properties can play such a role.
passage:
The clearest instances of universals are properties that inhere in their instances independently of context. The redness of a tomato and of a fire engine is present in each object regardless of its relations to other things. The sphericity of a billiard ball and of the Earth is a geometrical feature that each possesses in isolation. The mass of an electron is strictly identical across every instance, grounding the universality of physical law. In each background case, the property is intrinsic: it characterises the particular without reference to anything external.

One might attempt to codify these observations by defining a genuine universal as a property that is instantiated by at least two particulars, is intrinsic to each, and grounds objective resemblance among its instances. This definition captures the core physical and geometrical universals with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers properties of the social world. Consider the property of being famous. Many distinct particulars possess this property — Napoleon, Shakespeare, the Mona Lisa. Famous things genuinely resemble one another in respect of fame: they are widely recognised, discussed, and depicted. Yet fame is manifestly not intrinsic. Napoleon's fame depends upon the attitudes of millions of people; it is constituted by the relation between the individual and a community of observers. The second condition of the definition fails. Nevertheless, one might observe that fame is a genuine, objective feature of the social world — it is not arbitrary, it admits of degrees, and it has real explanatory power. The background cases suggest that the mark of a genuine universal is its capacity to ground objective resemblance and to play an explanatory role. Fame does both. It appears that the definition, by insisting upon intrinsicality, captures one important class of universals but cannot accommodate the relational properties that are equally real and equally explanatory.

background_cases: When a stone is thrown at a window and the window shatters, the causal connection is sustained by a continuous physical process — the stone traverses the intervening space, strikes the glass, and transmits kinetic energy sufficient to fracture it. The cause and the effect are linked by a spatiotemporally continuous chain of physical interactions.

When a flame is applied to a sheet of paper and the paper ignites, the causal connection is sustained by a continuous physical process — the flame heats the paper to its ignition temperature, at which point the cellulose undergoes rapid oxidation. The cause and the effect are linked by a transfer of thermal energy through direct contact.

When a billiard ball strikes another and the second ball moves, the causal connection is sustained by a continuous physical process — the first ball makes contact with the second and transfers momentum through the collision. No gap intervenes between cause and effect; the process is spatially and temporally continuous.
definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C occurs before E, (ii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred, and (iii) C and E are events of types that are regularly conjoined in experience.
conditions: C occurs before E
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
C and E are events of types regularly conjoined in experience
counterexample: Suppose a man drinks coffee every morning at eight o'clock, and at nine o'clock his neighbour's dog begins barking — not because of the coffee but because the postman arrives at nine. The coffee-drinking occurs before the barking. Events of these types are regularly conjoined — every morning, coffee at eight is followed by barking at nine. And had the man not drunk his coffee, he would have left the house earlier and been absent when the dog barked, so in a strained sense the counterfactual is satisfied. All three conditions appear met, yet the coffee does not cause the barking. The regular conjunction is a coincidence of scheduling, and the counterfactual dependence is an artefact of the man's routine rather than a genuine causal connection.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the coffee-and-barking case: in each, the cause is connected to the effect by a continuous physical process that transmits some conserved quantity — energy, momentum, or heat — from cause to effect. The stone transmits kinetic energy to the window; the flame transmits thermal energy to the paper; the billiard ball transmits momentum to its partner. In the coffee case, no physical process links the drinking to the barking. The definition captures temporal priority, counterfactual dependence, and regularity, but it omits the requirement of a connecting physical process — the very feature that distinguishes genuine causation from mere correlation in the paradigmatic cases.
passage:
When one examines the cases most clearly recognised as instances of causation, a structural feature emerges that goes beyond temporal priority, counterfactual dependence, and regular conjunction. When a stone shatters a window, the stone traverses the intervening space and transmits kinetic energy to the glass. When a flame ignites paper, thermal energy passes by direct contact from the flame to the cellulose. When a billiard ball sets another in motion, momentum is transferred through collision. In each case, the cause is connected to the effect by a continuous physical process — a spatiotemporally continuous chain along which some quantity is transmitted.

One might attempt to characterise causation as a relation in which the cause occurs before the effect, the effect would not have occurred without the cause, and the two are regularly conjoined. This definition captures important features of causal thinking. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency may be constructed from everyday coincidence. Suppose a man drinks coffee every morning at eight o'clock, and at nine o'clock his neighbour's dog begins barking — not because of the coffee but because the postman arrives at nine. The coffee-drinking precedes the barking. The two event-types are regularly conjoined in experience. And had the man not drunk his coffee, he would have left the house earlier and been absent when the dog barked, lending a surface plausibility to the counterfactual condition. All three conditions appear met, yet no one would say the coffee causes the barking. What the paradigmatic cases possess and the coffee case lacks is a continuous physical process linking cause to effect — a transmission of energy or momentum through an unbroken spatiotemporal chain. The definition, by relying on temporal order, counterfactual dependence, and regularity alone, cannot distinguish genuine causal connections from coincidental conjunctions sustained by independent schedules.

background_cases: When a river is dammed and its course diverted, the resulting stream is regarded as the same river despite the alteration of its path. The identity is sustained by a continuous flow of water from the same source, through a connected channel, to a new terminus. The river persists because the organising principle — the continuous flow from a particular source — persists.

When a university changes its name, relocates to a new campus, and replaces its entire faculty over the course of a century, it is still regarded as the same institution. The identity is sustained by an unbroken chain of institutional continuity — each change is authorised by the existing body, records are maintained, and the institution's charter is preserved.

When a human being grows from infancy to old age, losing and replacing nearly every cell in the body, the person is regarded as the same individual. The identity is sustained by the continuity of the living organism — each stage develops from the preceding one through a continuous biological process, and the organising structure of the body is maintained throughout.
definition: An entity A at time t1 is the same entity as B at time t2 if and only if (i) A and B are qualitatively similar in their essential respects, (ii) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path linking A at t1 to B at t2, and (iii) no disruption or discontinuity occurs along that path.
conditions: A and B are qualitatively similar in essential respects
There is a continuous spatiotemporal path linking them
No disruption or discontinuity occurs along the path
counterexample: Consider an amoeba that divides into two daughter cells. Prior to division, there is a single organism. After division, there are two organisms, each connected to the original by a continuous spatiotemporal path with no disruption. Each daughter cell is qualitatively similar to the original in essential respects — each is an amoeba with the same genetic constitution. All three conditions are satisfied for both daughter cells. Yet the original amoeba cannot be identical with both daughters, for the two daughters are distinct from one another, and identity is a relation that cannot hold between one entity and two.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the amoeba case: in each, the organising principle or structure that sustains identity is preserved in a single continuous successor rather than branching into multiple successors. The river's flow continues in one channel; the university's charter is held by one institution; the human organism develops as a single body. The amoeba case fails because the organising principle branches, producing two equally valid claimants to identity with the original. The definition captures qualitative similarity and spatiotemporal continuity but omits the requirement that the identity-sustaining continuity be non-branching — that it produce at most one successor — which the paradigmatic cases reveal to be essential.
passage:
The entities we most confidently identify across time share a structural feature that becomes visible only when we consider cases of branching. A river that is dammed and diverted remains the same river because its continuous flow from a single source proceeds through one connected channel. A university that changes its name and campus remains the same institution because its charter and records are maintained by a single continuous body. A human being who grows from infant to elder remains the same person because the living organism develops as a single continuous structure. In each case, the organising principle that sustains identity through change is preserved in one and only one successor.

One might attempt to characterise identity over time as obtaining when two temporal stages are qualitatively similar in essential respects, linked by a continuous spatiotemporal path, and connected without disruption. This definition captures the standard desiderata. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises in biology. Consider an amoeba that undergoes binary fission. Before the division, there is one organism. After it, there are two — each connected to the original by a continuous spatiotemporal path, each qualitatively similar to the original in every essential respect, and each arising without discontinuity. All three conditions are satisfied for both daughter cells. Yet the original cannot be identical with both, for the two daughters are distinct entities occupying different regions of space. Identity is a one-one relation, and a definition that permits it to hold between one predecessor and two successors simultaneously has failed to capture a necessary constraint. What the background cases possess and the amoeba case lacks is the non-branching character of the identity-sustaining continuity. The definition attends to similarity, continuity, and the absence of disruption, but omits the requirement that the process yield at most one successor — the very feature that the paradigmatic cases reveal to be indispensable.

background_cases: A bronze statue that is melted down and recast into a different shape ceases to exist as a statue even though the bronze persists. The matter survives, but the form — the particular arrangement that constituted the statue — has been destroyed. The statue's persistence depended upon the preservation of its form, not merely the survival of its material.

A clock whose mainspring breaks and whose gears are scattered across a workbench ceases to function as a clock even though all its parts continue to exist. The parts survive, but the functional organisation that made them a clock has been disrupted. The clock's persistence depended upon the maintenance of functional organisation, not merely the continued existence of components.

A choir whose members gradually retire and are replaced persists as the same choir so long as the replacements maintain the same repertoire, rehearsal schedule, and performance standards. The choir persists not because the same individuals sing but because the organising pattern — the repertoire and performance practice — is maintained across changes of membership.
definition: An object O persists through change if and only if (i) the matter composing O at t1 is the same matter composing O at t2, (ii) O occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region from t1 to t2, and (iii) O retains its recognisable outward appearance throughout.
conditions: The matter composing O is the same at both times
O occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region
O retains its recognisable outward appearance
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis. Within the chrysalis, most of the caterpillar's tissues are dissolved and reorganised into an entirely different body plan — that of a butterfly. The matter is largely the same. The organism occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region throughout the process. But the outward appearance changes beyond recognition: the crawling, segmented caterpillar is replaced by a winged, compound-eyed butterfly that bears no visible resemblance to its predecessor. The third condition fails. Yet biologists regard the butterfly as the same organism as the caterpillar — a single individual that has undergone a developmental transformation.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that persistence depends upon the maintenance of an organising principle — form, functional organisation, or pattern of practice — rather than upon sameness of matter or outward appearance. The statue ceases to exist when its form is destroyed, even though its matter survives. The clock ceases to exist when its functional organisation is disrupted, even though its parts survive. The choir persists when its organising pattern is maintained, even though its members change. The caterpillar-butterfly case shows that an organism can persist through a radical change in appearance because its organising principle — the developmental programme encoded in its genetic constitution — is maintained throughout. The definition requires sameness of matter and appearance but omits the organising principle, which the paradigmatic cases reveal to be the true bearer of persistence.
passage:
When one examines the conditions under which objects persist and cease to exist, a pattern emerges that the most familiar analyses fail to capture. A bronze statue melted down and recast ceases to exist as a statue even though every particle of bronze survives — the form has been destroyed. A clock whose gears are scattered ceases to exist as a clock even though every component is preserved — the functional organisation has been disrupted. A choir whose members are replaced one by one persists as the same choir so long as the repertoire and performance practice are maintained — the organising pattern survives the change of individual participants. In each case, persistence depends not on the survival of particular matter or outward appearance but on the maintenance of an organising principle.

One might attempt to characterise persistence as requiring sameness of matter, spatiotemporal continuity, and retention of recognisable appearance. This definition captures the intuition that a persisting object should look the same and be made of the same stuff. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by biological metamorphosis. A caterpillar enters a chrysalis and undergoes a transformation so radical that most of its tissues are dissolved and reorganised. It emerges as a butterfly — an organism with wings, compound eyes, and a body plan utterly unlike the segmented, crawling form it had before. The matter is largely preserved. The organism occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region. But the outward appearance has changed beyond recognition. The definition would deny that the butterfly is the same organism as the caterpillar. Yet biologists affirm without hesitation that it is — a single individual that has undergone a developmental transformation governed by its genetic programme. What the background cases and the metamorphosis case together reveal is that the true bearer of persistence is the organising principle — form, function, or developmental programme — rather than outward appearance or material composition. The definition attends to the visible surface but not to the underlying structure that maintains identity through change.

background_cases: The proposition that no bachelor is married is necessarily true, and its necessity is recognisable through analysis of the concepts involved: to be a bachelor is, by definition, to be unmarried. The necessity is grounded in the conceptual relations between the terms.

The proposition that seven plus five equals twelve is necessarily true, and its necessity is recognisable through the application of arithmetical rules. The necessity is grounded in the structure of the number system.

The proposition that all triangles have interior angles summing to one hundred and eighty degrees (in Euclidean geometry) is necessarily true, and its necessity is demonstrable from the axioms and definitions of the geometrical system. The necessity is grounded in the formal structure of the theory.
definition: A proposition P is possibly true if and only if (i) P is not self-contradictory, (ii) P is conceivable — that is, one can form a coherent mental picture of a state of affairs in which P obtains, and (iii) P is not ruled out by any necessary truth.
conditions: P is not self-contradictory
P is conceivable
P is not ruled out by any necessary truth
counterexample: Consider the proposition that water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen but of some entirely different elements. This proposition is not self-contradictory in any obvious logical sense — there is no formal contradiction in the conjunction of the concepts. One can conceive, or at least seem to conceive, of a clear, potable liquid that is not H2O. And whether the proposition is ruled out by a necessary truth depends on a prior question about whether the composition of water is necessary — the very question at issue. All three conditions appear satisfied on a superficial reading. Yet many philosophers maintain that water is necessarily H2O, so that no possible circumstances could make the proposition true. The definition, relying on conceivability, cannot detect necessities that are not transparently conceptual.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that the necessary truths we most confidently recognise — the analyticity of 'no bachelor is married,' the arithmetic of seven plus five, the geometry of triangles — are necessary in virtue of conceptual or formal relations that are transparent to analysis. Their negations are not merely false but recognisably incoherent upon reflection. The proposition that water is not H2O differs: its apparent conceivability arises because the necessity of the composition of water is not grounded in transparent conceptual relations but in the nature of the substance itself, discoverable only through empirical investigation. The definition uses conceivability as a test for possibility, but the background cases show that conceivability is a reliable guide only for necessities grounded in conceptual or formal structure. When necessity is grounded in the nature of things rather than in concepts, conceivability may yield a false positive — we can seem to conceive of what is in fact impossible.
passage:
The necessary truths we recognise most readily share a common feature: their necessity is transparent to conceptual or formal analysis. That no bachelor is married is necessary because the concept of bachelorhood contains the concept of being unmarried. That seven plus five equals twelve is necessary because it follows from the rules of arithmetic. That triangles have interior angles summing to one hundred and eighty degrees is necessary because it follows from the axioms of Euclidean geometry. In each case, one recognises the necessity by attending to the relations between concepts or the structure of a formal system.

One might attempt to characterise possibility as the absence of self-contradiction, the presence of conceivability, and consistency with all necessary truths. This definition captures the idea that a proposition is possible if it involves no logical incoherence and can be coherently imagined. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with propositions concerning the nature of natural substances. Consider the proposition that water is not composed of hydrogen and oxygen but of some entirely different elements. The proposition is not self-contradictory in any formal sense — the words do not generate a logical inconsistency. One can form, or seem to form, a coherent mental image of a clear, potable liquid with a different composition. And whether the proposition is ruled out by a necessary truth depends on the very question the definition is meant to settle. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet many philosophers maintain that water is necessarily H2O — that in any genuinely possible circumstances, water has the composition it actually has. If this is correct, the proposition is impossible despite being conceivable. What the background cases reveal is that conceivability is a reliable guide to possibility only when necessity is grounded in conceptual or formal relations. When necessity is grounded in the nature of things — in what a substance is, rather than in what we mean by its name — conceivability may fail as a test, for we can seem to conceive of what is in fact impossible.
