background_cases: When a billiard ball strikes another and the second ball moves, we judge the striking to be the cause because the first ball's momentum is transferred to the second through direct physical contact; the causal relation is grounded in a continuous process of energy transfer from one object to the other.

When a spark ignites a trail of gunpowder, we judge the spark to be the cause of the explosion because there is a continuous process — the propagation of combustion along the powder trail — linking the spark to the detonation; the causal relation is sustained by an unbroken physical process.

When sunlight causes a plant to grow, we judge the light to be the cause because there is a continuous process of photosynthesis — the absorption of photons, the conversion of carbon dioxide and water into glucose — linking the light to the growth; the causal connection is mediated by an identifiable physical mechanism.
definition: An event C is a cause of an event E if and only if (i) C is temporally prior to E, (ii) C and E are connected by a law-like regularity, and (iii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C is temporally prior to E
C and E are connected by a law-like regularity
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Suppose a factory siren sounds at five o'clock each afternoon, and the workers leave the factory at five o'clock each afternoon. The siren is temporally prior to the departure. There is a law-like regularity connecting the sounding of the siren with the departure of the workers. And had the siren not sounded — suppose the mechanism failed — the workers, lacking their customary signal, would have remained at their benches. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the siren does not cause the workers to leave; it is the end of the working day that causes both the siren and the departure. The siren is a signal, not a cause.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the cause and effect be linked by a continuous physical process — a transfer of energy, a propagation of a mechanism — rather than by a mere regularity or counterfactual dependence that may reflect a common cause rather than a direct causal connection.
passage:
Reflection upon paradigmatic cases of causation reveals a striking common feature. When a billiard ball strikes another and sets it in motion, the causal relation is grounded in a continuous process of momentum transfer through direct physical contact. When a spark ignites a trail of gunpowder, the causal connection is sustained by the continuous propagation of combustion along the trail. When sunlight causes a plant to grow, an identifiable physical mechanism — photosynthesis — links the light to the growth. In each case, the cause and effect are connected not merely by a regularity or a counterfactual dependence but by a continuous physical process.

One might attempt to characterise causation by stipulating that an event C causes an event E if C is temporally prior to E, the two are connected by a law-like regularity, and E would not have occurred had C not occurred. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from industrial life. Suppose a factory siren sounds each afternoon at five o'clock, and at five o'clock the workers leave the factory. The siren is temporally prior to the departure. A law-like regularity connects them. And had the siren not sounded — suppose the mechanism failed one day — the workers, lacking their accustomed signal, would have remained at their benches. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the siren does not cause the workers to leave. It is the end of the working day — the time being five o'clock — that causes both the sounding of the siren and the departure of the workers. The siren and the departure are joint effects of a common cause. What the successful cases of causation share, and what the definition fails to require, is a continuous physical process linking cause to effect, rather than a mere regularity or counterfactual connection that may be sustained by a common cause.

background_cases: When we judge that the oak tree in the garden today is the same tree that was planted twenty years ago, our judgement rests not merely on similarity of appearance but on the fact that the tree has maintained a continuous biological life — the same living organism has persisted through growth and change, with no interruption in its vital processes.

When we judge that a river today is the same river that flowed through the valley a century ago, our judgement rests on the continuity of the watercourse — the same channel has carried water from the same source to the same mouth, despite the fact that not a single molecule of water has remained the same.

When we judge that a person in old age is the same person as the child in a photograph, our judgement rests on the continuity of a single biological and psychological life — an unbroken chain of memories, habits, and bodily continuity linking the child to the aged individual.
definition: An object X at time t1 is identical with an object Y at time t2 if and only if (i) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path from X at t1 to Y at t2, (ii) X and Y are of the same fundamental kind, and (iii) the properties of Y can be explained as the result of gradual changes from the properties of X.
conditions: There is a continuous spatiotemporal path from X at t1 to Y at t2
X and Y are of the same fundamental kind
The properties of Y can be explained as the result of gradual changes from the properties of X
counterexample: Suppose a caterpillar enters a chrysalis, within which its body is almost entirely dissolved into a biological soup, and from this soup a butterfly emerges. There is a continuous spatiotemporal path from caterpillar to butterfly — the chrysalis was never empty. The caterpillar and the butterfly are of the same fundamental kind — both are stages of a single species of lepidopteran. And the butterfly's properties can be explained as the result of the metamorphic process that gradually transformed the caterpillar's tissues. All three conditions are met. Yet the metamorphosis involves such radical discontinuity — the near-total dissolution of the original body — that one might reasonably doubt whether the butterfly is the same individual as the caterpillar.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the object's characteristic organisation or structure is preserved throughout the changes — the tree retains its living structure, the river retains its channel, the person retains continuity of memory and bodily form. The definition fails to require that the object's organising principle or characteristic structure persist through the changes, rather than being destroyed and reconstituted.
passage:
When we judge that an oak tree is the same tree planted twenty years ago, our confidence rests on the continuity of a single biological life — the same organism has grown and changed without interruption. When we identify a river as the same watercourse known to earlier generations, we rely on the persistence of the channel, despite the complete replacement of the water. When we recognise an aged person as the child in a yellowed photograph, we trace an unbroken chain of memories, habits, and bodily continuity. In each case, identity over time is grounded not merely in spatiotemporal continuity but in the persistence of a characteristic organising principle — the life of the organism, the form of the channel, the continuity of the psychological life.

One might attempt to capture this by defining identity across time in terms of spatiotemporal continuity, sameness of fundamental kind, and the explicability of later properties as gradual changes from earlier ones. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the natural history of insects. Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis. Within the chrysalis, the caterpillar's body is very nearly dissolved — its tissues reduced to an undifferentiated biological substrate. From this substrate, a butterfly is assembled. There is a continuous spatiotemporal path from caterpillar to butterfly; the chrysalis was never vacant. Both are stages of the same species — the same fundamental kind. And the butterfly's properties can be explained as the outcome of a gradual metamorphic process. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the transformation involves such radical discontinuity — the effective destruction and reconstruction of the body — that one might reasonably doubt whether the butterfly is the same individual as the caterpillar. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the characteristic organising structure of the object persist through the changes, rather than being dissolved and reconstituted from its materials.

background_cases: When we regard a lump of gold as a substance, our judgement rests on the fact that gold has intrinsic causal powers — it conducts electricity, resists corrosion, reflects light of a characteristic colour — that are grounded in its atomic structure and that it exercises independently of any observer or convention.

When we regard a living horse as a substance, our judgement rests on the fact that the horse has intrinsic causal powers — it metabolises food, moves under its own power, reproduces — that are grounded in its biological organisation and that it exercises in its own right.

When we regard a drop of water as a substance, our judgement rests on the fact that it has intrinsic causal powers — it dissolves salts, freezes at a determinate temperature, refracts light — grounded in its molecular composition.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X is capable of independent existence, (ii) X bears properties, and (iii) X persists through changes in at least some of its properties.
conditions: X is capable of independent existence
X bears properties
X persists through changes in at least some of its properties
counterexample: Consider a particular whirlpool in a river. The whirlpool exists independently of any observer. It bears properties — a location, a diameter, a rate of spin, a depth. And it persists through changes in at least some of its properties: it may grow wider or narrower, speed up or slow down, shift slightly downstream, whilst remaining recognisably the same whirlpool. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet we should hesitate to call a whirlpool a substance. It is a pattern in the flow of water — a dynamic structure maintained by the river's current — possessing no intrinsic causal powers of its own apart from the water that constitutes it.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each substance possesses intrinsic causal powers grounded in its own constitution — powers it exercises in its own right, not merely by virtue of the activity of something else flowing through it or sustaining it. The definition fails to require that the entity's properties and persistence be grounded in its own intrinsic causal nature.
passage:
When we consider paradigmatic substances — a lump of gold, a living horse, a drop of water — a common feature emerges. Each possesses intrinsic causal powers grounded in its own constitution. Gold conducts electricity and resists corrosion by virtue of its atomic structure. A horse metabolises food and moves under its own power by virtue of its biological organisation. Water dissolves salts and freezes at a determinate temperature by virtue of its molecular composition. In each case, the substance exercises its characteristic powers in its own right.

One might attempt to capture the notion of substance by stipulating that an entity is a substance if it is capable of independent existence, bears properties, and persists through changes in at least some of those properties. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the observation of rivers. Consider a whirlpool formed where a river passes over a submerged rock. The whirlpool exists independently of any observer. It bears properties — a position, a diameter, a rate of rotation. And it persists through changes in its properties: it may grow wider as the river rises, narrow as it falls, and shift its position slightly as the current varies. All three conditions appear satisfied. Yet one might observe that a whirlpool is not a substance. It is a pattern — a dynamic structure maintained by the continuous flow of water through a particular configuration of the riverbed. It possesses no intrinsic causal powers of its own; its every property depends upon the activity of the water passing through it. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that a substance's properties and persistence be grounded in its own intrinsic causal nature, rather than in the activity of something else that flows through it or sustains it from without.

background_cases: When an iron bar is heated and expands, the change is genuine because the bar's intrinsic spatial dimensions — its length, its volume — are altered by the absorption of thermal energy; the bar itself is different after the heating in a way that can be detected without reference to any other object.

When a green leaf turns brown in autumn, the change is genuine because the leaf's intrinsic chemical composition is altered — chlorophyll degrades, revealing other pigments — and this alteration can be detected by examining the leaf alone, without reference to any other entity.

When a candle burns and diminishes in size, the change is genuine because the wax is being consumed by combustion, and the candle's mass and dimensions are intrinsically different from what they were before; the change is detectable in the candle itself.
definition: An object X has undergone a change if and only if (i) there is a property F such that X possesses F at one time and lacks F at a later time, (ii) X exists at both times, and (iii) the earlier and later states of X can be compared by a competent observer.
conditions: There is a property F such that X possesses F at one time and lacks F at a later time
X exists at both times
The earlier and later states of X can be compared by a competent observer
counterexample: Suppose a particular stone lies undisturbed on a hillside for a century. During this time, a new road is built nearby, and the stone acquires the relational property of being within one hundred metres of a road. There is a property — being within one hundred metres of a road — that the stone lacked and now possesses. The stone exists at both times. And the earlier and later states can be compared. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the stone has not changed in any genuine sense; it has remained utterly inert. The road has changed the landscape around the stone, but the stone itself is unaltered.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the property gained or lost is intrinsic to the object — it characterises the object's own constitution, dimensions, or composition, and can be detected by examining the object in isolation. The definition fails to distinguish intrinsic change from the mere acquisition or loss of relational properties brought about by changes in other things.
passage:
When we consider clear cases of genuine change, a pattern emerges. The heated iron bar has changed because its intrinsic spatial dimensions have been altered by the absorption of energy. The autumn leaf has changed because its chemical composition has been transformed by the degradation of chlorophyll. The burning candle has changed because its mass has diminished through combustion. In each case, the change is intrinsic — it characterises the object's own constitution and can be detected by examining the object alone.

One might attempt to characterise change by stipulating that an object has undergone a change if there is a property it possessed at one time and lacks at a later time, if it exists at both times, and if the two states can be compared by a competent observer. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed by considering a stone lying undisturbed on a hillside. Over the course of a century, a road is built nearby, and the stone acquires the relational property of being within one hundred metres of a road — a property it previously lacked. The stone exists at both times. A competent observer can compare the two states. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the stone has not genuinely changed. It has lain inert upon the hillside throughout; it is the landscape around it that has been altered. What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to require, is that the property in question be intrinsic to the object — that it characterise the object's own constitution rather than its relations to other things. The definition, by admitting relational properties alongside intrinsic ones, cannot distinguish genuine change from the mere acquisition of new relations brought about by alterations in the surrounding world.

background_cases: When a billiard ball strikes another and the second ball moves, the collision causes the motion: there is a transfer of energy from the first ball to the second, and the motion of the second ball is produced by the impact of the first. The causal relation involves a physical process connecting cause and effect.

When a spark ignites a pool of petrol and a fire results, the spark causes the fire: there is a physical process — the rapid oxidation of hydrocarbons initiated by the thermal energy of the spark — that connects the cause to the effect. The causal relation is mediated by a continuous physical mechanism.

When a hammer strikes a pane of glass and the glass shatters, the blow causes the shattering: the force transmitted through the hammer head exceeds the structural integrity of the glass, and the causal relation is constituted by this physical interaction.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) there is a continuous physical process connecting C to E, and (iii) C transfers energy or momentum to E.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
There is a continuous physical process connecting C to E
C transfers energy or momentum to E
counterexample: Consider a case of causation by omission: a gardener neglects to water her plants, and the plants die. The gardener's omission causes the death of the plants — this is the natural way of describing the situation, and the gardener may be held responsible. Yet there is no continuous physical process connecting the omission to the death; an omission is not an event that transfers energy or momentum. The failure to act is not a physical process at all.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that causation operates through a positive physical mechanism — a transfer of energy or momentum via a continuous process. But the definition mistakes this feature of mechanical causation for a universal requirement, overlooking causation by absence, omission, and prevention, in which the causal relation holds precisely because no physical process occurs.
passage:
The billiard ball collision, the spark igniting petrol, and the hammer shattering glass are paradigmatic cases of causation in which a physical process connects cause to effect through a transfer of energy or momentum. These cases suggest a general account: causation consists in temporal precedence, physical continuity, and the transfer of energy or momentum.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this mechanistic account may be drawn from the phenomenon of causation by omission. Suppose a gardener neglects to water her plants during a period of drought, and the plants die. We naturally say that the gardener's neglect caused the death of the plants. She may be held responsible by her employer, and the causal attribution is not merely figurative. Yet there is no continuous physical process connecting the omission to the death. An omission is not an event; it is the absence of an event. No energy or momentum is transferred from the gardener's inaction to the wilting stems. The death occurs precisely because nothing happens — because the physical process of watering, which would have sustained the plants, does not take place. It appears that the background cases, being instances of mechanical causation, share the feature of energy transfer, and that the definition has mistaken this feature for a universal condition of causation. Causation by absence and omission, one might observe, is a genuine species of causation that operates not through the presence of a physical mechanism but through its absence.

background_cases: A gold ring melted down and recast into a gold bar is regarded as involving the same gold — the material persists through the change of form. The identity of the gold is secured by the continuity of its material substance, not by the preservation of its shape.

A block of marble sculpted into a statue is regarded as involving the same marble — the marble persists through the sculptor's work. The identity of the material is secured by its physical continuity, even though its form is radically altered.

A river whose course shifts gradually over centuries — depositing silt here, eroding banks there — is regarded as the same river. Its identity is secured by the spatiotemporal continuity of its flowing water, even though the precise water molecules change constantly.
definition: An entity X at time t₁ is identical with an entity Y at time t₂ if and only if (i) X and Y are spatiotemporally continuous, (ii) X and Y share the same material substrate, and (iii) the kind to which X belongs at t₁ is the same as the kind to which Y belongs at t₂.
conditions: X and Y are spatiotemporally continuous
X and Y share the same material substrate
The kind to which X belongs at t₁ is the same as the kind to which Y belongs at t₂
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that undergoes metamorphosis and becomes a butterfly. The organism is spatiotemporally continuous — there is an unbroken biological path from caterpillar to chrysalis to butterfly. The material substrate is largely continuous, though substantially reorganised. But the kind to which it belongs changes: it begins as a larva — a crawling, wingless, leaf-eating creature — and ends as an imago — a winged, nectar-drinking adult. The third condition is violated if kinds are defined by characteristic form and behaviour. Yet biologists regard the caterpillar and the butterfly as the same individual organism.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the kind-membership of the entity does not change — gold remains gold, marble remains marble, a river remains a river. The definition generalises this stability of kind into a necessary condition, but identity can persist through changes of kind when what secures identity is biological or organisational continuity rather than fixed kind-membership.
passage:
The gold ring melted into a bar, the marble block carved into a statue, and the river whose course shifts over centuries all preserve identity through material and spatiotemporal continuity while maintaining their membership in the same natural kind — gold remains gold, marble remains marble, the river remains a river. These cases suggest that identity requires spatiotemporal continuity, material continuity, and stability of kind.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of the kind-stability condition may be drawn from the life cycle of holometabolous insects. Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and emerges as a butterfly. The organism is spatiotemporally continuous — there is an unbroken biological path connecting the larval stage to the adult stage, passing through a pupal phase during which much of the organism's structure is dissolved and rebuilt. The material substrate is largely continuous, though substantially reorganised at the cellular level. But the kind to which the organism belongs changes dramatically: the caterpillar is a crawling, wingless, leaf-eating larva; the butterfly is a winged, nectar-drinking imago. If kinds are defined by characteristic form, behaviour, and mode of life, then the organism changes kind. The third condition is violated. Yet biologists regard the caterpillar and the butterfly as the same individual organism. One might observe that the background cases are cases in which identity coincides with kind-stability, and that the definition has mistaken this coincidence for a necessary connection. Identity, in the biological case, is secured by organisational continuity rather than by fixed kind-membership.

background_cases: The duration of a pendulum's swing provides a measure of time that is uniform and repeatable — each swing takes the same interval, and the regularity of the process allows us to mark off equal temporal intervals. Time, in this case, is measured by the regular recurrence of a physical process.

The rotation of the earth upon its axis provides a measure of time — the day — that is defined by the regular recurrence of a physical process. One complete rotation constitutes one day, and the uniformity of the process underwrites the reliability of the measure.

The vibration of a quartz crystal at a determinate frequency provides a measure of time of extraordinary precision. The regularity of the vibrations allows the construction of clocks that keep time to within fractions of a second per year.
definition: Time is measurable if and only if (i) there exists a regular, periodic physical process that can serve as a standard, (ii) the process is uniform — each period is of equal duration, and (iii) the standard process is independent of the events being timed.
conditions: There exists a regular, periodic physical process that can serve as a standard
The process is uniform — each period is of equal duration
The standard process is independent of the events being timed
counterexample: Consider the measurement of time in the very early universe, fractions of a second after the initial singularity. At this epoch, no regular periodic physical process had yet been established — no pendulums, no planetary rotations, no stable atomic vibrations. The physical conditions were too extreme for any periodic process to occur. Yet physicists meaningfully assign temporal coordinates to events in this epoch and make precise claims about their duration. Time is measured not by periodic processes but by theoretical models and the equations of cosmology.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that time-measurement proceeds by comparison with a regular physical process, but the definition generalises from the availability of such processes in ordinary circumstances to a universal requirement, overlooking the fact that the measurability of time is ultimately grounded in the structure of physical theory rather than in the contingent availability of periodic processes.
passage:
The pendulum, the rotating earth, and the vibrating quartz crystal each provide a measure of time through the regular recurrence of a physical process. These cases suggest a general account: time is measurable when there exists a uniform, periodic, and independent physical process that can serve as a standard of temporal comparison.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from cosmology. Consider the measurement of time in the very early universe — the epoch lasting fractions of a second after the initial singularity, during which the temperature and density of matter were so extreme that no stable atomic structure existed, no periodic process could be sustained, and no clock of any kind could function. No pendulum could swing; no crystal could vibrate; no planet could rotate. The conditions required by all three of the definition's stipulations were absent. Yet physicists meaningfully assign precise temporal coordinates to events in this epoch and make quantitative claims about the duration of physical processes — the formation of elementary particles, the decoupling of fundamental forces. Time is measured in this context not by comparison with a periodic process but by the equations of physical theory, which assign temporal structure to the universe independently of the availability of any physical clock. It appears that the background cases — drawn from ordinary experience where periodic processes are plentifully available — have led to a definition that confuses the practical instruments of time-measurement with the underlying measurability of time itself.

background_cases: A stone that sits undisturbed in a field for a century persists through time by retaining all of its intrinsic properties — its mass, its mineral composition, its shape — unchanged. The stone at the end of the century is the same stone as the stone at the beginning because nothing about it has altered.

A diamond that passes from one owner to another over decades persists through time because its intrinsic properties — its crystalline structure, its hardness, its optical qualities — remain unchanged. The diamond's identity is secured by its unchanging physical nature.

A bronze statue that stands in a public square for generations persists because its material composition and form remain stable. The statue at the end of two centuries is the same statue as the statue at the beginning because its constitutive properties are preserved.
definition: An object O persists from time t₁ to time t₂ if and only if (i) O retains all of its essential intrinsic properties from t₁ to t₂, (ii) O occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region from t₁ to t₂, and (iii) no process of disassembly and reassembly interrupts O's existence between t₁ and t₂.
conditions: O retains all of its essential intrinsic properties from t₁ to t₂
O occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region from t₁ to t₂
No process of disassembly and reassembly interrupts O's existence between t₁ and t₂
counterexample: Consider a living oak tree. Over the course of a century, it replaces every cell in its body many times over through the processes of growth, metabolism, and repair. Its intrinsic properties change continuously: it grows taller, its trunk thickens, its canopy expands, its bark roughens. At the cellular level, a continuous process of disassembly and reassembly — the breakdown and replacement of cellular components — occurs throughout its life. All three conditions are at least arguably violated. Yet the oak tree persists — it is the same tree at the end of the century as at the beginning.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that they are inert objects whose persistence coincides with the retention of intrinsic properties and the absence of internal change. The definition generalises from these static cases, overlooking the fact that living things persist precisely through processes of continuous internal change, replacement, and reorganisation — processes that the definition treats as threats to persistence but that are in fact constitutive of it.
passage:
The stone in the field, the diamond passed from hand to hand, and the bronze statue standing in the public square each persist through time by retaining their intrinsic properties, occupying a continuous spatiotemporal region, and undergoing no process of disassembly and reassembly. These cases suggest a definition of persistence: an object persists when its essential properties are preserved, its spatiotemporal path is unbroken, and its existence is not interrupted by dissolution and reconstruction.

Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this account may be drawn from the biological world. Consider a living oak tree observed over the course of a century. During this period, the tree replaces every cell in its body many times over through the continuous processes of growth, metabolism, and cellular repair. Its intrinsic properties change without cease: it grows taller, its trunk thickens, its canopy expands and contracts with the seasons, its bark roughens, its root system spreads. At the cellular level, a continuous process of disassembly and reassembly takes place — the breakdown and replacement of cellular components is not an interruption of the tree's existence but the very mechanism by which it continues to exist. All three conditions are at least arguably violated, yet the oak tree persists. It is the same tree at the end of the century as at the beginning. One might observe that the background cases are all inert objects whose persistence happens to coincide with the retention of intrinsic properties, and that the definition has mistaken this coincidence for a universal requirement, overlooking the fact that living things persist through change rather than despite it.

background_cases: When Aristotle considered the bronze of a statue, he recognised that whilst the bronze persists through the sculptor's work and serves as the subject of various predicable forms, its substantiality derives not merely from persistence but from its capacity to exist as a unified, self-subsistent particular — the bronze is not reducible to a collection of qualities but possesses an intrinsic nature that grounds the qualities it bears.

When Leibniz reflected upon the nature of monads, he insisted that a genuine substance must possess an internal principle of activity — a source of change from within — such that its persistence through alteration is not passive endurance but the expression of an intrinsic dynamic nature that belongs to the substance itself.

When Spinoza argued that substance must be conceived through itself and not through anything else, the force of this requirement lay in the recognition that a genuine substance cannot be constituted by, or dependent upon, the arrangement of external elements; its existence must be grounded in its own nature rather than in the organisation of parts external to it.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X persists through change, (ii) X is the subject of predication for its properties, and (iii) X is not dependent upon any other particular entity for its existence.
conditions: X persists through change
X is the subject of predication for its properties
X is not dependent upon any other particular entity for its existence
counterexample: Consider a standing wave in a vibrating string — the pattern that remains stationary while the string oscillates about it. The wave persists through the continuous change of the string's motion, maintaining its position and form while the underlying medium fluctuates. It is the subject of predication: we ascribe to it a determinate frequency, amplitude, and wavelength. And it does not depend upon any other particular entity — no other particular wave or object is required for its existence. All three conditions are met. Yet the standing wave is not a substance; it is a pattern constituted entirely by the motions of the string, possessing no intrinsic nature independent of the medium through which it is expressed.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share, and what the definition fails to capture, is the requirement that a genuine substance possess an intrinsic constitutive nature — that its persistence and its capacity to bear properties be grounded in what it is in itself, rather than in the arrangement or behaviour of elements external to it.
passage:
The question of what constitutes a substance has occupied metaphysicians since the earliest systematic inquiries into the nature of being. When Aristotle considered the bronze of a statue, he recognised that its claim to substantiality rested not merely upon its persistence through the sculptor's transformative work but upon its capacity to exist as a unified, self-subsistent particular possessing an intrinsic nature that grounds the qualities predicated of it. When Leibniz reflected upon monads, he insisted that genuine substances must harbour an internal principle of activity, a source of change arising from within, so that their persistence is not mere passive endurance but the expression of a dynamic inner nature. When Spinoza maintained that substance must be conceived through itself, the force of his requirement lay in the recognition that a genuine substance cannot be constituted by or dependent upon the arrangement of elements external to it.

These cases invite a general characterisation: a substance is an entity that persists through change, serves as the subject of predication for its properties, and is not dependent upon any other particular for its existence. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the physics of vibrating media. Consider a standing wave in a taut string — the pattern that remains fixed in position while the string oscillates about it. The standing wave persists through continuous change, maintaining its form and location while the underlying medium fluctuates. It serves as the subject of predication: we attribute to it a determinate frequency, a particular amplitude, and a specific wavelength. And it does not depend upon any other particular entity for its existence — no other particular wave or object is required. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the standing wave is not a substance in any robust sense. It is a pattern constituted entirely by the motions of the string, possessing no intrinsic nature that could survive the removal of its medium. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that a genuine substance must possess an intrinsic constitutive nature grounding its persistence and its capacity to bear properties, rather than being a mere pattern emergent from the behaviour of elements that are themselves more fundamental.

background_cases: When one billiard ball strikes another and the second begins to move, the causal connexion is manifest not merely in the temporal succession of the two events but in the transfer of momentum from the first ball to the second — there is a physical quantity that passes from cause to effect, and it is this transfer that constitutes the causal relation rather than the mere regularity of the sequence.

When a spark ignites a pool of petrol, the causal relation consists not merely in the regular succession of sparks and fires but in the physical process by which the thermal energy of the spark raises the temperature of the petrol vapour above its ignition point, initiating a chain of exothermic chemical reactions — the cause produces the effect through a determinate physical mechanism.

When the moon's gravitational pull raises the tides, the causation is constituted not by the mere correlation between lunar position and water level but by the continuous gravitational interaction between the moon and the earth's oceans — a physical process that connects cause and effect through the transmission of force across space.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) there is a law-like regularity connecting events of the type of C with events of the type of E, and (iii) C is a necessary condition for E — that is, had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
There is a law-like regularity connecting events of the type of C with events of the type of E
C is a necessary condition for E — had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Consider a case in which a man strikes a match and the match lights. The striking precedes the lighting; there is a law-like regularity connecting matchstrikings and lightings; and had the man not struck the match, it would not have lit. All three conditions are satisfied, and indeed we rightly call the striking the cause. But now consider a variant: two men, A and B, simultaneously strike two separate matches at opposite ends of a trail of gunpowder, and the trail ignites from A's end a fraction of a second before B's flame could have reached it. The explosion is caused by A's match. Yet B's match also satisfies all three conditions with respect to the explosion — it precedes the explosion, there is a law-like regularity, and had B not struck his match, the explosion would still have occurred (since A's match sufficed). Thus B's striking is not a necessary condition, and the definition correctly excludes it. But now alter the case so that both flames reach the powder simultaneously and either alone would have caused the explosion. Neither is a necessary condition, for had either not occurred, the other would have sufficed. The definition entails that neither caused the explosion, which is absurd.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that causation involves the transmission of some physical quantity or the operation of a determinate mechanism connecting cause and effect. The definition, by relying upon counterfactual necessity, cannot accommodate cases of causal overdetermination in which two independent processes each suffice to produce the effect, rendering neither individually necessary.
passage:
Reflection upon paradigmatic instances of causation reveals a recurring structural feature that the most familiar analyses tend to overlook. When one billiard ball strikes another, the causal relation consists in the transfer of momentum — a physical quantity that passes from the first ball to the second. When a spark ignites a pool of petrol, the causation is constituted by the physical process through which thermal energy raises the vapour above its ignition point. When the moon raises the tides, the causal connexion is the continuous gravitational interaction between the lunar mass and the earth's oceans.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an event C causes an event E when C precedes E, there is a law-like regularity linking the two, and C is a necessary condition for E in the sense that, had C not occurred, E would not have occurred. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed by considering overdetermination. Suppose two men, A and B, each strike a match and simultaneously set fire to opposite ends of a trail of gunpowder. Both flames reach the powder at the same instant, and the trail explodes. Either flame alone would have sufficed to produce the explosion. It follows that neither is a necessary condition for the explosion, since had either failed to act, the other's flame would have produced the very same result. The definition, applied to this case, entails that neither man caused the explosion — a conclusion that is plainly absurd. What the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that causation consists in the operation of a physical mechanism or the transmission of a quantity from cause to effect, a relation that obtains even in cases of overdetermination where counterfactual necessity fails.

background_cases: When we recognise that the interior angles of a triangle sum to two right angles, the necessity of this truth is grounded not in our psychological inability to imagine otherwise but in the geometrical structure of Euclidean space — the theorem holds because of the nature of the objects involved, not because of any limitation in our powers of conception.

When we affirm that no bachelor is married, the necessity derives from the meaning of the term 'bachelor' — the proposition could not be false because the concept of bachelorhood includes unmarried status as a constitutive element, and the necessity is thus anchored in the semantic structure of the concepts involved.

When we hold that nothing can be simultaneously red all over and green all over, the impossibility is grounded in the natures of the colours themselves — in an exclusion relation that belongs to the properties red and green and would obtain regardless of whether any mind were present to contemplate it.
definition: A proposition P is necessary if and only if (i) P is true, (ii) the negation of P is inconceivable by any rational agent, and (iii) P holds in every circumstance that can be coherently described.
conditions: P is true
The negation of P is inconceivable by any rational agent
P holds in every circumstance that can be coherently described
counterexample: Consider the proposition that every continuous function on a closed interval is bounded. This is a theorem of real analysis — it is true. Its negation is, for any mathematician who understands the relevant concepts, inconceivable in the sense that no coherent scenario can be described in which a continuous function on a closed interval fails to be bounded. And it holds in every circumstance that can be coherently described within the framework of standard analysis. All three conditions are met. Yet the proposition fails to hold in non-standard frameworks — in constructive analysis, for instance, the theorem requires additional assumptions. Whether the proposition is genuinely necessary or merely a consequence of a particular mathematical framework is a substantive question that the definition, by appealing to conceivability and coherent description, cannot adjudicate.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the source of necessity lies in the nature of the objects or concepts involved — in the structure of space, the meaning of terms, or the intrinsic character of properties — rather than in the limits of what can be conceived or coherently described. The definition, by grounding necessity in conceivability and coherence, conflates the subjective inability to imagine otherwise with the objective ground of necessary truth.
passage:
The most compelling instances of necessary truth share a feature that invites careful analysis. When we recognise that the angles of a Euclidean triangle sum to two right angles, the necessity is grounded in the structure of Euclidean space itself. When we affirm that no bachelor is married, the necessity derives from the constitutive meaning of the term. When we hold that nothing can be simultaneously red all over and green all over, the impossibility is anchored in the natures of the colours themselves, in an exclusion relation that would obtain whether or not any mind were present to contemplate it.

One might attempt to capture this by defining necessity as truth whose negation is inconceivable and which holds in every circumstance that can be coherently described. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the foundations of mathematics. Consider the proposition that every continuous function on a closed interval is bounded — a standard theorem of real analysis. The proposition is true. Its negation is, for any mathematician who grasps the relevant concepts, inconceivable in the relevant sense: no coherent scenario within standard analysis can be described in which a continuous function on a closed interval fails to be bounded. And the proposition appears to hold in every circumstance that can be coherently described. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet the theorem rests upon specific assumptions about the structure of the real numbers and the completeness of the continuum; in alternative mathematical frameworks — constructive analysis, for example — the theorem requires additional hypotheses and does not hold in its standard form. Whether the proposition is genuinely necessary or merely a consequence of a particular framework is a substantive philosophical question. One might observe that what the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to require — is that genuine necessity be grounded in the nature of the objects or concepts involved, rather than in the limits of what rational agents happen to be able to conceive or describe.

background_cases: When a river persists through the continuous flow of its waters, the persistence is grounded in the maintenance of a stable causal structure — the banks channel the water, the source feeds the stream, and the system as a whole sustains itself through continuous causal processes that preserve its characteristic form and function despite the constant replacement of its material constituents.

When a biological organism persists through the replacement of its cells over the course of years, the persistence derives from the continuous operation of the metabolic and developmental processes that maintain the organism's structure — a living causal organisation that sustains itself through the assimilation of new material and the repair or replacement of old.

When a flame persists upon a candle, its persistence consists in the continuous process of combustion — the sustained causal activity by which wax is drawn up, vaporised, and oxidised, generating light and heat. The matter of the flame is constantly changing, but the causal process that constitutes the flame is continuous.
definition: An object O persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) O exists at t1 and at t2, (ii) O retains its essential properties throughout the interval, and (iii) there is no time between t1 and t2 at which O ceases to exist.
conditions: O exists at t1 and at t2
O retains its essential properties throughout the interval
There is no time between t1 and t2 at which O ceases to exist
counterexample: Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis, emerging as a butterfly. The organism exists before and after the transformation, and at no point during the process does it cease to exist — the pupa is a continuous stage in the life of a single organism. Yet the caterpillar does not retain its essential properties throughout: its body plan, its mode of locomotion, its sensory apparatus, and its reproductive capacity are all radically altered. One might say that virtually every property that characterised the caterpillar as the kind of thing it was has been replaced. The second condition is not met, yet the butterfly is the same organism that was once the caterpillar.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that persistence is constituted by the continuity of a sustaining causal process or organisation, rather than by the retention of any particular set of properties. The definition, by requiring that essential properties be retained, cannot accommodate cases in which an entity persists through radical transformation by virtue of a continuous causal process that links its earlier and later stages.
passage:
The question of what it is for an object to persist through time has generated a rich body of philosophical analysis. When a river persists through the continuous flow of its waters, the persistence is grounded in the maintenance of a stable causal structure — the banks, the source, and the drainage system sustain the river's characteristic form despite the constant replacement of its material constituents. When a biological organism persists through the gradual replacement of its cells, the continuity derives from the uninterrupted operation of metabolic and developmental processes that maintain the organism's structure. When a candle flame persists, its identity over time consists in the continuous process of combustion — the sustained causal activity by which wax is consumed, vaporised, and oxidised.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an object persists when it exists throughout an interval, retains its essential properties, and never ceases to exist during that interval. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be drawn from the natural history of insects. Consider a caterpillar that enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis. At no point during the process does the organism cease to exist — the pupa is a continuous stage in the life of a single biological individual. Yet the caterpillar does not retain its essential properties: its body plan is dissolved and reconstituted, its mode of locomotion changes entirely, its sensory apparatus is transformed, and it acquires reproductive capacities it previously lacked. Virtually every property that characterised the caterpillar as the particular kind of entity it was has been replaced. The second condition of the definition is not met. Yet the butterfly is the same organism that was once the caterpillar — a fact confirmed by genetic continuity and the unbroken developmental process linking the two stages. One might observe that what the background cases reveal — and what the definition fails to capture — is that persistence is constituted by the continuity of a sustaining causal process or organisation, rather than by the retention of any fixed set of essential properties.

background_cases: When a stone is thrown at a window and the window shatters, we identify the throwing as the cause of the shattering because the throwing is a distinct event that precedes the shattering, is spatiotemporally contiguous with it, and is such that, had the throwing not occurred, the window would have remained intact. The causal relation here appears to be a matter of one event necessitating another in the absence of which the second would not have occurred.

When a spark ignites a pool of petrol and a fire results, we identify the spark as the cause because it is a distinct, prior event contiguous with the ignition, and because, had the spark not occurred under those conditions, the fire would not have arisen. Again, the causal nexus is constituted by the counterfactual dependence of the effect upon the cause.

When a surgeon's incision causes bleeding in a patient, we identify the incision as the cause because it is a temporally prior, spatiotemporally contiguous event such that, had it not been made, the bleeding would not have occurred. The relation between cause and effect is once more captured by the counterfactual conditional linking the two events.
definition: An event C is the cause of an event E if and only if (i) C and E are distinct events, (ii) C temporally precedes E and is spatiotemporally contiguous with it, and (iii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C and E are distinct events
C temporally precedes E and is spatiotemporally contiguous with it
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Suppose two snipers, stationed at different positions and acting independently, fire at a soldier at precisely the same instant. Both bullets strike the soldier simultaneously, and either bullet alone would have been lethal. Consider the first sniper's shot as C and the soldier's death as E. C and E are distinct events. C temporally precedes E and is spatiotemporally contiguous with it. Yet it is not the case that had C not occurred, E would not have occurred, since the second bullet would have killed the soldier regardless. The counterfactual condition is not met, and yet the first shot is plainly a cause of the death.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that each involves a single sufficient cause whose removal would eliminate the effect. The inferred definition captures this pattern faithfully. Yet the case of simultaneous overdetermination reveals that causation can obtain even when the counterfactual dependence of effect upon cause is absent, because multiple independently sufficient causes converge. The generalisation from the background cases breaks down precisely where the assumption of a unique sufficient cause fails.
passage:
Reflection upon the most familiar instances of causation suggests a common structure. When a stone shatters a window, we identify the throwing as the cause because, had the stone not been thrown, the window would have remained intact. When a spark ignites petrol, we take the spark as the cause because, absent its occurrence, no fire would have arisen. When a surgeon's incision produces bleeding, we identify the incision as the cause because the bleeding would not have occurred without it. In each case, the causal relation appears to be constituted by the counterfactual dependence of the effect upon a distinct, temporally prior, spatiotemporally contiguous event.

These cases invite a general characterisation: an event C causes an event E just when C and E are distinct, C precedes E and is contiguous with it, and E would not have occurred had C not occurred. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the domain of simultaneous overdetermination. Suppose two snipers, stationed at different positions and acting independently of one another, fire at a soldier at precisely the same instant. Both bullets strike the soldier simultaneously, and either alone would have been lethal. Take the first sniper's shot as the candidate cause and the soldier's death as the effect. The shot and the death are distinct events. The shot precedes the death and is contiguous with it. Yet had the first shot not been fired, the soldier would still have died from the second bullet. The counterfactual condition is not satisfied. Nevertheless, one might observe that we cannot reasonably deny that the first shot was a cause of the death. What the background cases share — and what the definition successfully captures — is a structure in which a single sufficient cause is operative and its removal would eliminate the effect. The generalisation breaks down where this assumption fails: when two independently sufficient causes converge, counterfactual dependence evaporates even as causation plainly obtains. The limits of inductive generalisation are reached at precisely the point where the background cases, each involving a unique sufficient cause, give way to configurations of causal redundancy.

background_cases: The proposition that every bachelor is unmarried is necessarily true: its truth is secured by the meanings of the terms involved, and no possible state of affairs could render it false. The negation of this proposition — that some bachelor is married — is not merely false but incoherent, for it contradicts the very definition of bachelorhood.

The proposition that nothing is both entirely red and entirely green at the same time is necessarily true: its truth is grounded in the incompatibility of the colour predicates, and one cannot coherently conceive of a surface that is wholly red and wholly green simultaneously. The negation is excluded not by empirical investigation but by the structure of our colour concepts.

The proposition that two plus three equals five is necessarily true: no rearrangement of circumstances could render it false, and its negation is not merely improbable but unintelligible. Mathematical truths of this kind hold with a necessity that transcends any particular state of the physical world.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) P is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms, (ii) the negation of P is unintelligible or self-contradictory, and (iii) P can be known to be true independently of empirical investigation.
conditions: P is true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms
The negation of P is unintelligible or self-contradictory
P can be known to be true independently of empirical investigation
counterexample: Consider the proposition that gold has the atomic number 79. Many philosophers have argued that this proposition is necessarily true — that in any possible state of affairs, if a substance is gold, then it has atomic number 79, for the atomic number is constitutive of what gold is. Yet this proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms, for the meaning of 'gold' in ordinary language does not include a specification of atomic number. Its negation — that gold does not have atomic number 79 — is perfectly intelligible, even if ultimately false. And the proposition cannot be known independently of empirical investigation; it was discovered through chemical analysis. None of the three conditions is satisfied, and yet the proposition may be necessarily true.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the necessity of each proposition is transparent to reason: the meanings of the terms, the self-contradictoriness of the negation, and the independence from empirical inquiry all converge. The definition captures this pattern well. Yet the case of necessary a posteriori truths — propositions whose necessity is real but discoverable only through empirical investigation — reveals that the background cases represent only one species of necessity. The inductive generalisation from conceptual and mathematical truths to a universal account of necessary truth breaks down when we encounter necessities that are grounded not in meaning but in the nature of things.

Reflection upon the most perspicuous instances of necessary truth suggests a common pattern. The proposition that every bachelor is unmarried owes its necessity to the meanings of its terms; its negation is self-contradictory; and its truth is known independently of experience. The proposition that nothing is both wholly red and wholly green simultaneously is necessary in virtue of the structure of our colour concepts, and its negation is unintelligible. The proposition that two and three make five holds with a necessity that no rearrangement of the physical world could disturb, and it is known a priori.

These cases invite the generalisation that a necessarily true proposition is one that is true in virtue of meanings, whose negation is self-contradictory, and that is knowable without empirical inquiry. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this characterisation may be drawn from the domain of natural kinds. Consider the proposition that gold has the atomic number 79. There are strong reasons to hold that this proposition is necessarily true — that in any possible state of affairs, a substance that is gold has atomic number 79, for the atomic number is constitutive of what it is to be gold. Yet this proposition is not true in virtue of the meanings of its terms: the ordinary meaning of 'gold' involves a yellow, malleable metal, not a specification of atomic structure. Its negation is perfectly intelligible; one can coherently entertain the thought that gold might have had a different atomic number. And the proposition was discovered through empirical chemical analysis, not through a priori reflection. None of the three stated conditions is satisfied. One might observe that the background cases, however instructive, represent only one species of necessary truth — the species grounded in meaning and transparent to reason. The generalisation from these cases to a universal account of necessity breaks down when we encounter propositions whose necessity is grounded in the nature of things rather than in the structure of our concepts.

background_cases: A bronze statue stands in a public square for two hundred years, exposed to weather, touched by thousands of hands, and gradually developing a green patina. Throughout this time it remains the same statue: it occupies a continuous spatiotemporal path, retains the same material constitution (with only superficial oxidation), and falls under the same sortal concept — it is still a statue. Its persistence is unproblematic.

A family heirloom pocket watch is passed from grandfather to grandson over three generations. At no point is any part replaced; it is cleaned and oiled but its material composition remains the same. It occupies a continuous spatiotemporal path and falls under the same sortal concept throughout. Its persistence as the same watch is beyond question.

A particular oak tree grows in a churchyard for four centuries, gradually increasing in size but retaining the same trunk, root system, and overall structure. It occupies a continuous spatiotemporal region, its material composition changes only through the slow addition of new wood, and it remains an oak tree throughout. Its persistence as numerically the same tree is not in doubt.
definition: An object O at time t1 is numerically identical with an object O* at time t2 if and only if (i) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path from O at t1 to O* at t2, (ii) the material composition of O changes at most gradually and incrementally between t1 and t2, and (iii) O and O* fall under the same sortal concept.
conditions: There is a continuous spatiotemporal path from O at t1 to O* at t2
The material composition of O changes at most gradually and incrementally between t1 and t2
O and O* fall under the same sortal concept
counterexample: Suppose a restorer gradually replaces every timber, plank, and nail of an ancient wooden ship over the course of twenty years, each replacement occurring at the ship's permanent berth so that the spatiotemporal path is continuous. At the end of the process, no original material remains, yet the replacements were gradual and incremental, and the object is still a ship. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might doubt that the fully restored ship is numerically the same ship as the original, for not a single particle of the original remains. If a collector reassembles all the discarded original timbers into a ship elsewhere, we face two candidates for identity with the original, and the definition provides no means of choosing between them.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that material composition remains substantially intact throughout the period of persistence, so that continuity of path, gradualness of change, and sortal constancy all converge unproblematically. The definition, generalised from these cases, treats gradual material change as sufficient for persistence. But the case of total gradual replacement reveals that incrementality of change is compatible with the complete loss of original material, and that in such cases the definition cannot distinguish the continuously maintained object from a rival candidate assembled from the original parts. The inductive generalisation breaks down where the cumulative effect of gradual changes exceeds the threshold that the background cases tacitly respect.
