The most familiar and uncontroversial instances of persistence through time share a reassuring structure. A bronze statue stands in a public square for two centuries, developing a patina but retaining its material constitution, its continuous spatiotemporal location, and its identity as a statue. A family heirloom watch passes through three generations without any replacement of parts, its material intact and its spatiotemporal path unbroken. An oak tree grows in a churchyard for four hundred years, adding new wood gradually while its trunk and root system endure. In each case, numerical identity through time is secured by the convergence of spatiotemporal continuity, material constancy, and sortal persistence.

These cases invite the generalisation that an object persists as numerically the same individual so long as there is a continuous spatiotemporal path, the material composition changes at most gradually and incrementally, and the object continues to fall under the same sortal concept. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the ancient puzzle of gradual replacement. Suppose a restorer replaces every timber, plank, and nail of an ancient wooden ship over twenty years, each substitution occurring at the ship's permanent berth so that the spatiotemporal path is continuous. The replacements are gradual — one piece at a time, with long intervals between changes. And the object remains a ship throughout. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet at the end of the process, not a single particle of the original vessel remains. If a collector reassembles all the discarded original timbers into a ship elsewhere, we are confronted with two candidates for identity with the original, and the definition offers no basis for selecting between them. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to acknowledge — is that the material changes in those cases were not merely gradual but also limited in cumulative extent. The generalisation breaks down when incrementality of replacement is carried to completion.

background_cases: It is possible that the Eiffel Tower might have been painted blue rather than its customary colour. Nothing in the laws of physics or the nature of iron prevents this; the choice of colour was a contingent decision by the tower's designers. We recognise this as a genuine possibility because we can identify the point at which the actual course of events could have diverged — the moment of the paint selection — without violating any physical or logical constraint.

It is possible that Napoleon might have won the Battle of Waterloo. The military situation was such that a different sequence of tactical decisions could have produced a different outcome, and no law of nature or logic excludes the alternative. We recognise this possibility because the actual outcome depended on contingent factors — weather, timing, the movements of particular regiments — any of which could have been otherwise.

It is possible that a particular acorn might have failed to germinate. The acorn's growth depended upon contingent conditions of soil, moisture, and temperature, and we recognise the possibility of non-germination because those conditions might readily have been different without any violation of natural law.
definition: A state of affairs S is genuinely possible if and only if (i) there is no logical contradiction in the description of S, (ii) S is compatible with the laws of nature as we understand them, and (iii) the actual course of events could have diverged at some identifiable point so as to produce S.
conditions: There is no logical contradiction in the description of S
S is compatible with the laws of nature as we understand them
The actual course of events could have diverged at some identifiable point so as to produce S
counterexample: Suppose one considers the state of affairs in which a human being runs a mile in thirty seconds — roughly four times faster than any recorded performance. The description contains no logical contradiction. And if we cannot identify a specific law of nature that sets a precise upper bound on human sprinting speed (as distinct from engineering estimates of biomechanical limits), one might argue that the state of affairs is not strictly incompatible with the laws of nature. Furthermore, one might imagine an identifiable point of divergence — a different genetic endowment, a different training regimen — from which this performance could have arisen. All three conditions appear to be met. Yet we should hesitate to call this state of affairs genuinely possible; the biomechanical, physiological, and thermodynamic constraints on the human body render it practically impossible, even if no single law explicitly prohibits it.
abductive_insight: What the background cases share is that the possibility in question is close to actuality — the point of divergence is small, the alternative outcome is well within the range of outcomes that the relevant causal processes can produce. The definition captures the structure of these near-actual possibilities but breaks down when applied to states of affairs that, while not formally contradicting any single law, lie far beyond the range of outcomes that the relevant natural processes can realistically produce. The inductive generalisation from the background cases tacitly assumes that logical and nomological consistency suffices for genuine possibility, but the case reveals that a further constraint — proximity to the capacities of the relevant causal processes — is needed.
passage:
The most uncontroversial ascriptions of genuine possibility share a common structure. We say that the Eiffel Tower could have been painted blue because the choice of colour was contingent and nothing in the laws of nature or logic excluded the alternative. We say that Napoleon might have won at Waterloo because the outcome depended upon tactical decisions and contingent circumstances that could readily have been otherwise. We say that a particular acorn might have failed to germinate because the conditions of soil and moisture upon which its growth depended were themselves contingent. In each case, the possibility in question is recognised because there is no logical contradiction, no violation of natural law, and an identifiable point at which the actual course of events could have diverged.

These cases invite the generalisation that a state of affairs is genuinely possible when its description is logically consistent, compatible with natural law, and derivable from an identifiable divergence in the actual course of events. Yet a case which reveals the insufficiency of this formulation may be constructed from the domain of human physical performance. Suppose one considers the state of affairs in which a human being runs a mile in thirty seconds. The description contains no logical contradiction. If no specific law of nature sets a precise upper limit on human sprinting speed, one might argue that the state of affairs is not strictly incompatible with the laws of nature. And one might identify a point of divergence — a different genetic constitution, a different regimen of training — from which the performance could in principle have arisen. All three conditions appear to be satisfied. Yet one might observe that this state of affairs is not genuinely possible in any robust sense. The biomechanical, physiological, and thermodynamic constraints on the human organism render it practically impossible. What the background cases share — and what the definition fails to require — is that the alternative state of affairs lies within the range of outcomes that the relevant causal processes can realistically produce. The generalisation breaks down when logical and nomological consistency is treated as sufficient for possibility, without regard to the capacities of the processes involved.

background_cases: A block of marble is shaped into a column, chipped, weathered, and repainted over centuries, yet we regard it as the same piece of marble throughout — it is a substance that endures through accidental changes.

A river flows continuously, its water constantly replaced, yet we treat the river as a persisting particular — it bears properties, is not itself a property, and exists with a degree of independence from any given parcel of water.
A diamond, once formed deep in the earth, persists through enormous changes in its surroundings — transported, cut, polished, set in a ring — while remaining the very same gem.
definition: An entity X is a substance if and only if (i) X persists through changes in its accidental properties, (ii) X is the bearer of properties without being a property of anything else, and (iii) X has determinate spatial boundaries at any time at which it exists.
conditions: X persists through changes in its accidental properties
X is the bearer of properties without being a property of anything else
X has determinate spatial boundaries at any time at which it exists
counterexample: Consider a case in which a cloud drifts across the sky. The cloud persists through changes in its accidental properties — it grows larger, changes shape, becomes lighter or darker. It bears properties — colour, density, altitude — and is not itself a property of anything else. Yet the cloud lacks determinate spatial boundaries: its edges are diffuse, trailing off gradually into clear air, and no precise line separates the cloud from the atmosphere surrounding it. Condition (iii) fails, yet we commonly speak of the cloud as a thing, a particular, and even as persisting through change. The cloud presents an instance that tells against the proposed analysis, for it exhibits the persistence and property-bearing character of a substance while lacking the determinate boundaries the definition demands.
abductive_insight: The best explanation of why the definition derived from the background cases fails here is that those cases — marble, rivers, diamonds — all happen to possess relatively determinate spatial boundaries. The generalisation to condition (iii) was an artefact of the sample rather than a genuine mark of substantiality. The cloud reveals that persistence and property-bearing are the deeper criteria, while determinate boundaries are a contingent feature of certain paradigmatic substances but not a necessary condition of substantiality as such.
passage:
If one surveys certain paradigmatic instances of what philosophers have called substances, a pattern emerges with apparent clarity. A block of marble endures through centuries of alteration — it is carved, chipped, weathered, repainted — and yet it remains the same block. A river persists despite the continual replacement of its constituent water. A diamond retains its identity through extraction, cutting, and setting. In each case, the entity bears properties, is not a property of anything else, persists through accidental change, and possesses determinate spatial boundaries at every moment of its existence. It is natural to infer from these cases that possession of determinate spatial boundaries is among the conditions that any substance must satisfy.

Yet let us consider the case of a cloud. A cloud drifts across the afternoon sky, and as it drifts it undergoes manifold changes: it swells, contracts, brightens in the sunlight, darkens as it thickens. It bears properties — a determinate colour at any given moment, a measurable altitude, an approximate density — and it is not itself a property of any other entity. It persists through these changes in a manner that permits us to speak of it as the same cloud that was overhead an hour ago. In all these respects, the cloud resembles the marble, the river, and the diamond. But the cloud lacks determinate spatial boundaries. Its edges are diffuse and ill-defined; there is no precise surface at which the cloud ends and the surrounding atmosphere begins. The transition from cloud to clear air is gradual, and any boundary we might draw would be to some degree arbitrary. The definition we have inferred from our paradigmatic cases is thus revealed to be insufficiently general. The requirement of determinate boundaries was suggested by the sample rather than by the nature of substance itself, and the cloud provides an instance that tells against its necessity. One is led to conclude that persistence through change and the bearing of properties are the more fundamental marks of substantiality, while determinacy of spatial boundary is a contingent feature of certain substances but not a condition that all substances must meet.

background_cases: A billiard ball strikes a second ball, which thereupon moves — the impact is spatially and temporally contiguous with the motion, and the first ball's motion is the cause of the second's.
A man strikes a match against a rough surface and the match ignites — the friction precedes and is contiguous with the ignition, and it would be natural to call the striking the cause of the flame.
A stone is thrown at a window and the window shatters — the impact is contiguous with the shattering and plainly precedes it, and the throw is the cause of the breakage.
definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if (i) C temporally precedes E, (ii) C is spatiotemporally contiguous with E, and (iii) had C not occurred, E would not have occurred.
conditions: C temporally precedes E
C is spatiotemporally contiguous with E
Had C not occurred, E would not have occurred
counterexample: Consider a case in which two assassins, acting independently, each fire a bullet at a man at precisely the same moment. Both bullets strike simultaneously and either alone would have been sufficient to kill him. Each shot precedes the death, each is spatiotemporally contiguous with the fatal wound, and yet for neither can it be said that had it not occurred, the death would not have occurred — for the other shot would have sufficed. Condition (iii) fails for both events individually, and yet each is, intuitively, a cause of the man's death. The case reveals the insufficiency of the analysis when applied to situations of causal redundancy.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve situations in which there is a single, unrivalled cause for each effect. The counterfactual condition, extracted from such cases, works well when causation is not redundant. But the case of simultaneous overdetermination reveals that the counterfactual analysis was tacitly dependent upon there being only one sufficient causal path. When two independent paths converge upon the same effect, the counterfactual test fails for each, exposing the limits of an inductive generalisation drawn from causally simple cases.
passage:
The billiard ball, the struck match, and the thrown stone are cases in which the causal relation seems transparent. In each, one event precedes another with which it is spatiotemporally contiguous, and one judges without hesitation that had the first event not occurred, the second would not have occurred either. From these paradigmatic instances, one might reasonably infer that causation consists in temporal precedence, spatiotemporal contiguity, and counterfactual dependence. The three conditions appear individually necessary and jointly sufficient.

Yet suppose that two assassins, unknown to one another, each resolve to kill the same man at the same moment. Each fires a bullet; both bullets strike the victim simultaneously; and either bullet, considered alone, would have been lethal. The man dies. Now, each shot precedes the death and is spatiotemporally contiguous with it — conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied for both. But condition (iii) presents a difficulty. Had the first assassin not fired, the second bullet would still have killed the man; had the second not fired, the first would have sufficed. For neither shot, taken individually, is it true that the death would not have occurred had it not taken place. The counterfactual condition fails for both, and yet we should wish to say that each shot is a cause of the man's death. The most plausible explanation of this difficulty is that the counterfactual analysis was drawn from cases in which a single, unrivalled causal path connects cause and effect. In such cases, counterfactual dependence tracks causation reliably. But when two independent causal paths converge upon the same outcome, the counterfactual test breaks down, for the availability of an alternative sufficient cause renders the dependence of the effect upon any single cause merely apparent. The generalisation from our background cases, it turns out, was sound only within the restricted domain of non-redundant causation, and the definition requires supplementation if it is to handle cases of overdetermination.

background_cases: The proposition that all bachelors are unmarried is necessarily true — its denial yields a contradiction, and it holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms involved.
The proposition that no square has five sides is necessarily true — the concept of a square entails exactly four sides, and any denial would be self-contradictory.
The proposition that if A is taller than B and B is taller than C then A is taller than C is necessarily true — the transitivity of the relation is guaranteed by its logical structure.
definition: A proposition P is necessarily true if and only if (i) the denial of P is self-contradictory, (ii) the truth of P can be established by analysis of the meanings of its constituent terms alone, and (iii) P holds universally and without exception.
conditions: The denial of P is self-contradictory
The truth of P can be established by analysis of the meanings of its constituent terms alone
P holds universally and without exception
counterexample: Consider the proposition that the morning star is identical with the evening star. This identity holds necessarily — the two names designate the same celestial body, and identity statements, if true, are true necessarily. The proposition holds universally and without exception, satisfying condition (iii). Yet condition (ii) is not satisfied, for one cannot establish the truth of this identity by analysing the meanings of 'morning star' and 'evening star' alone; their co-reference was discovered by astronomical observation. Similarly, condition (i) is doubtful, for a person ignorant of the astronomical fact might deny the identity without any sense of self-contradiction.
abductive_insight: The background cases — bachelors, squares, transitivity — are all analytic truths whose necessity derives from the meanings of the terms involved. The generalisation that all necessary truths are analytic was drawn from a sample that happened to contain only this species of necessity. The identity of the morning star with the evening star reveals a necessary truth that is not analytic — its truth depends upon how the world is, not upon the meanings of words. The inductive inference from the background cases therefore covered only one species of necessity while mistakenly treating it as the genus.
passage:
Reflection upon certain familiar propositions suggests a straightforward characterisation of necessary truth. That all bachelors are unmarried, that no square has five sides, that tallness is transitive — each of these holds in virtue of the meanings of the relevant terms, each is self-contradictory in its denial, and each holds universally. It is natural to infer from these cases that a proposition is necessarily true precisely when its denial is self-contradictory, its truth can be established by conceptual analysis, and it admits of no exception. The three conditions seem to pick out just those truths that could not have been otherwise.

Yet one may reflect upon a proposition of a rather different character. Consider the claim that the morning star is identical with the evening star. Both names refer to the same celestial body, and it is a widely held view in metaphysics that true identity statements are necessarily true — that if a thing is identical with itself, it could not have failed to be so. The proposition holds universally and without exception; there is no possible situation in which the morning star differs from the evening star, for they are one and the same object. Condition (iii) is thus satisfied. But conditions (i) and (ii) are another matter. A person who is ignorant of the relevant astronomical discovery might deny the identity with perfect equanimity and no sense of contradiction whatever. One cannot establish the truth of the proposition by analysing the meanings of 'morning star' and 'evening star' alone, for these expressions have different senses — one designates the bright object visible at dawn, the other the bright object visible at dusk — and their co-reference was discovered by empirical investigation, not by conceptual analysis. The case thus reveals that the definition derived from our background cases was too narrow. Those cases were all analytic truths — truths dependent upon meaning — and the inductive generalisation mistakenly treated analyticity as a necessary feature of all necessary truths. The morning-star proposition shows that there are truths which are necessary without being analytic, and that the definition must be broadened accordingly.

background_cases: A cat grows from a kitten to an old cat, gradually changing in size, temperament, and appearance, yet we say it is the same cat throughout — persistence is secured by spatiotemporal continuity and continuity of biological organisation.

A tree that is planted as a sapling grows, loses its leaves each autumn, regrows them each spring, and eventually towers over the garden. It is the same tree throughout its life, persisting through continual material exchange with its environment.
A river flows for centuries, its water perpetually replaced, yet the river persists — it retains its course, its banks, and its characteristic form.
definition: An object O persists from time t1 to time t2 if and only if (i) there is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting O at t1 with O at t2, (ii) O retains the same type of internal organisation throughout the interval, and (iii) the material composition of O changes only gradually.
conditions: There is a continuous spatiotemporal path connecting O at t1 with O at t2
O retains the same type of internal organisation throughout the interval
The material composition of O changes only gradually
counterexample: Consider a case in which a caterpillar enters a chrysalis and undergoes metamorphosis. During this process, the caterpillar's internal organisation is dissolved almost entirely — its tissues are broken down into an undifferentiated cellular mass before being reorganised into the radically different structure of a butterfly. The internal organisation of the caterpillar is not retained; the material composition does not change merely gradually but is subjected to a wholesale reorganisation. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are both violated. Yet there is a strong and widespread conviction that the butterfly is the same organism as the caterpillar — it emerged from the same chrysalis, and biological science treats them as stages of a single individual.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the cat, the tree, the river — all involve changes that are gradual and that preserve the broad type of internal organisation throughout. The definition abstracted from these cases incorporated gradualness and organisational continuity as necessary conditions. But metamorphosis reveals a form of biological persistence that proceeds through radical discontinuity in organisation and composition while retaining identity through a deeper continuity — genetic identity and unbroken causal process. The inductive generalisation from the background cases was therefore too conservative, treating the contingent gradualness of the sample as an essential feature of persistence.
passage:
When one considers the persistence of living things, a natural pattern presents itself. The cat grows from kitten to aged feline through a continuous process of gradual change, retaining throughout its characteristic feline organisation. The tree grows from sapling to mature specimen, exchanging matter with its environment continuously but never losing its arboreal structure. The river flows for centuries, its water perpetually renewed, yet its form and course persist. From these cases, one might reasonably infer that an object persists provided there is spatiotemporal continuity, retained internal organisation, and only gradual change in material composition. The three conditions appear to capture what is common to these diverse instances of persistence.

Yet nature furnishes a case that sits most uncomfortably with this analysis. Consider the metamorphosis of a caterpillar into a butterfly. The caterpillar enters a chrysalis, and within that enclosure its internal organisation is dissolved in the most thorough fashion — its tissues are broken down into what amounts to an undifferentiated cellular mass. This mass is then reorganised into the entirely different structure of a butterfly, with wings, compound eyes, and a proboscis where once there were stubby legs and simple mouthparts. The type of internal organisation is not merely altered but overthrown and replaced by something qualitatively new. The change in material composition is not gradual but catastrophic in its scope. Conditions (ii) and (iii) of the proposed definition are both violated in the most dramatic manner. And yet no biologist, and indeed no ordinary observer, would say that the butterfly is a different organism from the caterpillar. They are stages of the same individual life, connected by an unbroken causal process and by genetic identity. The most compelling explanation of why the definition fails is that the background cases from which it was drawn all happened to involve gradual and organisation-preserving changes. The generalisation to conditions (ii) and (iii) was an artefact of the sample. Persistence, it appears, can survive even radical discontinuity in organisation and composition, provided there is a deeper continuity — biological, causal, or genetic — that binds the earlier and later stages together.

definition: A truth is necessary if and only if it holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms involved, is knowable through reason alone, and admits of no conceivable exception.
conditions: The truth holds in virtue of the meanings of the terms involved
It is knowable through reason alone
It admits of no conceivable exception
background_cases: The proposition that all bachelors are unmarried holds in virtue of the meanings of 'bachelor' and 'unmarried,' is knowable through reason alone, and admits of no conceivable exception.
The proposition that no circle is a square holds in virtue of the meanings of 'circle' and 'square,' is knowable by pure reason, and no conceivable case could violate it.
The proposition that seven plus five equals twelve holds in virtue of arithmetical meanings, is knowable a priori, and admits of no conceivable exception.
counterexample: Suppose that the proposition 'gold has atomic number 79' is considered. This truth admits of no conceivable exception — in every possible circumstance, any substance that is genuinely gold has atomic number 79. Yet it does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the terms alone; it was discovered by empirical investigation, not by analysis of the concept 'gold.' Nor is it knowable through reason alone; it required chemical experimentation. The proposition is necessary but fails the first two conditions.
abductive_insight: The background cases tempt one to infer that necessity is always a matter of meaning and aprioricity, since the salient examples are all analytic and a priori. But the case of necessary a posteriori truths shows that inductive generalisation from paradigm analytic truths yields too narrow a conception of necessity, one that cannot accommodate the necessary truths discovered by empirical science.
passage:
When we survey the most familiar examples of necessary truths, a pattern appears to emerge with considerable clarity. The proposition that all bachelors are unmarried holds in virtue of the meanings of its constituent terms and is knowable by reason alone; no conceivable case could serve to refute it. The proposition that no circle is a square similarly holds in virtue of the definitions of 'circle' and 'square,' requires no empirical investigation for its confirmation, and admits of no exception however remote or fanciful. And the arithmetical truth that seven added to five yields twelve appears to belong to the same family: grounded in meaning, accessible to pure reason, and immune to conceivable violation. From these cases one might reasonably infer that a truth is necessary just in case it holds in virtue of the meanings of its terms, is knowable a priori, and admits of no conceivable exception.

Yet we should hesitate before endorsing this generalisation without reservation. Suppose we consider the proposition that gold has atomic number seventy-nine. There are strong grounds for holding this to be a necessary truth: in every possible circumstance, any substance that is genuinely gold possesses this atomic number, and a substance lacking it would not be gold regardless of its superficial resemblance. The proposition admits of no conceivable exception in the relevant sense. However, it does not hold in virtue of the meanings of the terms alone; the connection between 'gold' and 'atomic number seventy-nine' was established through painstaking empirical chemical investigation, not through conceptual analysis. Nor is it knowable through reason alone; generations of chemists laboured to uncover this fact. The proposition thus satisfies the condition of admitting no exception while failing the conditions of analyticity and aprioricity. This case reveals that our inductive generalisation from paradigm analytic truths yields too narrow a conception of necessity, one that excludes an important class of necessary truths discovered through the methods of empirical science rather than through the analysis of meaning.

definition: An event C causes an event E if and only if C is a necessary condition for E, C temporally precedes E, and there is a continuous physical process connecting C to E.
conditions: C is a necessary condition for E
C temporally precedes E
There is a continuous physical process connecting C to E
background_cases: Striking a match causes the flame: the striking is necessary for the flame (without it, no flame occurs), it precedes the flame temporally, and there is a continuous physical process of friction, heat generation, and ignition connecting the two.

Watering a plant causes its growth: the watering is necessary for the growth (without water the plant would die), it precedes the growth, and there is a continuous physical process of absorption, transport, and cellular expansion connecting them.

Dropping a glass causes it to shatter: the dropping is necessary (the glass would remain intact otherwise), it precedes the shattering, and there is a continuous physical process of acceleration, impact, and fracture propagation.
counterexample: Consider a case in which a lightning bolt strikes a dry forest, igniting a fire. At the very same instant, an arsonist's incendiary device, planted independently, detonates at the same location. Either event alone would have been sufficient to start the fire. The lightning is not a necessary condition for the fire, since the arsonist's device would have produced it regardless; nor is the device a necessary condition, since the lightning would have sufficed. Yet both events temporally precede the fire and are connected to it by continuous physical processes. We are nevertheless inclined to say that both the lightning and the device caused the fire.
abductive_insight: The background cases uniformly feature single causes operating in isolation, tempting one to infer that causation requires the cause to be necessary for the effect. But cases of causal overdetermination, where two independently sufficient causes operate simultaneously, reveal that this generalisation extends beyond its warrant: genuine causes need not be necessary conditions when multiple sufficient pathways converge upon the same effect.
passage:
The background cases that inform our ordinary causal thinking tend to share a common structure. When a match is struck and a flame appears, the striking is a necessary condition for the flame: had the match not been struck, no flame would have arisen. A continuous physical process of friction, heat, and ignition connects the two events. When a plant is watered and subsequently grows, the watering is again necessary for the growth, preceding it in time and connected by a continuous chain of absorption and cellular expansion. And when a glass is dropped and shatters upon the floor, the dropping is plainly necessary for the shattering, with a continuous physical process of gravitational acceleration, impact, and fracture propagation linking cause to effect. From such cases one might reasonably infer that causation requires the cause to be a necessary condition for the effect, temporally prior to it, and connected to it by a continuous physical process.

Yet we should hesitate to extend this generalisation without qualification. Suppose that a lightning bolt strikes a dry forest at precisely the instant that an arsonist's incendiary device, planted independently and unbeknownst to anyone, detonates in the same location. Either event, taken alone, would have been fully sufficient to ignite the forest. The lightning bolt is not a necessary condition for the fire, since the device alone would have produced it; the device is not a necessary condition, since the lightning alone would have sufficed. Yet both events temporally precede the fire and each is connected to it by a continuous physical process. We are strongly inclined to affirm that both the lightning and the device caused the fire. This case reveals that our induction from simple, single-cause scenarios has produced a conception of causation that is too narrow. When two independently sufficient causal pathways converge upon the same effect, neither cause is necessary, yet both are genuine causes. The requirement of necessity, so natural when abstracted from isolated cases, fails to accommodate the richer structure of causal overdetermination.

definition: An object persists through a change if and only if it retains its material composition throughout the change, maintains spatial continuity, and continues to perform the same function.
conditions: The object retains its material composition throughout the change
The object maintains spatial continuity
The object continues to perform the same function
background_cases: A bronze bell that is dented retains its bronze composition, remains spatially continuous, and continues to ring; it persists through the denting.
A wooden table that is scratched retains its wood, remains spatially continuous, and continues to serve as a surface for placing objects; it persists through the scratching.
A leather shoe that is scuffed retains its leather composition, maintains spatial continuity, and continues to function as footwear; it persists through the scuffing.
counterexample: Suppose that the ship of a maritime museum is gradually restored over many decades: every plank, nail, and fitting is replaced one by one with new materials, the replacements taking place while the ship remains in its dock. At the end of the process, no original material remains; the material composition has been entirely altered. Yet the ship has maintained perfect spatial continuity throughout and has continued to serve its function as a museum exhibit. We are inclined to say the ship persists, yet the first condition is wholly unsatisfied.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve minor changes that leave material composition intact, encouraging the inference that persistence requires retention of material composition. But the gradual replacement case shows that this inference overreaches: when replacement is sufficiently gradual and continuity of form and function is maintained, we judge the object to persist even though its entire material basis has changed. Material composition is a reliable indicator of persistence in simple cases but is not a necessary condition.
passage:
Our ordinary judgements about the persistence of objects tend to be formed in circumstances where the relevant changes are modest and the object's material constitution remains largely intact. A bronze bell that suffers a dent retains its bronze composition, continues to occupy a connected region of space, and still produces its characteristic ring; it persists through the alteration without controversy. A wooden table that acquires a scratch retains its wood, maintains its spatial integrity, and continues to serve as a surface upon which things may be placed. A leather shoe that is scuffed retains its leather, its spatial continuity, and its function as footwear. In each case, persistence is accompanied by the retention of material composition, spatial continuity, and functional constancy. From such instances one might reasonably generalise that an object persists through a change just in case it retains its material composition, maintains spatial continuity, and continues to perform the same function.

Yet this generalisation, natural though it is, proves insufficient when tested against more demanding cases. Suppose that the ship of a maritime museum is gradually restored over the course of many decades. One by one, every plank, nail, rope, and fitting is replaced with a new counterpart of identical form and specification, each replacement carried out while the ship remains in its berth and the surrounding structure is undisturbed. At the conclusion of this protracted process, not a single particle of the original material remains; the material composition has been entirely altered. Yet the ship has maintained perfect spatial continuity throughout the restoration and has continued without interruption to serve its function as a museum exhibit. We are strongly inclined to affirm that the ship persists, that it is the very same vessel that first entered the museum. The condition of material retention is wholly unsatisfied, yet persistence obtains. Our inductive generalisation from simple cases has thus led us astray: material composition, so reliable an indicator of persistence when changes are minor, is not a necessary condition when replacement is sufficiently gradual and continuity of form and function is maintained.

definition: A property is a universal if and only if it is instantiated by multiple distinct particulars, it is perceivable through sensory experience, and it admits of degrees.
conditions: The property is instantiated by multiple distinct particulars
It is perceivable through sensory experience
It admits of degrees
background_cases: Redness is instantiated by many distinct objects (a pillar box, a rose, a sunset), is perceivable through vision, and admits of degrees (crimson is a deeper red than scarlet).
Warmth is instantiated by many distinct bodies, is perceivable through touch, and admits of degrees (a candle flame is warmer than lukewarm water).
Loudness is instantiated by many distinct sounds, is perceivable through hearing, and admits of degrees (a shout is louder than a whisper).
counterexample: Consider the property of primality as instantiated by the numbers two, three, five, seven, and infinitely many others. Primality is instantiated by multiple distinct particulars (the prime numbers). However, it is not perceivable through any sensory experience; one cannot see, hear, or touch primality. Moreover, it does not admit of degrees: a number is either prime or it is not, and no number is more prime than another. Yet primality appears to be a genuine universal, multiply instantiated and possessed of the generality and repeatability characteristic of universals.
abductive_insight: The background cases, drawn exclusively from sensible qualities, tempt one to infer that universals must be perceivable and gradable. But the case of mathematical properties such as primality reveals that this inference reflects the limitations of the sample rather than the nature of universals. Induction from sensory properties alone cannot capture the full range of multiply instantiable features, which includes abstract and non-gradable properties.
passage:
When one considers the most familiar examples of properties shared by many distinct objects, a striking pattern seems to emerge. Redness is instantiated by a great variety of particulars — a pillar box, a rose, an evening sky — and is perceivable through vision; moreover, it admits of degrees, for crimson is a deeper shade of red than scarlet. Warmth is similarly instantiated across many distinct bodies, perceived through the sense of touch, and possessed of a natural gradation from tepid to scorching. Loudness, too, is multiply instantiated, apprehended through hearing, and admits of degrees from the faintest whisper to the most deafening roar. From these cases one might plausibly infer that a property qualifies as a universal just when it is instantiated by multiple distinct particulars, is perceivable through sensory experience, and admits of degrees.

Yet we should hesitate to accept this generalisation without scrutiny. Suppose we turn our attention to the property of primality — the property possessed by a natural number that is divisible only by itself and by one. This property is instantiated by infinitely many distinct particulars: two, three, five, seven, eleven, and so on without limit. It exhibits the generality and repeatability that we take to be characteristic of universals. However, primality is not perceivable through any mode of sensory experience; one cannot see, hear, or touch it. Furthermore, it does not admit of degrees: a number is either prime or it is not, and no prime number is more prime than any other. The second and third conditions of our inductively derived definition are both unsatisfied. Yet primality appears to be as genuine a universal as any, multiply instantiated and fully general. This case reveals the insufficiency of generalising from sensory qualities alone: our induction has captured features that happen to accompany the perceivable universals in our sample but are not constitutive of universality as such.
